abstract.title: Pattern Recognition

blocks.value: Pattern Recognition 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 30.1 Visual Patterns (Shapes, Colors, Sequences) before
moving on. 2. The Intuition
are everywhere once you train your eye to see them. The CET tests your ability
to spot these patterns quickly — a skill you can develop with practice.
3. The Build-Up
Visual Pattern Elements: - Shapes: Circle, square, triangle, diamond, pentagon,
hexagon - Colors: Red, blue, green, yellow, black, white - Sizes: Small, medium, large
(or 1x, 2x, 3x) - Positions: Top, bottom, left, right, center, corners - Orientations:
Rotated (90°, 180°, 270°), flipped, mirrored
Common Visual Pattern Rules: Rule Type Description

Rotation Shape turns 90° each step △ → ▷ → ▽ → ◁ Alternation Switches between 2 elements □■□■□■ Progression Size/number increases ● ● ●● → ●●● Addition Element added each step △ → △○ → △○□ Subtraction Element removed each step △○□ → △○ → △ Position Shift Element moves position Top → Right → Bottom → Left How to Solve Visual Patterns (4 Steps):
Identify changing element (shape? color? size? position?)
Find the rule (how does it change each step?)
Predict next (apply rule to last shown)
Match to options (pick the one that fits)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 30.1 Visual Patterns (Shapes, Colors,
Sequences). Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Pattern: ● ● ●● ? (Dots increasing) - A) ● - B) ●● - C) ●●● -
D) ●●●● Answer**: C) ●●●
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 30.2 Number Patterns and Sequences before moving on.
2. The Intuition
are everywhere once you train your eye to see them. The CET tests your ability
to spot these patterns quickly — a skill you can develop with practice.
3. The Build-Up Common Number Pattern Rules: Rule Type Description
Arithmetic + same number each time 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 (+2) Rule Type Description

Geometric × same number each time 3, 6, 12, 24, 48 (×2) Square Numbers 1, 4, 9, 16, 25 (1², 2², 3²…) Cube Numbers 1, 8, 27, 64, 125 (1³, 2³, 3³…) Fibonacci Add 2 previous numbers 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13… Alternating 2 different rules alternating 1, 10, 2, 20, 3, 30… Prime Numbers Only divisible by 1 and itself 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13…
How to Find Number Pattern: 1. Check differences: 2, 4, 6, 8 → differences = 2, 2, 2
(arithmetic +2) 2. Check ratios: 3, 6, 12, 24 → ratios = 2, 2, 2 (geometric ×2) 3. Check
special sequences: Squares, cubes, Fibonacci, primes 4. Test the rule on ALL numbers
(not just first 2!) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Testing only first 2 numbers - Rule must work for ALL numbers
Confusing arithmetic vs. geometric - Arithmetic = + same; Geometric = ×
Missing alternating patterns - Check if odd/even positions have different rules
Filipino Context
Number Pattern = Pattern ng Numero / Tulaan ng Bilang
Arithmetic = Aritmetiko / Pagdaragdag
Geometric = Heometriko / Pag-parami
Sequence = Sunod-sunod / Pagkasunod
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: 1, 4, 9, 16, ? - A) 20 - B) 25 - C) 36 - D) 49 Answer**: B) 25
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 30.3 Geometric Patterns before moving on.
2. The Intuition
are everywhere once you train your eye to see them. The CET tests your ability
to spot these patterns quickly — a skill you can develop with practice.
3. The Build-Up Geometric Transformations: Transformation Description

Rotation Turn around center point △ rotated 90° → ▷ Reflection Mirror image (flip) △ → ▽ (mirrored) Translation Slide to new position △ → △ (moved right) Dilation Size change (bigger/smaller) ● → ●● (2x size)
Common Geometric Pattern Rules: - Rotation: 90°, 180°, 270° (clockwise/
counterclockwise) - Reflection: Over vertical/horizontal axis - Shape Change: Circle
→ Square → Triangle (cycles) - Combined: Rotate + Change color, etc.
Polygons (Number of Sides): - Triangle = 3 sides, Square = 4, Pentagon = 5, Hexagon =
6, Heptagon = 7, Octagon = 8 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 30.3 Geometric Patterns. Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Pattern: ● (small) → ●● (medium) → ? - A) ● (small) - B)
●● (medium) - C) ●●● (large) - D) ○ (hollow) Answer**: C) ●●● (large
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 30.4 Abstract Patterns (CET Style) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
are everywhere once you train your eye to see them. The CET tests your ability
to spot these patterns quickly — a skill you can develop with practice.
3. The Build-Up
Abstract Reasoning Questions (CET Format): - 4-5 boxes with shapes/patterns - Find
the rule - Pick the NEXT box from options

Common CET Abstract Pattern Rules: Description What to Look For Shape Change Different shape each step □ → △ → ○ → ◇ Color Change Different color each step Black → White → Black → White Position Change Element moves Top → Right → Bottom → Left Number Change +1 or -1 element each step ● → ●● → ●●● Combination 2+ rules at once Shape + Color + Position
Strategy (5 Steps): 1. Identify ALL changing elements (shape, color, size, position) 2.
Find rule for EACH element (how does each change?) 3. Apply rules to predict
next 4. Eliminate wrong options (which element is wrong?) 5. Verify answer (does it
match ALL rules?) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focusing on only ONE element - Check shape, color, size, position ALL
Assuming simple rule - CET often combines 2+ rules
Not checking ALL previous boxes - Rule must work for entire sequence
Filipino Context
Abstract = Abstrak / Hindi konkreto
Pattern = Pattern / Tulaan
Shape = Hugis / Anyo
Color = Kulay
Position = Posisyon / Lugar
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Pattern: [□ black, top] → [△ white, right] → [○ black,
bottom] → ? - A) [□ white, left] - B) [△ black, top] - C) [◇ white, top] - D) [○ black,
left] Answer**: A) [□ white, left] 6. Practice Problems
Basic:
1. Review core concepts and definitions from this section.
Standard:
2. Apply concepts to sample problems. Exam-Level:
3. Timed practice under exam conditions.
7. One-Page Lock-In Core Definitions & Key Facts: Concept Key Information
Key CET topic — focus on understanding
One Thing to Never Forget: Master the core concepts of 30.4 Abstract Patterns (CET
Style) for CET success. 8. Section Reminders (Error Prevention)
Understand the fundamentals of 30.4 Abstract Patterns (CET Style) thoroughly
Practice with CET-style questions regularly
Review examples step by step
Focus on understanding, not just memorization
Use this section for quick review before the exam
Filipino Context
Abstract = Abstrak / Hindi konkreto
Pattern = Pattern / Tulaan
Shape = Hugis / Anyo
Color = Kulay
Position = Posisyon / Lugar
Chapter Practice Questions
2. Visual pattern: □ ■ □ ■ □ ? - A) □ - B) ■ - C) △ - D) ○
5. Abstract: [● top] → [●● right] → [●●● bottom] → ? - A) [●●● left] - B) [●●● top]
- C) [●●●● right] - D) [● bottom]
6. Number pattern: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, ? (Fibonacci) - A) 11 - B) 13 - C) 15 - D) 20

Answer Key
Rotate 90° each step (0°→90°→180° ✓) 5. A) [●●● left] - +1 dot each step, position
clockwise (top→right→bottom→left ✓) 6. B) 13 - Fibonacci (5+8=13 ✓) 7. B) ● -
Alternates ○ ● ○ ● ○ ● ✓) 8. B) 25 - Square numbers (5²=25 ✓) 9. A) [□ white] - Shape
cycles □△○, Color alternates Black-White ✓) 10. A) ⬃ - Rotate 90° clockwise
(⬀→⬁→⬂→⬃ ✓) Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas:
Common Pattern Mistakes (Filipino Students):
Focusing on only 2 numbers - Check buong sequence!
Confusing arithmetic vs. geometric - + same vs. × same
Not checking all options - Eliminate one by one
Pattern Recognition sa CET:
Hanapin ang nagbabagong elemento (shape, color, size, position)
Tukuyin ang tuntunin (paano nagbabago bawat hak bang?)
Hulaan ang susunod (apply tuntunin sa huling nakita)
Tanggalin ang mali (eliminate wrong options)
Number Patterns sa Tunay na Buhay:
Arithmetic: 10, 20, 30, 40… (jeepney fare increase)
Geometric: 2, 4, 8, 16… (cell division)
Fibonacci: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5… (nautral patterns in nature)
Tips para sa CET:
Isulat ang mga numero (write down the numbers) - madaling makita ang
pattern
Gumamit ng lapis (use pencil) - markahan ang differences/ratios
Huwag magmadali (don’t rush) - check ALL numbers
Tandaan ang common patterns (squares, cubes, Fibonacci, primes)

abstract.title: Logical Reasoning

blocks.value: Number Patterns 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 31.1 Deductive Reasoning (Syllogisms) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Logic puzzles follow strict rules. If the premises are true and the logic is valid, the
conclusion must be true — no matter how surprising.
3. The Build-Up
Syllogism: Deductive reasoning with 2 premises + 1 conclusion.
Structure:
Premise 1: All A are B. Premise 2: All C are A.
Conclusion: Therefore, all C are B.
Example:
Premise 1: All humans are mortal. Premise 2: Socrates is human.
Conclusion: Therefore, Socrates is mortal.
Valid vs. True: - Valid: Logic is correct (even if factually wrong) - True: Factually
correct (even if logic is bad)
Valid Syllogism (Factually FALSE, but logic valid):
Premise 1: All cats are dogs. (FALSE) Premise 2: Tom is a cat. (TRUE)
Conclusion: Therefore, Tom is a dog. (LOGIC VALID, but FALSE)
Common Syllogism Patterns: Pattern
All A are B
All A → B All birds have feathers.
Some A are B
A ∩ B ≠ ∅ Some students like math.
No A are B
A ∩ B = ∅
are mammals.
Most A are B
Majority of A → B Most Filipinos love rice. 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 31.1 Deductive Reasoning (Syllogisms).
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Which is VALID (logic correct)? Premise 1: All birds can fly.
Premise 2: Penguin is a bird. - A) Penguin can fly. - B) Penguin cannot fly. - C) All
birds are penguins. - D) Some birds are not penguins. Answer: A) Penguin can
fly. (Logic is valid: ALL birds → fly, penguin → bird, ∴ penguin → fly. Factually
FALSE, but logic VALID!
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 31.2 Inductive Reasoning (Generalizations) before moving
2. The Intuition
Understanding 31.2 Inductive Reasoning (Generalizations) is key to performing well on
the CET. Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Inductive Reasoning (Bottom-Up): - Start with SPECIFIC observations → Build
GENERAL conclusion - Example: I saw 100 Filipino students, all love rice. ∴ All
Filipinos love rice. - Result: NEVER 100% certain (maybe next Filipino hates rice!)
Comparison Table: Feature Deductive Inductive Direction General → Specific Specific → General Certainty 100% (if valid) Probable (never 100%) Start with Rule/Law Observations
Math proofs Scientific theories
Abductive Reasoning (Guess best explanation): - Start with observation → Guess
MOST LIKELY explanation - Example: Floor is wet → MOST LIKELY: rain came in
(could also be: someone spilled water) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 31.2 Inductive Reasoning (Generalizations).
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “All cats are mammals. Mittens is a cat. ∴ Mittens is a
mammal.” What type? - A) Inductive - B) Deductive - C) Abductive - D) Fallacy
Answer: B) Deductive (General rule → Specific case = deductive
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 31.3 Logical Test Questions (CET Style) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Logic puzzles follow strict rules. If the premises are true and the logic is valid, the
conclusion must be true — no matter how surprising.
3. The Build-Up CET Logical Reasoning Question Types:
Analogies (A:B :: C:?) - Find relationship
Syllogisms - Valid/invalid conclusion?
Logical Sequences - What comes next in pattern?
Statement-Argument - Identify premise/conclusion
Inference - What can be logically concluded?
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 31.3 Logical Test Questions (CET Style).
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Premise 1: All teachers are patient. Premise 2: Ms. Cruz is a
teacher. Valid conclusion? - A) Ms. Cruz is patient. - B) All patient people are
teachers. - C) Ms. Cruz is not patient. - D) Some teachers are not patient. Answer:
A) Ms. Cruz is patient. (Deductive: All teachers → patient, Ms. Cruz → teacher, ∴
patient
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 31.4 Non-Verbal Reasoning (Abstract Logic) before moving
2. The Intuition
Logic puzzles follow strict rules. If the premises are true and the logic is valid, the
conclusion must be true — no matter how surprising.
3. The Build-Up
Non-Verbal Reasoning: Solving problems WITHOUT words (purely visual).

Common Non-Verbal Question Types: Description What to Do Shape Patterns Shapes change in sequence Find rule (rotation, color, size) Number Patterns Numbers in abstract form Find mathematical rule Description What to Do Matrix Logic 2x2 or 3x3 grid with pattern Find missing cell Odd One Out 4-5 items, pick different Find which doesn’t fit Series Completion Sequence with missing item Predict next/previous Matrix Logic Example: □  ■  □ ■  □  ■ □  ■  ?
Rule: Alternates □ ■ □ ■ (columns too!) Answer: □
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 31.4 Non-Verbal Reasoning (Abstract
Logic). Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Matrix - [1, 2, 3] [4, 5, ?] - A) 6 - B) 7 - C) 8 - D) 9 Answer:
A) 6 (Row pattern: +1 each = 1,2,3 and 4,5,6
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 31.5 Psychometric Tests (Aptitude + Personality) before
moving on. 2. The Intuition
Understanding 31.5 Psychometric Tests (Aptitude + Personality) is key to performing
well on the CET. Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Psychometric Tests: Measure mental capabilities and behavioral style.
Types of Psychometric Tests: Measures
Numerical Reasoning Math/number skills Number patterns, data interpretation Verbal Reasoning Language/logic skills Analogies, syllogisms Measures

Abstract Reasoning Pattern recognition Shape/color/number patterns Spatial Reasoning 3D visualization Mental rotation, 2D→3D Mechanical Reasoning Mechanical aptitude Simple machines, forces Personality Tests Behavioral style Introvert/extrovert, etc.
Common CET Psychometric Qs: - Number sequences (IQ test style) - Shape
patterns (abstract reasoning) - Word analogies (verbal reasoning) - Syllogisms
(logical reasoning) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 31.5 Psychometric Tests (Aptitude +
Personality). Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Odd one out: 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 - A) 3 - B) 5 - C) 7 - D) 9 Answer:
D) 9 (All others are prime numbers, 9 = 3² NOT prime
6. Practice Problems
Basic:
1. Review core concepts and definitions from this section.
Standard:
2. Apply concepts to sample problems. Exam-Level:
3. Timed practice under exam conditions.

(Aptitude + Personality) for CET success.
8. Section Reminders (Error Prevention)

thoroughly

Chapter Practice Questions
1. Deductive: All A are B. All C are A. Conclusion? - A) All C are B - B) All B are C -
C) Some C are B - D) No C are B
2. Inductive: Saw 50 students, all love math. ∴? - A) All students love math. - B) 50
students love math. - C) Some students love math. - D) No students love math.
4. Syllogism: All birds fly. Penguin is bird. VALID conclusion? - A) Penguin can fly.
- B) Penguin cannot fly. - C) All birds are penguins. - D) Some birds are not penguins.
5. Odd one out: Red, Blue, Green, Yellow, Black - A) Red - B) Blue - C) Green - D) All
are colors.
10. Deductive: Valid vs. True? Premise1: All cats are dogs. Premise2: Tom is cat.
Conclusion: Tom is dog. - A) Valid + True - B) Valid, NOT True - C) NOT Valid, but
True - D) NOT Valid, NOT True Answer Key
1. A) All C are B (Deductive: A→B, C→A, ∴ C→B) 2. A) All students love math
A) Penguin can fly (VALID logic, FALSE factually) 5. D) All are colors (None is odd one
dot each step) 9. B) 13 (Primes: 2,3,5,7,11,13) 10. B) Valid (logic correct) but NOT True
(factually wrong) Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas:
Common Logical Mistakes (Filipino Students):
Confusing Valid vs. True - Valid = logic correct; True = factually correct
“Some” ≠ “Most” - “Some” = at least one (could be just one!)
“All A are B” ≠ “All B are A” - All dogs are mammals, but NOT all mammals are
Syllogisms sa Filipino:
Premis 1: Lahat ng Pilipino ay kumakain ng kanin.
Premis 2: Si Juan ay Pilipino.
Konklusyon: Si Juan ay kumakain ng kanin.
Inductive Reasoning sa Tunay na Buhay:
Observation: Nakita ko 50 dyipni, lahat may kulay.
Konklusyon: Lahat ng dyipni ay may kulay. (HINDI 100% sure!)
Tips para sa CET:
Hanapin ang relasyon (find the relationship) sa analogies
Suriin ang lohika (check logic) sa syllogisms
Huwag madaling maglapat (don’t rush to conclude) sa inductive reasoning
Kilalanin ang fallasiya (recognize fallacies) sa arguments

reasoning
IQ Tests: - IQTest Prep.com - Free IQ tests (pattern recognition) - Free-IQTest.com -
Quick pattern quizzes - Mensa IQ Test - High-level abstract reasoning
Practice Tests: - Quizlet - “Syllogisms”, “Logical fallacies”, “Analgies” - Test-
Questions.com - Logical reasoning practice - Khan Academy - Logic and reasoning
exercises

abstract.title: Spatial Reasoning

blocks.value: 1. What You Need First

Master the fundamentals of 32.1 Mental Rotation Tests before moving on. You should be comfortable identifying basic 2D shapes (squares, triangles, circles, L-shapes, T-shapes, arrows) and understanding how clockwise and counterclockwise turns work.
2. The Intuition

Mental rotation is the ability to visualize how an object looks when turned around a point or axis without physically moving it. This skill is essential for the CET spatial reasoning section. Research shows that mental rotation ability correlates strongly with success in STEM fields, and CET examiners test it heavily.
3. The Build-Up
The cognitive process of rotating a 2D or 3D object in your imagination to determine what it would look like from a different angle or orientation.

Common Rotations for 2D Shapes: - 90° Clockwise (CW): Turn the shape a quarter turn to the right. What was pointing up now points right. - 180°: Turn the shape halfway around (upside down). What was pointing up now points down. - 270° Clockwise (or 90° Counterclockwise): Turn the shape a quarter turn to the left. What was pointing up now points left. - 360°: Full rotation — the shape returns to its original position.
Key Rule for Coordinates: When rotating a point (x, y) around the origin: - 90° CW: (x, y) → (y, −x) - 180°: (x, y) → (−x, −y) - 90° CCW: (x, y) → (−y, x)
90° CCW (same as 270° CW): (x, y) → (−y, x)

Mental Rotation Strategy (4 Steps): 1. Pick a distinctive reference point (e.g., the pointed tip of an arrow, a notch, or the longest side of an L-shape). 2. Determine the rotation direction — clockwise (CW) means turning right like a clock's hands; counterclockwise (CCW) means turning left. 3. Trace where the reference point moves to after the rotation. 4. Match to the given options, eliminating choices where the reference point is in the wrong position.

3D Mental Rotation: For 3D objects (cubes, pyramids, prisms), the same principles apply but with an added depth dimension. Key tips: - Track a specific face or corner of the 3D object. - When a cube is rotated, adjacent faces remain adjacent; only the orientation changes. - If a cube face with a specific symbol is on top, after rotating 90° forward, that face moves to the front, and what was the back moves to the top.

Example 1 (Concrete): Q: An arrow (→) is rotated 90° clockwise. What does it look like? A) → (same) B) ↓ (pointing down) C) ← (pointing left) D) ↑ (pointing up) Answer: B) ↓ — Rotating 90° CW turns the arrow from pointing right to pointing down. Think: right → down → left → up (clockwise cycle).

Example 2 (3D Rotation): Q: A cube has a dot on its top face and a stripe on its front face. If the cube is rotated 90° forward (tipping forward), where are the dot and stripe now? A) Dot on front, stripe on bottom B) Dot on back, stripe on top C) Dot on front, stripe on top D) Dot on bottom, stripe on back Answer: A) Dot on front, stripe on bottom — When a cube tips forward 90°, the top face moves to the front position, and the front face moves to the bottom.
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Common Mistakes: - Confusing clockwise vs. counterclockwise — Clockwise = turn RIGHT (like a clock). - Not picking a reference point — Without an anchor, it's nearly impossible to track the rotation accurately. - Forgetting 270° CW = 90° CCW — They produce the same result, so use whichever is easier to visualize. - Mixing up 2D rotation with reflection — Rotation turns the shape; reflection flips it like a mirror.
Filipino Context
Mental Rotation = Pag-ikot sa isip / Mental na pag-ikot
Clockwise = Pakanan / Paikot pakanan
Counterclockwise = Pakaliwa / Paikot pakaliwa
Reference Point = Punto ng sanggunian
5. Worked Examples

Example 3 (CET-Level): Q: Rotate the letter 'F' 180°. What does it look like? A) F (same) B) A backwards F (mirror of F) C) An upside-down F (┐ with bottom bar) D) A rotated F where the top bar is now at the bottom right Answer: D) — Rotating 'F' 180° flips it both horizontally and vertically. The top-right bar becomes a bottom-left bar, and the middle-right bar becomes a middle-left bar. This is NOT the same as a mirror image.

Example 4 (Challenging): Q: An L-shaped figure (two connected rectangles forming an L) is rotated 270° clockwise. Which option matches? The result is equivalent to rotating 90° counterclockwise — the L now opens toward the upper-right instead of the original lower-right. Strategy: Trace the corner of the L to confirm the answer.
1. What You Need First

Understand the difference between rotation and reflection. Reflection creates a mirror image — it flips the object across a line of symmetry, whereas rotation turns it around a point. Be familiar with the concepts of left-right reversal and vertical/horizontal axes.
2. The Intuition

Mirror images are one of the most frequently tested spatial reasoning types on the CET. The key insight is that a mirror reverses the axis perpendicular to the mirror surface. A vertical mirror (left-right) reverses horizontal positions but keeps vertical positions the same. A horizontal mirror (like a floor reflection) reverses vertical positions but keeps horizontal positions the same.
3. The Build-Up
A reflected copy of an object where the left and right sides (for a vertical mirror) or top and bottom (for a horizontal mirror) are swapped, as if the object were seen in a mirror.

Vertical Mirror (Left-Right Reversal): This is the most common CET type. When an object is reflected in a vertical mirror (placed to the left or right): - Left becomes right, right becomes left. - Top stays on top, bottom stays on bottom. - Letters that look the same in a vertical mirror: A, H, I, M, O, T, U, V, W, X, Y. - Letters that change: B ↔ (no match), b ↔ d, p ↔ q, E ↔ Ǝ, L ↔ reversed L.

Horizontal Mirror (Top-Bottom Reversal): When an object is reflected in a horizontal mirror (placed above or below): - Top becomes bottom, bottom becomes top. - Left stays left, right stays right. - Example: The letter 'E' in a horizontal mirror looks like '∃' (top-bottom flipped).

Mirror Image Strategy: 1. Identify the mirror type — vertical (most common) or horizontal. 2. For each distinctive feature (arrow tip, letter opening, dot position), determine its new position after reflection. 3. Eliminate options where key features are in the wrong positions. 4. Watch for trick options that are rotations instead of reflections — rotation ≠ mirror image!
Vertical mirror at x = a: Point (x, y) → (2a − x, y)
Horizontal mirror at y = b: Point (x, y) → (x, 2b − y)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Think of holding a word up to an actual mirror — the letters reverse left-to-right. The word 'AMBULANCE' in a mirror reads backwards. On real ambulances, the text is written in mirror image so drivers see it correctly in their rearview mirrors! For CET questions, imagine the object is printed on a transparent sheet. Flip it horizontally (like turning a page) to get the vertical mirror image.

Example 1: Q: What is the mirror image of the letter 'b' in a vertical mirror? A) b B) d C) p D) q Answer: B) d — In a vertical mirror, left and right swap. The bump of 'b' that was on the right now appears on the left, making it look like 'd'.

Example 2: Q: The word 'TOP' is placed in front of a vertical mirror. What does the mirror show? A) TOP (same) B) POT C) ⊥OԀ (reversed letters) D) A mirror-reversed 'TOP' reading right-to-left Answer: D) — Each letter is individually mirror-reversed, AND the order is reversed. So 'T' stays 'T', 'O' stays 'O', 'P' becomes a reversed P, and the sequence reads right-to-left. Note: B) POT is a rotated version, not a mirror image.

Example 3: Q: An arrow pointing to the right (→) is reflected in a vertical mirror. Which direction does it point? A) Right (→) B) Left (←) C) Up (↑) D) Down (↓) Answer: B) Left (←) — A vertical mirror reverses the horizontal direction. Right becomes left.
5. CET Tip

On CET exams, mirror image questions often pair with letter/number recognition. Remember the 'b-d-p-q' family: - b ↔ d (vertical mirror swap) - p ↔ q (vertical mirror swap) - b ↔ q (180° rotation, NOT mirror) - d ↔ p (180° rotation, NOT mirror). Knowing these 4 relationships solves most mirror letter questions instantly.
1. What You Need First

Be familiar with 2D shapes and how they relate to 3D objects. Know the basic solids: cube (6 square faces), rectangular prism (6 rectangular faces), pyramid (1 square base + 4 triangular faces), cylinder (2 circles + 1 curved surface), cone (1 circle + 1 curved surface).
2. The Intuition

A net is a flat, 2D pattern that can be folded along edges to form a 3D solid. Understanding nets requires you to mentally 'fold' a flat layout into a 3D shape — or conversely, 'unfold' a 3D shape into a flat layout. This is one of the most common spatial reasoning question types on the CET.
3. The Build-Up
A two-dimensional pattern of connected shapes that can be cut out and folded along the edges to form a three-dimensional solid.

Common Nets: Cube Net — A cube has 11 distinct nets (different valid arrangements of 6 squares). The most common are the cross/T-shape (4 squares in a column, 1 on each side of the middle square) and the L-shape variations. All cube nets share these properties: - Exactly 6 squares. - Each square shares at least one edge with another square. - When folded, no two squares overlap.

Pyramid Net (Square-Based) — 1 square (the base) with 4 triangles attached to each side. The triangles fold upward and meet at the apex. Rectangular Prism Net — 6 rectangles arranged so that opposite faces are equal. Cylinder Net — 2 circles (top and bottom) connected by 1 rectangle (the curved surface). The rectangle's width equals the cylinder's height; its length equals the circumference (2πr).

Paper Folding Questions: These questions show a sequence of folds and a hole punch, then ask what the paper looks like when unfolded. Strategy: 1. Count the layers — each fold doubles the number of layers. Fold once = 2 layers, twice = 4 layers, three times = 8 layers. 2. One punch through all layers creates one hole per layer. 3. Use symmetry — the fold lines become lines of symmetry for the hole pattern when unfolded. 4. Work backwards — start from the punch position and trace the holes through each unfold.
Layers after n folds = 2ⁿ
Holes after 1 punch through n folded layers = 2ⁿ holes when unfolded

2D to 3D Shape Mapping: - Circle → Sphere / Cylinder - Square → Cube / Rectangular Prism - Triangle → Pyramid / Cone - Rectangle → Rectangular Prism / Cylinder - Pentagon → Pentagonal Prism - Hexagon → Hexagonal Prism
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Think of a net like a cardboard box before it's assembled — it's flat, with fold lines marked. To solve net questions, imagine picking up the sides of the flat pattern and folding them upward to form the box. For paper folding, grab a real piece of paper, fold it, punch a hole, and unfold it to see the pattern — then apply that logic mentally.

Example 1: Q: A square net has 4 squares in a row, with 1 square attached above the second square and 1 square attached below the third square. Can this fold into a cube? A) Yes B) No — too many squares C) No — squares overlap when folded D) No — squares are not connected Answer: A) Yes — This is one of the 11 valid cube nets. It has 6 connected squares that fold without overlap.

Example 2: Q: A paper is folded in half once (making 2 layers), then in half again (making 4 layers). One hole is punched through all layers. How many holes appear when the paper is fully unfolded? A) 2 B) 4 C) 6 D) 8 Answer: B) 4 — 2 folds = 2² = 4 layers. One punch creates 4 holes (one per layer), arranged symmetrically.

Example 3: Q: Which net folds into a square-based pyramid? A) 6 squares in a cross shape B) 1 square with 4 triangles on each side C) 2 circles and 1 rectangle D) 4 triangles in a row Answer: B) — A square-based pyramid needs 1 square base and 4 triangular faces. The net shows the square in the center with triangles on all four sides.
5. Worked Examples

Example 4 (CET-Level Paper Folding): Q: A rectangular paper is folded in half vertically (left over right), then in half horizontally (top over bottom). A hole is punched in the bottom-right corner of the folded paper. When unfolded, where do the holes appear? A) 1 hole in bottom-right only B) 2 holes: bottom-right and top-right C) 4 holes: one in each quadrant corner D) 4 holes: bottom-right, bottom-left, top-right, top-left Answer: C) — 2 folds = 4 layers. The punch at bottom-right of the folded paper corresponds to holes at all four corners when unfolded, because each fold creates a mirror line.

Example 5 (Identifying Cube Opposite Faces): Q: Given a cube net where the faces are labeled A, B, C, D, E, F — Face A is at the center of the cross, B is above A, C is below A, D is to the left of A, E is to the right of A, and F is attached to the right of E. Which face is opposite to A? A) B B) C C) F D) E Answer: C) F — In this cross net, the top (B) and bottom (C) fold to be opposite. The left (D) and right of right (F) fold to be opposite. The center (A) and the far-right (F) fold to be opposite faces.

sections.title: Figure Assembly, Hidden Figures, and Views

blocks.value: 1. What You Need First

Be comfortable recognizing basic shapes and how they combine. Understand the concepts of top view, front view, and side view of 3D objects. Know what Euler's formula is (V − E + F = 2 for convex polyhedra).
2. The Intuition

This section covers three important spatial reasoning subtypes: (a) Figure assembly — combining separate pieces to form a complete figure. (b) Hidden figures — identifying a simple shape concealed within a complex figure. (c) Orthographic views — determining what a 3D object looks like from the top, front, or side.
3. The Build-Up
A spatial reasoning task where you must determine which combination of separate pieces, when fitted together without overlap, forms the target shape.
A simpler geometric shape embedded within a more complex figure. You must identify which of the given simple shapes can be found within the complex drawing.
A method of representing a 3D object using 2D views from different directions — typically top view, front view, and right-side view.

Figure Assembly Strategy: 1. Look at the target shape and note its overall outline, number of sides, and symmetry. 2. Try fitting the pieces mentally — start with the most distinctive piece (the one with the most unusual shape). 3. Check that all pieces are used exactly once with no overlap and no gaps. 4. If pieces can be rotated, consider multiple orientations for each piece.

Hidden Figure Strategy: 1. Look at the complex figure and trace along its lines to find the boundary of the simpler shape. 2. Ignore extra lines that are not part of the target shape. 3. Focus on the vertices of the target shape — find corners that match. 4. Common hidden shapes: triangles inside pentagons, squares inside hexagons, smaller rectangles inside complex polygons.

Top/Front/Side Views: - Top View (Plan View): Imagine looking straight down at the object from above. You see the outline of the object's footprint — length and width, but no height. - Front View (Elevation): Imagine looking straight at the object from the front. You see height and width, but no depth. - Right-Side View: Imagine looking at the object from the right side. You see height and depth, but no width. Strategy: For each view, imagine the object is 'flattened' in that direction. Only edges visible from that angle appear in the view.
Euler's Formula: V − E + F = 2 (for convex polyhedra)

Where V = number of vertices (corners), E = number of edges, and F = number of faces. This formula works for all convex polyhedra — cubes, pyramids, prisms, and any solid with flat faces and no holes. Euler's formula helps verify whether a given polyhedron is correctly described and is commonly used in CET counting questions.

Common Polyhedra and Their Properties: Cube: V=8, E=12, F=6 → 8−12+6=2 ✓ Rectangular Prism: V=8, E=12, F=6 → 8−12+6=2 ✓ Triangular Prism: V=6, E=9, F=5 → 6−9+5=2 ✓ Square Pyramid: V=5, E=8, F=5 → 5−8+5=2 ✓ Triangular Pyramid (Tetrahedron): V=4, E=6, F=4 → 4−6+4=2 ✓ Pentagonal Prism: V=10, E=15, F=7 → 10−15+7=2 ✓

Useful Derived Formulas for Regular Polyhedra: - Edges from faces: Each face shares its edges with adjacent faces, so E = (total edges of all faces) ÷ 2. - For a cube: 6 faces × 4 edges each = 24, but each edge is shared by 2 faces, so E = 24 ÷ 2 = 12. - For a square pyramid: 4 triangular faces × 3 edges + 1 square base × 4 edges = 16, but 4 base edges are shared, so E = (12 + 4) ÷ 2 = 8.
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

For figure assembly, think of jigsaw puzzles — find the unique piece first and build around it. For hidden figures, imagine tracing the outline of a simple shape with your finger inside the complex drawing. For views, think of how a building looks from different sides — the front of a house looks different from the side. For Euler's formula, remember: 'Vertices minus Edges plus Faces equals Two' — it's like a checksum for 3D shapes.

Example 1 (Figure Assembly): Q: You have 3 pieces: a right triangle, a rectangle, and a small square. Which shape can they form when assembled? A) A larger square B) A pentagon C) A circle D) A hexagon Answer: A) A larger square — The right triangle and rectangle can form an L-shape, and the small square fills the gap to complete the square.

Example 2 (Top/Front/Side View): Q: A cylinder is standing upright (like a can). What does its top view look like? A) A rectangle B) A circle C) An oval D) A square Answer: B) A circle — Looking down at a cylinder from above, you see the circular top face. The front view would be a rectangle.
Example 3 (Euler's Formula): Q: A polyhedron has 8 faces and 12 edges. How many vertices does it have? A) 4 B) 6 C) 8 D) 10 Answer: B) 6 — Using V − E + F = 2: V − 12 + 8 = 2, so V = 2 + 12 − 8 = 6.

Example 4 (Hidden Figure): Q: A complex figure shows overlapping rectangles and triangles. Which simple shape is hidden within it? A) A circle B) A parallelogram C) A pentagon D) A hexagon Answer: B) A parallelogram — By tracing along the interior lines, you can identify a 4-sided figure with two pairs of parallel sides (parallelogram) embedded in the drawing.

sections.title: CET Spatial Reasoning Tips and Timed Practice

blocks.value: 1. What You Need First

Review all previous sections: Mental Rotation, Mirror Images, Paper Folding/Nets, Figure Assembly, Hidden Figures, Views, and Euler's Formula. You should understand the strategies for each question type.
2. The Intuition

The CET (College Entrance Test) is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format, managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly. Spatial reasoning questions on the CET are designed to be solved quickly with the right approach.
3. The Build-Up — Cube Painting

Cube painting is a classic CET spatial reasoning topic. An n×n×n cube is painted on all faces, then cut into n³ unit cubes. You must determine how many small cubes have paint on a specific number of faces.
Cubes with 3 painted faces = 8 (always the corner cubes)
Cubes with 2 painted faces = 12(n − 2) (the edge cubes, excluding corners)
Cubes with 1 painted face = 6(n − 2)² (the face-center cubes)
Cubes with 0 painted faces = (n − 2)³ (the interior cubes)
Verification: 8 + 12(n−2) + 6(n−2)² + (n−2)³ = n³. This always works because it accounts for every small cube in the large cube.
4. Build-Up — Shadow and Projection

Shadow/projection questions ask you to determine what shadow a 3D object casts when light shines from a specific direction. Key rules: - Light from the side → shadow shows the side profile (height × depth). - Light from above → shadow shows the top view (length × width). - Light from the front → shadow shows the front elevation (height × width). Strategy: Flatten the object in the direction opposite to the light source.
5. Time-Saving CET Strategies

1. Elimination First — Don't try to build the correct answer from scratch. Instead, eliminate options that are obviously wrong (wrong number of faces, wrong symmetry, wrong orientation). This can reduce 4 options to 2 in seconds.

2. Reference Point Method — For rotation and mirror questions, always identify one distinctive feature (a dot, arrow, notch, or letter) and track only that feature through the transformation. Don't try to track the entire figure.
3. Paper Trick — If allowed, use your scratch paper to physically fold, rotate, or draw figures. A 10-second physical demonstration can replace 60 seconds of mental visualization.

4. Symmetry Shortcut — For paper folding with hole punches, remember: n folds → 2ⁿ layers → 2ⁿ holes. The holes are arranged symmetrically along the fold lines. If you can determine the symmetry axes, you can predict hole positions instantly.
5. Euler Check — For counting questions about polyhedra, always verify with V − E + F = 2. If the numbers don't satisfy this equation, recheck your count.
6. Time Allocation — Spatial reasoning questions should take 30–60 seconds each. If a question takes more than 90 seconds, mark it and move on. Return after completing easier questions.
6. Practice Problems (Timed — 60 seconds each)
1. Rotate the letter 'R' 180°. What does it look like? A) R (same) B) A backwards R C) An upside-down R D) Я (reversed R)

2. A square folds into a cube. The net shows faces labeled 1–6. Face 1 is opposite to Face 6, Face 2 is opposite to Face 5. If Face 1 is on top and Face 2 is in front, which face is on the bottom? A) 3 B) 4 C) 5 D) 6
3. A paper is folded in half three times (8 layers). One hole is punched. How many holes when unfolded? A) 4 B) 6 C) 8 D) 16
4. A pentagonal prism has how many vertices? A) 5 B) 7 C) 10 D) 12
5. A 4×4×4 cube is painted and cut into 64 small cubes. How many have exactly 1 painted face? A) 6 B) 24 C) 48 D) 56
6. What is the mirror image of '3' in a vertical mirror? A) 3 (same) B) E C) ε D) Ƹ
7. A cylinder is lying on its side. What shape is its top view? A) Circle B) Rectangle C) Oval D) Triangle
8. An L-shape is rotated 90° clockwise. If the original L opens to the right, which direction does it open after rotation? A) Right B) Down C) Left D) Up
9. A square pyramid has V vertices, E edges, and F faces. What is V + E + F? A) 12 B) 14 C) 16 D) 18

10. A cube has a circle on its top face and a cross on its bottom face. It is tipped forward 90° (rotated about its front edge toward the viewer). Where is the circle now? A) Top B) Front C) Back D) Bottom
Answer Key

1. C) An upside-down R — 180° rotation flips both horizontally and vertically. The 'R' becomes an inverted R (not a mirror image). 2. D) 6 — Face 1 is on top, so its opposite Face 6 is on the bottom. 3. C) 8 — Three folds → 2³ = 8 layers → 1 punch × 8 layers = 8 holes. 4. C) 10 — A pentagonal prism has 2 pentagon bases (5 vertices each) = 10 vertices. Verify: V=10, E=15, F=7 → 10−15+7=2 ✓. 5. B) 24 — Formula: 6(n−2)² = 6(4−2)² = 6×4 = 24. 6. B) E — The number '3' in a vertical mirror looks like a backwards 3, which resembles the letter 'E'. 7. B) Rectangle — A cylinder on its side: top view shows the rectangular outline (length = cylinder length, width = diameter). 8. B) Down — 90° CW rotation: if the L originally opens to the right (like ⌐), after 90° CW it opens downward (like ⊓). 9. D) 18 — Square pyramid: V=5, E=8, F=5. V+E+F = 5+8+5 = 18. (Note: V−E+F=2 checks out: 5−8+5=2 ✓.) 10. B) Front — When a cube tips forward 90° (rotates about its front edge), the top face swings forward and becomes the front face, the front face becomes the bottom, the bottom swings back and becomes the back, and the back moves up to become the top. The circle was on top → it is now on the front face. Answer: B) Front.
Filipino Context Applications
Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas: Spatial Reasoning sa CET:
Mental Rotation: I-ikot sa isip (rotate shapes mentally)
2D to 3D: Kilalanin ang balangkas (recognize nets)
Directions: Gamitin ang mapa (use maps)
Paper Folding: Tupiin sa isip (fold mentally)
Mirror Images: Salamin / Larawan sa salamin
Common Mistakes (Filipino Students):
Clockwise vs. Counterclockwise — Pakanan vs. Pakaliwa
is UP — Lagi ay Hilaga sa taas sa mapa
Relative directions — Kaliwa/kanan depende sa kinahaharap
Real-Life Applications:
Navigating Manila: MRT/LRT lines (North-South)
Baha (flooding): Spatial awareness (avoid low areas)
Construction/Engineer: 2D blueprints → 3D buildings
Architecture: Floor plans (2D) → Actual house (3D)
Tips para sa CET:
Gumamit ng papel (use paper) — fold/unfold physically if allowed
Isulat ang mga direksyon (write down directions) — N, S, E, W
Visualize (isaisip) — imagine rotating shapes in your head
Practice nets (praktis balangkas) — recognize cube/pyramid nets
Chapter Summary
Mental Rotation: 90° CW (right), 180° (upside down), 270° CW = 90° CCW (left). Use reference points and coordinate rules.
Mirror Images: Vertical mirror swaps left-right; horizontal mirror swaps top-bottom. Remember b↔d, p↔q relationships.
Paper Folding & Nets: n folds → 2ⁿ layers → 2ⁿ holes. A cube has 11 distinct nets. Pyramid net = 1 square + 4 triangles.
2D → 3D: Circle→Sphere, Square→Cube, Triangle→Pyramid, Rectangle→Rectangular Prism.
Euler's Formula: V − E + F = 2 for all convex polyhedra. Use it to verify counts of vertices, edges, and faces.
Cube Painting (n×n×n): 3 faces=8, 2 faces=12(n−2), 1 face=6(n−2)², 0 faces=(n−2)³
Views: Top (plan), Front (elevation), Side — flatten the object in the viewing direction.
Filipino Tips: I-ikot sa isip, Kilalanin ang balangkas, Gamitin ang mapa, Tupiin sa isip
End Chapter Checkpoint
Review these key questions before moving on:
Can you explain the core concepts from this chapter in your own words?
Which worked example was most challenging — and why?
What one mistake are you most likely to make on this topic?
If you can answer confidently, you're ready for the next chapter. If not, revisit the Worked Examples and practice problems above.

abstract.description: Covers mental rotation, mirror images, paper folding, figure assembly, and spatial reasoning strategies.

abstract.title: Mechanical Reasoning

blocks.value: 1. What You Need First

Master the fundamentals of 33.1 Simple Machines and Mechanical Advantage before moving on. You should understand what force is (a push or pull measured in Newtons) and be comfortable with basic multiplication and division. Familiarity with the concept of work (force × distance) will help you grasp why simple machines are useful.
2. The Intuition

Simple machines are the building blocks of all mechanical devices. From the seesaw at a playground to the gears inside a clock, simple machines help us trade one physical quantity for another — usually reducing the force needed by increasing the distance over which that force acts. The CET frequently tests your ability to identify these machines, calculate mechanical advantage, and reason about how forces interact in everyday tools.
3. The Build-Up

A device that changes the direction or magnitude of a force, making work easier by providing a mechanical advantage. There are six classical simple machines: lever, pulley, inclined plane, wheel and axle, screw, and wedge.
The Six Simple Machines in Detail:

1. LEVER — A rigid bar that pivots on a fixed point called the fulcrum. Levers come in three classes based on the relative positions of the fulcrum (F), effort/input force (E), and load/output force (L):

Class 1 Lever: Fulcrum is between effort and load. Examples: seesaw, crowbar, scissors, pliers. The fulcrum acts as the pivot in the middle. If the effort arm is longer than the load arm, you get a mechanical advantage greater than 1 (less force needed).

Class 2 Lever: Load is between fulcrum and effort. Examples: wheelbarrow, nutcracker, bottle opener. The load sits between where you push and the pivot point. Class 2 levers always have MA > 1 because the effort arm is always longer than the load arm.

Class 3 Lever: Effort is between fulcrum and load. Examples: fishing rod, tweezers, human forearm, broom. The effort is applied between the pivot and the load. Class 3 levers have MA < 1, meaning you need more force than the load — but you gain speed and range of motion.
Lever MA = Length of effort arm / Length of load arm

2. PULLEY — A wheel with a groove for a rope or cable. A fixed pulley changes the direction of force (pull down to lift up) but does not multiply force (MA = 1). A movable pulley attaches to the load and moves with it, giving MA = 2. Compound pulley systems (block and tackle) combine fixed and movable pulleys for even greater advantage. A system with 4 supporting rope segments has MA = 4.
Pulley MA = Number of rope segments supporting the load

3. INCLINED PLANE — A slanted surface (ramp) that allows you to lift a heavy object by pushing it along the slope instead of lifting it straight up. The gentler the slope, the less force needed — but the longer the distance traveled. MA = length of slope / height of slope.

4. WHEEL AND AXLE — A large wheel attached to a smaller axle. When force is applied to the wheel, the axle turns with greater force (or vice versa). Examples: car steering wheel, doorknob, screwdriver handle, water well crank. MA = radius of wheel / radius of axle.

5. SCREW — An inclined plane wrapped around a cylindrical post. The threads of a screw act as a ramp — turning the screw converts rotational force into linear force. Examples: jar lids, drill bits, bolts, corkscrews. Closer threads mean higher MA but more turns needed.

6. WEDGE — Two inclined planes joined back-to-back, used to split or cut. Examples: axe head, knife blade, nail, chisel, zipper. A thinner wedge (sharper angle) has higher MA — this is why a sharp knife cuts more easily than a dull one.

The ratio of output force to input force. MA > 1 means the machine multiplies your force (easier). MA = 1 means no advantage (just changes direction). MA < 1 means you lose force but gain speed or distance.
MA = Output Force / Input Force

GEAR RATIOS: Gears are toothed wheels that transfer rotational motion between shafts. When two gears mesh, the gear with more teeth rotates more slowly but with more torque (twisting force). The gear ratio determines the speed and force trade-off.
Gear Ratio = Number of teeth on driven gear / Number of teeth on driver gear

If the driver gear has 10 teeth and the driven gear has 30 teeth, the gear ratio = 30/10 = 3. This means the driven gear rotates 3 times slower than the driver, but with 3 times the torque. If the gear ratio is less than 1, the driven gear spins faster but with less torque. Gears spinning in the same direction: use an 'idler' gear between them. Meshing gears always spin in opposite directions.

Example 1 (Concrete): Q: A crowbar is used to lift a rock. The fulcrum is 5 cm from the rock and 50 cm from where you push. What is the MA? A) 5 B) 10 C) 50 D) 500 Answer: B) 10 — MA = effort arm / load arm = 50 cm / 5 cm = 10. You only need 1/10th the weight of the rock as input force.

Example 2 (Gears): Q: Driver gear has 12 teeth. Driven gear has 48 teeth. If the driver spins at 200 RPM, how fast does the driven gear spin? A) 50 RPM B) 100 RPM C) 200 RPM D) 800 RPM Answer: A) 50 RPM — Gear ratio = 48/12 = 4. Driven speed = 200/4 = 50 RPM.

Example 3 (Pulleys): Q: A block-and-tackle system has 4 rope segments supporting the load. If the load weighs 400 N, what input force is needed? A) 100 N B) 200 N C) 400 N D) 1600 N Answer: A) 100 N — MA = 4, so Input = Output/MA = 400/4 = 100 N.
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Think of simple machines as 'force multipliers.' They don't create energy from nothing — they let you spread your effort over a longer distance so you need less force at any given moment. A long lever arm makes lifting easy. A ramp lets you push instead of lift. A pulley lets you pull down instead of up. Gears let you trade speed for strength. The trade-off is always the same: less force means more distance (or more turns, or more rope pulled).
5. Common Mistakes

Confusing lever classes — Remember: Class 1 has fulcrum in the middle, Class 2 has load in the middle, Class 3 has effort in the middle. Forgetting that pulleys change direction too — A fixed pulley gives MA = 1 but lets you pull down (using your body weight) to lift up. Thinking a machine creates free energy — Energy is conserved; you always pay in distance or speed. Mixing up gear ratio direction — The driven gear's teeth go on top (numerator).
Filipino Context

Simple Machines sa Araw-Araw: Crowbar (pangbungkal) = Class 1 lever. Wheelbarrow (kariton) = Class 2 lever. Sisidlan ng bote (bottle opener) = Class 2 lever. Fishing rod (pangingisda) = Class 3 lever. Flagpole rope (tulay ng watawat) = pulley system. Baguio zigzag road = inclined plane. Steering wheel (manibela) = wheel and axle. Corkscrew (pambukas ng alak) = screw. Itak (bolo) = wedge.

Practice: 1. A seesaw has the fulcrum in the center. What class of lever is it? (Answer: Class 1) 2. A wheelbarrow has the load between the wheel (fulcrum) and your hands (effort). What class? (Answer: Class 2) 3. A fishing rod has your hand (effort) between the handle end (fulcrum) and the fish (load). What class? (Answer: Class 3) 4. If a gear system has ratio 5:1 and the input torque is 10 Nm, what is the output torque? (Answer: 50 Nm)
1. What You Need First

Master the fundamentals of 33.2 Forces, Friction, and Newton's Laws before moving on. You should know that force is measured in Newtons (N), mass in kilograms (kg), and acceleration in meters per second squared (m/s²). Understanding vectors (quantities with both magnitude and direction) will also help.
2. The Intuition

Forces explain why objects start moving, stop moving, or change direction. Newton's three laws of motion describe the relationship between forces and motion. Friction is a force that opposes motion and is always present in real-world mechanical problems. The CET tests your ability to apply these laws to practical scenarios — from pushing boxes to understanding why seatbelts matter.
3. The Build-Up
A push or pull that can change an object's state of motion, direction, or shape. Measured in Newtons (N). Forces are vectors — they have both magnitude (how much) and direction (which way).
Newton's Three Laws of Motion:

An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity, unless acted upon by a net external force. Example: A ball on a flat surface stays still until you kick it. A book on a table doesn't move because gravity (down) is balanced by the table's normal force (up).

The net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration. More mass requires more force for the same acceleration. More force produces more acceleration for the same mass. This is the most tested law on the CET.
F = ma (Force = mass × acceleration)

For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force. Example: When you push against a wall, the wall pushes back on you with equal force. A rocket pushes exhaust gases downward; the gases push the rocket upward.
FRICTION — A force that opposes motion when two surfaces are in contact. Friction always acts in the direction opposite to motion (or intended motion).

The friction force that prevents an object from starting to move. It must be overcome before the object can slide. Static friction is always greater than or equal to kinetic friction — this is why it's harder to start pushing a heavy box than to keep it moving.

The friction force acting on an object that is already sliding. It is usually constant for a given pair of surfaces and is less than the maximum static friction. Once you get the box moving, it becomes easier to keep it moving.
Friction Force = μ × Normal Force (f = μN)

Where μ (mu) is the coefficient of friction. μ_static > μ_kinetic for the same surfaces. On a flat surface, the normal force N equals the object's weight (mg). Rougher surfaces have higher μ values. Lubricants (oil, water) reduce μ.

Gravity and Weight: On Earth, gravity accelerates objects at g = 9.8 m/s². All objects in free fall (no air resistance) accelerate at the same rate regardless of mass. Terminal velocity is reached when air resistance equals gravitational force — the object stops accelerating.
W = mg (Weight = mass × gravitational acceleration)
Pressure: Force distributed over an area. Larger area = lower pressure (snowshoes). Smaller area = higher pressure (knife edge, needle tip).
P = F / A (Pressure = Force / Area)
Example 1 (F=ma): Q: A 500 N force is applied to a 100 kg object. What is its acceleration? A) 0.2 m/s² B) 2 m/s² C) 5 m/s² D) 50 m/s² Answer: C) 5 m/s² — F = ma → 500 = 100 × a → a = 5 m/s².

Example 2 (Friction): Q: A 20 kg box sits on a rough floor (μ_static = 0.5). What minimum horizontal force is needed to start moving it? A) 10 N B) 20 N C) 98 N D) 196 N Answer: C) 98 N — Normal force = mg = 20 × 9.8 = 196 N. Min force = μ × N = 0.5 × 196 = 98 N.

Example 3 (Newton's Laws): Q: A swimmer pushes water backward. According to Newton's 3rd Law, what happens? A) The water pushes the swimmer forward B) The swimmer slows down C) The water stays still D) Gravity increases Answer: A) The water pushes the swimmer forward — Action-reaction: swimmer pushes water back, water pushes swimmer forward.
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Newton's 1st Law says things keep doing what they're already doing unless something forces them to change. Newton's 2nd Law says bigger force or lighter mass means faster acceleration. Newton's 3rd Law says forces always come in pairs — you can't push something without it pushing you back. Friction is nature's brake — it's why things slow down. Static friction holds things still; kinetic friction slows things that are already moving. Gravity pulls everything down at the same rate.
Filipino Context

Puwersa (Force) = Pagtulak o paghila. Pwersa ng friction = Puwersang humahadlang sa paggalaw. Gravity (Grabidad) = Puwersang humila pababa. W = mg (Timbang = masa × grabidad). P = F/A (Presyon = puwersa / lugar). Tips: Isulat ang F=ma bago sumagot. Alamin ang direksyon ng puwersa. Tandaan: static friction > kinetic friction.

Practice: 1. A 10 kg box is pushed with 50 N force. What is its acceleration (ignore friction)? (Answer: 5 m/s²) 2. Which requires more force to overcome: static or kinetic friction? (Answer: Static friction) 3. A 5 kg object weighs how much on Earth? (Answer: 49 N) 4. If you double the force on an object, what happens to acceleration? (Answer: It doubles) 5. You push a wall with 100 N. How much force does the wall push back? (Answer: 100 N — Newton's 3rd Law)
1. What You Need First

Master the fundamentals of 33.3 Fluid Mechanics before moving on. You should understand that fluids include both liquids and gases, and that pressure increases with depth. Familiarity with density (mass/volume) and the concept that forces can act in all directions in a fluid will be helpful.
2. The Intuition

Fluid mechanics is the study of how liquids and gases behave when at rest and in motion. Three key principles — Archimedes' principle, Pascal's principle, and Bernoulli's principle — explain everyday phenomena from why ships float to how airplane wings generate lift. The CET tests these concepts to assess your ability to reason about pressure, buoyancy, and fluid flow.
3. The Build-Up

An object submerged in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces. If the buoyant force is greater than or equal to the object's weight, the object floats. If less, it sinks.
Buoyant Force = Weight of fluid displaced = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g

Why ships float: A steel ship is denser than water, but its hull shape displaces a large volume of water. The weight of that displaced water equals the ship's weight, so the buoyant force keeps it afloat. A solid steel ball of the same mass would sink because it displaces less water. Key insight: It's not about the object's density alone — it's about how much fluid the object displaces.
A pressure change applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of its container. This principle is the basis for hydraulic systems.
P = F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂ (Pressure is equal throughout the fluid)

Hydraulic systems use Pascal's principle to multiply force. A small force on a small piston creates pressure that acts on a larger piston, producing a much larger force. Examples: car brakes, hydraulic jacks, dentist chairs, excavator arms. If piston 1 has area 10 cm² and piston 2 has area 100 cm², a 100 N force on piston 1 produces 1000 N on piston 2.
In a flowing fluid, an increase in the fluid's speed occurs simultaneously with a decrease in pressure. Faster-moving fluid = lower pressure. Slower-moving fluid = higher pressure.

Applications of Bernoulli's principle: Airplane wings (airfoil shape) — air moves faster over the curved top surface than the flat bottom, creating lower pressure on top, which generates lift. Shower curtain effect — fast-moving water/air inside the shower creates low pressure, pulling the curtain inward. Curveball in baseball — spinning ball creates different air speeds on each side, causing the ball to curve.

Example 1 (Archimedes): Q: An object displaces 2 liters of water when submerged. What is the buoyant force? (Density of water = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.8 m/s²) A) 2 N B) 9.8 N C) 19.6 N D) 196 N Answer: C) 19.6 N — Volume displaced = 2 L = 0.002 m³. Buoyant force = ρVg = 1000 × 0.002 × 9.8 = 19.6 N.

Example 2 (Pascal): Q: In a hydraulic press, the small piston has area 5 cm² and the large piston has area 50 cm². If you push with 20 N on the small piston, what force does the large piston exert? A) 20 N B) 50 N C) 200 N D) 1000 N Answer: C) 200 N — P = F₁/A₁ = 20/5 = 4 N/cm². F₂ = P × A₂ = 4 × 50 = 200 N.

Example 3 (Bernoulli): Q: Why does a shower curtain blow inward when the water is running? A) Hot air rises B) The fast-moving water creates low pressure inside the shower C) The curtain is attracted to water D) Gravity pulls the curtain Answer: B) Fast-moving water/air creates lower pressure inside, and higher pressure outside pushes the curtain in.
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Archimedes: Things float if they push away enough water to equal their own weight. Pascal: Squeeze a fluid in one place, and the push shows up everywhere equally. Bernoulli: Where fluid moves fast, pressure drops. Where it moves slow, pressure rises. Think of a river — narrow sections flow fast (low pressure), wide sections flow slow (high pressure).
Filipino Context

Archimedes = Lumulutang ang bagay kung magaan ang naitatabi nitong tubig. Pascal = Ang presyon sa likido ay pantay sa lahat ng direksyon. Bernoulli = Mabilis na daloy = mababang presyon. Mga Halimbawa: Bangka sa ilog = Archimedes. Hydraulic jack sa talyer = Pascal. Pakpak ng eroplano = Bernoulli.

Practice: 1. An object weighs 50 N in air and 30 N when submerged in water. What is the buoyant force? (Answer: 20 N) 2. In a hydraulic system, if the small piston area is doubled, what happens to the force on the large piston? (Answer: It doubles) 3. An airplane wing has faster air on top and slower air below. Which way does the net force point? (Answer: Upward — lift) 4. A wooden block floats with 60% of its volume submerged. If its volume is 500 cm³, what volume of water does it displace? (Answer: 300 cm³)
1. What You Need First

Master the fundamentals of 33.4 Heat Transfer and Basic Circuits before moving on. You should understand that heat is a form of energy that flows from hotter to cooler objects, and that electric current is the flow of charged particles (electrons) through a conductor. Basic algebra (solving for unknowns) is essential for circuit calculations.
2. The Intuition

Heat transfer and electricity are two topics that frequently appear in CET mechanical reasoning questions because they involve everyday practical knowledge. Understanding how heat moves (conduction, convection, radiation) and how basic circuits work (Ohm's law, series vs. parallel) allows you to solve problems about cooking, insulation, wiring, and power consumption.
3. The Build-Up
The movement of thermal energy from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature. There are three methods of heat transfer: conduction, convection, and radiation.
HEAT TRANSFER METHODS:

Heat transfer through direct contact between particles in a solid (or between touching solids). Energy passes from particle to particle without the material itself moving. Metals are excellent conductors (copper, aluminum, iron). Non-metals are poor conductors called insulators (wood, plastic, rubber, air). Example: Touching a hot metal spoon — heat conducts from the hot end to your hand.

Heat transfer through the bulk movement of fluid (liquid or gas). Hot fluid rises because it becomes less dense when heated; cool fluid sinks to replace it, creating a convection current. Example: Boiling water — hot water at the bottom rises, cool water at the top sinks. Weather patterns — warm air rises, cool air moves in (wind).

Heat transfer through electromagnetic waves (primarily infrared). No medium is required — radiation can travel through a vacuum. All objects emit radiation; hotter objects emit more. Example: Feeling the heat from a bonfire without touching it. The Sun heating the Earth through the vacuum of space.
BASIC ELECTRIC CIRCUITS:
The voltage (V) across a conductor equals the current (I) through it times its resistance (R). This fundamental relationship governs all simple circuit calculations.
V = IR (Voltage = Current × Resistance)

Units: Voltage (V) in Volts, Current (I) in Amperes (Amps), Resistance (R) in Ohms (Ω). Rearranged: I = V/R (more voltage = more current; more resistance = less current). R = V/I (higher resistance limits current flow).
The rate at which electrical energy is consumed or dissipated. Power is measured in Watts (W).
P = IV (Power = Current × Voltage)
Combining with Ohm's Law: P = I²R and P = V²/R. A 100W bulb connected to a 220V outlet draws I = P/V = 100/220 ≈ 0.45 A.

Components connected end-to-end in a single path. Same current flows through all components. Voltage is divided across components. Total resistance = sum of individual resistances. If one component fails, the entire circuit breaks.
Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ...

Components connected across the same two points, creating multiple paths for current. Same voltage across all branches. Current is divided among branches. Total resistance is less than the smallest individual resistance. If one branch fails, the others continue working.
Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃ + ...
Example 1 (Ohm's Law): Q: A 12V battery is connected to a 4Ω resistor. What current flows? A) 0.33 A B) 3 A C) 16 A D) 48 A Answer: B) 3 A — I = V/R = 12/4 = 3 A.

Example 2 (Series): Q: Three resistors (2Ω, 3Ω, 5Ω) are connected in series to a 10V battery. What is the current? A) 1 A B) 3.3 A C) 10 A D) 30 A Answer: A) 1 A — R_total = 2+3+5 = 10Ω. I = V/R = 10/10 = 1 A.

Example 3 (Parallel): Q: Two resistors (6Ω and 12Ω) are connected in parallel. What is the total resistance? A) 2Ω B) 4Ω C) 9Ω D) 18Ω Answer: B) 4Ω — 1/R = 1/6 + 1/12 = 2/12 + 1/12 = 3/12 = 1/4. R = 4Ω.
Example 4 (Power): Q: A 60W bulb runs on 120V. What current does it draw? A) 0.5 A B) 2 A C) 60 A D) 7200 A Answer: A) 0.5 A — I = P/V = 60/120 = 0.5 A.
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Heat Transfer: Conduction = touching something hot (spoon in soup). Convection = hot air/water rising (boiling pot, room heater). Radiation = feeling heat from a distance (campfire, sunlight). Circuits: Voltage = electrical 'pressure' (like water pressure). Current = flow of electrons (like water flow). Resistance = opposition to flow (like a narrow pipe). Ohm's Law V=IR is the most important circuit formula. Series = one path (Christmas lights — one burns out, all go dark). Parallel = multiple paths (house wiring — one light burns out, others stay on).
Filipino Context

Conduction = Paghahatid ng init sa paghawak (pagluto gamit ang kawali). Convection = Pag-akyat ng mainit na hangin/tubig (electric fan, kumukulo na tubig). Radiation = Init mula sa araw o apoy nang hindi hawak. V = IR (Boltahe = Kasalukuyan × Resistansya). P = IV (Kapangyarihan = Kasalukuyan × Boltahe). Series = Sunud-sunod (isang daanan). Parallel = Kasabay (maraming daanan).

Practice: 1. What type of heat transfer occurs when you put a metal勺 in hot soup? (Answer: Conduction) 2. Why does hot air rise? (Answer: Convection — heated air becomes less dense and rises) 3. A 24V battery powers a circuit with 8Ω resistance. What current flows? (Answer: 3 A) 4. Two 10Ω resistors in parallel have what total resistance? (Answer: 5Ω) 5. A device uses 1500W at 220V. What current does it draw? (Answer: ~6.8 A)

sections.title: CET Mechanical Reasoning Strategies and Practice

blocks.value: 1. What You Need First

Master the fundamentals of 33.5 CET Mechanical Reasoning Strategies and Practice before moving on. You should have studied simple machines (33.1), forces and Newton's laws (33.2), fluid mechanics (33.3), and heat transfer with circuits (33.4). This section ties everything together with exam strategies and comprehensive practice.
2. The Intuition

The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Mechanical reasoning questions test your practical understanding of how things work — not your ability to memorize formulas. Knowing the format, managing your time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly. Many mechanical reasoning problems can be solved by thinking about real-world situations rather than performing calculations.
3. The Build-Up
COMMON CET MECHANICAL QUESTION TYPES:

Type 1 — Tool Identification: 'What simple machine is this?' Strategy: Look for the key features — a rigid bar with a pivot = lever, a wheel with rope = pulley, a slanted surface = ramp/inclined plane, a wheel on a shaft = wheel and axle, threads around a cylinder = screw, two inclined planes back-to-back = wedge.

Type 2 — Mechanical Advantage: 'Which setup is easier?' or 'What force is needed?' Strategy: Calculate or compare MA values. Higher MA = less force needed. For levers, longer effort arm = higher MA. For pulleys, more supporting ropes = higher MA. For inclined planes, gentler slope = higher MA.

Type 3 — Force Direction: 'Which way does the object move?' or 'In which direction does force act?' Strategy: Draw a quick free-body diagram. Identify all forces (gravity, friction, applied force, normal force). The object accelerates in the direction of the net force.

Type 4 — Newton's Laws: 'Which law applies?' Strategy: 1st Law = object stays at rest or constant velocity (no net force). 2nd Law = force causes acceleration (F=ma). 3rd Law = action-reaction pair (two objects pushing each other).

Type 5 — Fluid/Heat/Circuit: 'What happens when...?' Strategy: Apply the relevant principle. Buoyancy = Archimedes. Pressure in enclosed fluid = Pascal. Fast fluid = low pressure = Bernoulli. Touching hot = conduction. Rising hot air = convection. Feeling warmth at a distance = radiation. V=IR for circuits.
EXAM STRATEGIES FOR MECHANICAL REASONING:
1. Visualize first, calculate second — Many problems can be answered by imagining the real-world scenario before writing any equations. Picture the lever, the pulley, or the circuit in your mind.
2. Draw it out — A quick sketch or free-body diagram takes 10 seconds and prevents 90% of errors. Label forces with arrows showing direction and relative magnitude.

3. Use elimination — If you can rule out two obviously wrong answers, your odds jump from 25% to 50%. Look for answers that violate basic principles (e.g., a 'mechanical advantage' less than 1 for a simple lever with a long effort arm).
4. Check units — Force is in Newtons, pressure in Pascals (or N/m²), current in Amps, voltage in Volts, resistance in Ohms. If the units don't match the question, the answer is wrong.

5. Remember the trade-offs — Simple machines trade force for distance. Gears trade speed for torque. Parallel circuits trade resistance for current paths. These trade-offs are the most common conceptual questions.
6. Time management — If a problem takes more than 90 seconds, mark it and move on. Come back after finishing easier questions. Aim for 60-75 seconds per mechanical reasoning item.
KEY FORMULAS SUMMARY:
MA = F_out / F_in (Mechanical Advantage)
Gear Ratio = Teeth_driven / Teeth_driver
F = ma (Newton's 2nd Law)
W = mg (Weight)
f = μN (Friction Force)
P = F/A (Pressure)
F_buoyant = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g (Archimedes)
V = IR (Ohm's Law)
P = IV (Electrical Power)
Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + ... | Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ...

Example 1 (Tool ID): Q: A nutcracker has the nut between the fulcrum and your hands. What class of lever? A) Class 1 B) Class 2 C) Class 3 D) Not a lever Answer: B) Class 2 — Load (nut) is between fulcrum and effort.

Example 2 (Multi-concept): Q: A 50 kg box sits on a rough surface (μ = 0.4). You push with 250 N horizontally. What is the net force and acceleration? A) 54 N, 1.08 m/s² B) 56 N, 1.12 m/s² C) 250 N, 5 m/s² D) 304 N, 6.08 m/s² Answer: A) 54 N, 1.08 m/s² — Friction = μmg = 0.4 × 50 × 9.8 = 196 N. Net force = 250 - 196 = 54 N. Acceleration = F/m = 54/50 = 1.08 m/s².

Example 3 (Circuit): Q: A 9V battery powers two 6Ω resistors in parallel. What is the total current from the battery? A) 0.75 A B) 1.5 A C) 3 A D) 54 A Answer: C) 3 A — 1/R = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 → R = 3Ω. I = V/R = 9/3 = 3 A.

Example 4 (Heat Transfer): Q: You can hold a wooden stick near a fire without burning your hand, but not a metal stick of the same size. Why? A) Wood is hotter B) Metal is a better conductor C) Wood conducts heat better D) Fire doesn't affect metal Answer: B) Metal is a better conductor — heat travels through metal to your hand much faster than through wood.
COMPREHENSIVE PRACTICE PROBLEMS:

Practice Set A (Simple Machines): 1. A bottle opener is which type of lever? (Answer: Class 2 — load between fulcrum and effort) 2. A gear system has a 12-tooth driver and a 36-tooth driven gear. What is the gear ratio? (Answer: 3:1) 3. A ramp is 6 m long and 2 m high. What is the MA? (Answer: 3) 4. How many rope segments support the load in a pulley system with MA = 5? (Answer: 5)

Practice Set B (Forces & Friction): 5. A 2000 kg car accelerates at 3 m/s². What force does the engine produce? (Answer: 6000 N) 6. A 10 kg box has μ_static = 0.6. What force is needed to start moving it? (Answer: 58.8 N) 7. Which has more inertia: a 5 kg ball or a 15 kg ball? (Answer: 15 kg ball — more mass = more inertia) 8. You push a heavy door. The door pushes back with equal force. Which law? (Answer: Newton's 3rd Law)

Practice Set C (Fluids): 9. A block displaces 0.01 m³ of water. Buoyant force? (Answer: 98 N) 10. Hydraulic press: small piston 2 cm², large piston 20 cm², input force 50 N. Output force? (Answer: 500 N) 11. Air moves faster over the top of a wing. What effect does this create? (Answer: Lower pressure on top = lift)

Practice Set D (Heat & Circuits): 12. What heat transfer method warms your hands by a campfire? (Answer: Radiation) 13. A 15Ω resistor has 3A flowing through it. What voltage is across it? (Answer: 45V) 14. Three 9Ω resistors in parallel. Total resistance? (Answer: 3Ω) 15. A 220V appliance draws 5A. What is its power consumption? (Answer: 1100W)
Filipino Context — CET Mechanical Reasoning Tips

Mga Payo sa CET Mechanical Reasoning: Kilalanin ang kagamitan (identify the tool) — lever, pulley, ramp, wheel & axle, screw, o wedge. Tukuyin ang bentahe mekanikal (determine mechanical advantage) — mas mataas = mas madali. Alamin ang direksyon ng puwersa (know force direction) — gumuhit ng free-body diagram. Tandaan ang tatlong batas ni Newton — 1st = Inersya, 2nd = F=ma, 3rd = Aksyon-Reaksyon. Isulat ang mga formula bago sumagot — V=IR, P=F/A, W=mg, F=ma. Gamitin ang elimination — tanggalin ang maling sagot muna. Mag-praktis araw-araw — ang mechanical reasoning ay nakabatay sa karanasan at pag-unawa, hindi sa memorization lamang.

Filipino Real-Life Applications: Crowbar sa konstruksyon = Class 1 lever. Kariton ng magsasaka = Class 2 lever. Sisidlan ng bote = Class 2 lever. Pangingisda = Class 3 lever. Poso (water well) = wheel and axle. Rampa ng mga PWD = inclined plane. Tulay ng watawat = pulley. Electric fan = converts electrical to mechanical energy. Plancha (flat iron) = conduction. Aircon = convection. Araw = radiation.
Chapter Summary

Six Simple Machines: Lever (3 classes), Pulley, Inclined Plane, Wheel and Axle, Screw, Wedge. Mechanical Advantage: MA = Output Force / Input Force; higher MA = less effort needed. Gear Ratios: Ratio = Driven teeth / Driver teeth; trades speed for torque. Newton's Laws: 1st = Inertia, 2nd = F=ma, 3rd = Action-Reaction. Friction: Static > Kinetic; f = μN. Pressure: P = F/A; smaller area = higher pressure. Fluid Mechanics: Archimedes (buoyancy), Pascal (hydraulic pressure), Bernoulli (fast fluid = low pressure). Heat Transfer: Conduction (solids), Convection (fluids), Radiation (EM waves). Circuits: V=IR (Ohm's Law), P=IV (Power), Series (R adds), Parallel (1/R adds). CET Strategies: Visualize first, draw diagrams, eliminate wrong answers, check units, manage time.
End Chapter Checkpoint
Review these key questions before moving on:

Can you identify all 6 simple machines and give an example of each? Can you calculate MA for levers, pulleys, and inclined planes? Do you know the three classes of levers and how to distinguish them? Can you explain Newton's three laws with real-world examples? Can you calculate friction force, weight, and pressure? Do you understand Archimedes', Pascal's, and Bernoulli's principles? Can you solve series and parallel circuit problems using Ohm's law? Can you identify conduction, convection, and radiation examples?

What one mistake are you most likely to make on this topic? (Common errors: confusing lever classes, forgetting static > kinetic friction, mixing up series and parallel formulas, or misidentifying heat transfer type.)
If you can answer confidently, you're ready for the next chapter. If not, revisit the Worked Examples and practice problems above.

abstract.description: Covers simple machines, forces and friction, fluid mechanics, heat transfer, and mechanical reasoning practice.

abstract.title: IQ Tests & Comprehensive Review

blocks.value: 1. What You Need First

Before tackling number sequences, you should be comfortable with basic arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division), exponents and roots, and the concept of differences between consecutive terms. These fundamentals are the building blocks for every sequence pattern you will encounter on the CET.
2. The Intuition

Number sequences are the bread and butter of IQ-style questions on the CET. The exam gives you a list of numbers with one missing, and your job is to figure out the rule connecting them. The key insight is that almost every sequence follows one of a small set of mathematical relationships — your task is to identify which one. With practice, you will start recognizing these patterns within seconds.
3. The Build-Up

A sequence where each term differs from the previous one by a constant value called the common difference (d). The general term is aₙ = a₁ + (n−1)d. To detect it: compute the differences between consecutive terms — if they are all the same, you have an arithmetic sequence.
Arithmetic Example: 3, 7, 11, 15, 19, ___. Differences: 7−3=4, 11−7=4, 15−11=4, 19−15=4. Common difference d = 4. Next term: 19 + 4 = 23.

A sequence where each term is multiplied by a constant value called the common ratio (r) to get the next term. The general term is aₙ = a₁ × r^(n−1). To detect it: compute the ratios of consecutive terms — if they are all the same, you have a geometric sequence.
Geometric Example: 2, 6, 18, 54, ___. Ratios: 6÷2=3, 18÷6=3, 54÷18=3. Common ratio r = 3. Next term: 54 × 3 = 162.

A sequence where each term (from the third onward) is the sum of the two preceding terms. The classic Fibonacci sequence starts 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, … Variants may start with different seed numbers but follow the same additive rule.
Fibonacci Example: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ___. Rule: each term = sum of previous two. Next term: 8 + 13 = 21. Variant (seeds 2, 5): 2, 5, 7, 12, 19, 31, ___. Next: 19 + 31 = 50.

A sequence where the second differences (differences of differences) are constant. This indicates the general term follows a quadratic formula aₙ = An² + Bn + C. To detect it: compute first differences, then second differences. If the second differences are all equal, the sequence is quadratic.
Quadratic Example: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ___. First differences: 3, 5, 7, 9, 11. Second differences: 2, 2, 2, 2 (constant!). These are perfect squares: n². Next term: 6² = 36.

Square and Cube Patterns: Many CET sequences are built from perfect squares (1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 121, 144, …) or perfect cubes (1, 8, 27, 64, 125, 216, …). Sometimes these are disguised with offsets or combined with other operations.

Square Pattern: 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, ___. These are 2², 3², 4², 5², 6². Next: 7² = 49. Cube Pattern: 1, 8, 27, 64, ___. These are 1³, 2³, 3³, 4³. Next: 5³ = 125. Mixed: 0, 3, 8, 15, 24, ___. These are n² − 1: 1²−1, 2²−1, 3²−1, 4²−1, 5²−1. Next: 6² − 1 = 35.

Detection Strategy: 1. Compute differences between consecutive terms. If constant → arithmetic. 2. Compute ratios between consecutive terms. If constant → geometric. 3. Check if each term = sum of previous two → Fibonacci. 4. Compute second differences. If constant → quadratic (n² pattern). 5. Check if terms are perfect squares or cubes (possibly with offset).
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Think of number sequences like a recipe. The recipe has a rule, and each number is made by applying that rule to the previous number(s). Your job is to reverse-engineer the recipe. Start simple: are the gaps between numbers always the same? If yes, add that gap. Are the gaps growing in a pattern? Check if multiplying by the same number each time works.
