english.title: Reading Comprehension

blocks.value: Reading Flowchart 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 21.1 Reading Strategies before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 21.1 Reading Strategies is key to performing well on the CET. Focus on
core concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up The BIG 5 Reading Comprehension Skills:
is the text MOSTLY about?
Supporting Details - Facts/examples that support main idea
is IMPLICIT (not directly stated)?
Tone/Purpose - Author’s attitude and reason for writing
Vocabulary in Context - Guess meaning from context clues
SQ3R Reading Method: 1. Survey - Skim headings, first/last paragraphs 2. Question -
Ask: “What is this about?” 3. Read - Read actively, underline key points 4. Recite -
Summarize in your own words 5. Review - Go back, check understanding
Active Reading Tips: - Underline/highlight KEY sentences (topic sentences, thesis) -
Write margin notes (summarize each paragraph in 3 words) - Ask: “Why is the author
telling me this?” - Predict what comes next
Filipino Context Main Idea = Pangunahing Ideya / Punto
Supporting Details = Mga Detalyeng Sumusuporta
Inference = Pagpapalagay / Implikasyon
Tone = Ton / Tangos ng Tinig
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 21.1 Reading Strategies. Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Apply the concepts above to practice questions.
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 21.2 Main Idea and Supporting Details before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 21.2 Main Idea and Supporting Details is key to performing well on the
CET. Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Main Idea (Topic Sentence): - Usually in FIRST or LAST sentence of paragraph -
Summarizes the ENTIRE paragraph (not just one part) - Answers: “What is this
paragraph MOSTLY about?”
Supporting Details: - Examples, facts, statistics, quotes - Explain, prove, or illustrate
main idea - Can be specific data or anecdotes
How to Find Main Idea: 1. Read entire paragraph 2. Cross out details (examples,
numbers, names) 3. What’s LEFT = main idea
Common Trap (CET!): - Too narrow: Only covers first/last sentence - Too broad:
Covers everything in universe (too general) - Just right: Matches paragraph’s focus
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Choosing first sentence always - Sometimes topic sentence is LAST

blocks.term: Picking a detail as main idea - Main idea
blocks.value: is BIGGER (summarizes all)
Too broad - “Animals are great” is too broad for a paragraph about dogs
Filipino Context
Main Idea = Pangunahing Ideya (dapat sakop ang buong talata)
Too narrow = Sobrang spesipiko (nila sa isang detalye)
Too broad = Sobrang lawak (hindi tiyak sa talata)
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Which is a supporting detail? - A) Main idea - B) Topic
sentence - C) Example or fact - D) Conclusion Answer: C) Example or fact
(Supporting details = examples, facts, data
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 21.3 Inference and Conclusion before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 21.3 Inference and Conclusion is key to performing well on the CET.
Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Inference: Reading BETWEEN the lines (not directly stated, but implied).
Signal Words for Inference: - “Suggests”, “implies”, “indicates”, “can be inferred” -
“The author probably believes…” - “It can be concluded that…”
How to Make Inferences: 1. Find FACTS in text (what is directly stated) 2. Add your
BACKGROUND KNOWLEDGE 3. Combine → Inference (logical conclusion)
Example: - Text: “JM has studied 5 hours, drank 3 coffees, and looks exhausted.” - Fact:
JM studied 5 hrs, drank coffee, looks tired - Background: Studying long + coffee =
preparing for exam - Inference: JM is preparing for an important test.
Conclusion vs. Inference: - Conclusion: Final thought (usually stated directly) -
Inference: Implied meaning (not directly stated)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 21.3 Inference and Conclusion. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Text: “Lola Maria has owned her sari-sari store for 30 years.
Everyone in barangay knows her.” What can you infer? - A) Lola Maria is new in the
area. - B) Lola Maria is well-known in her community. - C) Lola Maria will close
her store soon. - D) Lola Maria sel s only rice. Answer: B) Lola Maria is well-
known in her community. (30 years + everyone knows her = well-known
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 21.4 Tone and Author’s Purpose before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 21.4 Tone and Author’s Purpose is key to performing well on the CET.
Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Tone: Author’s attitude toward subject (like tone of voice).
Common Tone Words: Meaning

Optimistic Hopeful, positive “The future looks bright!” Pessimistic Negative, hopeless “Nothing will ever improve.” Nostalgic Longing for past “Those were the good old days.” Sarcastic Mocking, ironic “Oh great, another typhoon.” Objective Neutral, factual “The data shows 50% increase.” Subjective Personal opinion “I think this is the best policy.” Formal Academic, serious “The results demonstrate significance.” Informal Casual, conversational “Hey, check this out!” Meaning
Critical Finding fault “The plan has major flaws.” Sympathetic Understanding, kind “I understand your struggle.”
Author’s Purpose (PIE): - Persuade - Convince you to agree/buy/do - Inform - Teach
facts (textbook, news) - Entertain - Amuse, tell story (novel, movie)
How to Identify Tone: 1. Look for STRONG WORDS (adjectives, adverbs) 2. Ask:
“How would I read this aloud?” 3. Match feeling to tone word
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 21.4 Tone and Author’s Purpose. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “Ugh, another traffic jam in EDSA.” What is the tone? - A)
Optimistic - B) Sarcastic/Frustrated - C) Objective - D) Nostalgic Answer: B)
Sarcastic/Frustrated (“Ugh” = frustration, “another” = annoyance
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 21.5 Literary Devices and Poetry before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 21.5 Literary Devices and Poetry is key to performing well on the CET.
Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up Common Literary Devices: Device Definition

Simile Comparison using “like” or “as” “Her smile is like sunshine.” Metaphor Direct comparison (no “like/as”) “Her smile is sunshine.” Personification Give human traits to non-human “The flowers danced in the wind.” Alliteration Repeated initial consonant sounds “Sally sold seashells.” Onomatopoeia Word sounds like its meaning “Buzz”, “Bang”, “Splash” Device Definition
Hyperbole Extreme exaggeration “I’ve told you a million times!” Irony Opposite of what’s expected Fire station burns down. Symbolism Something represents something else
Dove = peace, Red = danger
Foreshadowing Hints at future events Dark clouds before storm. Flashback Returns to past events Character remembers childhood.
Poetry Elements: - Rhyme - End sounds match (cat, hat, mat) - Rhythm/Meter -
Pattern of stressed/unstressed syllables - Stanza - Paragraph of a poem - Line - One row
of a poem - Speaker - Voice of the poem (NOT always the poet!)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 21.5 Literary Devices and Poetry. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “The books flew off the shelves.” What device? - A) Simile -
B) Metaphor - C) Personification - D) Hyperbole Answer: C) Personification
(Books can’t “fly” — human action given to objects
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 21.6 CET Reading Comprehension Strategies before moving
2. The Intuition
The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,
managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly.
3. The Build-Up The CET Reading Strategy (5 Steps):
1. Read the QUESTIONS first (30 seconds) - Know what to look for - Underline
KEYWORDS in questions
2. Skim the passage (1 minute) - Read first/last sentences of each paragraph - Note
the topic/main idea
3. Read passage CAREFULLY (3-4 minutes) - Underline topic sentences - Note
transition words (however, therefore, but)
4. Answer questions (1-2 minutes per question) - Go BACK to passage (don’t rely
on memory) - Eliminate obviously wrong answers
5. CHECK your answers - Does your answer match the text? - Did you fall for a trap?
Common CET Traps: - “All of the above” - Only pick if ALL others are correct -
“None of the above” - Only pick if ALL others are wrong - Too extreme - “Always”,
“never”, “all”, “none” (usually wrong) - Out of scope - Not mentioned in passage at all
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 21.6 CET Reading Comprehension
Strategies. Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Which word is a “trap” word? - A) Some - B) Often - C)
Always - D) Usually Answer: C) Always (Too extreme = usually wrong in CET
6. Practice Problems
Basic:
1. Review core concepts and definitions from this section.
Standard:
2. Apply concepts to sample problems. Exam-Level:
3. Timed practice under exam conditions.
7. One-Page Lock-In Core Definitions & Key Facts: Concept Key Information
Key CET topic — focus on understanding
One Thing to Never Forget: Master the core concepts of 21.6 CET Reading
Comprehension Strategies for CET success.
8. Section Reminders (Error Prevention)
Understand the fundamentals of 21.6 CET Reading Comprehension Strategies
thoroughly
Practice with CET-style questions regularly
Review examples step by step
Focus on understanding, not just memorization
Use this section for quick review before the exam
Filipino Context
Strategy = Estratehiya
Skim = Sipiin / Basang-takbo
Underline = Salungguhitan
Eliminate = Tanggalin / Bawasan
Chapter Practice Questions
1. Main idea of: “Jeepneys are colorful. They have unique designs. They are a
Filipino symbol.” - A) Jeepneys are colorful. - B) Jeepneys are a unique Filipino symbol.
- C) Jeepneys have designs. - D) Jeepneys are used for transport.
2. Text: “JM studied 5 hours and drank 3 coffees.” What can you infer? - A) JM is
lazy. - B) JM is preparing for an exam. - C) JM hates coffee. - D) JM studied for 10 hours.
3. “The results demonstrate a 40% increase.” What is the tone? - A) Sarcastic - B)
Optimistic - C) Objective - D) Nostalgic
4. “Her eyes are like stars.” What device? - A) Simile - B) Metaphor - C)
Personification - D) Hyperbole
5. Author’s purpose of a textbook? - A) Persuade - B) Inform - C) Entertain - D)
Confuse
6. Which is a “trap” word in CET? - A) Some - B) Usually - C) Never - D) Often
7. “The wind whispered through the trees.” What device? - A) Simile - B) Metaphor
- C) Personification - D) Onomatopoeia
8. Text: “Lola has owned sari-sari store for 30 years. Everyone knows her.” What
inference? - A) Lola is new. - B) Lola is well-known. - C) Lola will close soon. - D) Lola
sel s only rice.
9. What should you do FIRST in CET reading? - A) Read passage carefully - B) Read
questions first - C) Answer immediately - D) Guess all answers
10. “Bang!” What device? - A) Simile - B) Metaphor - C) Personification - D)
Onomatopoeia Answer Key
1. B - Covers all sentences (colorful + designs + Filipino symbol) 2. B - 5 hrs + coffee =
preparing for exam (inference) 3. C - Just facts, no emotion = objective 4. A - Uses “like”
= simile 5. B - Textbook = inform (teach facts) 6. C - “Never” is too extreme (trap word)
7. C - Wind can’t “whisper” (human action = personification) 8. B - 30 years + everyone
knows her = well-known 9. B - Read questions FIRST (know what to look for) 10. D -
“Bang!” sounds like the noise = onomatopoeia
Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas:
Reading Comprehension sa Filipino Subjects:
Main Idea = Hanapin ang pangunahing ideya sa bawat talata
Supporting Details = Mga ebidensya o halimbawa
Inference = “Ano ang ibig sabihin nito?” (What does this mean?)
Common Filipino English Reading Mistakes:
“Bang” (Filipino) vs. “Bang!” (English onomatopoeia)
“Parang” (Filipino simile) vs. “Like” (English simile)
“Tulad” (Filipino metaphor) vs. “Is” (English metaphor)
Literary Devices sa Filipino:
Pagwawangis = Simile (gamit ang “parang” o “tulad”)
Pagbibigay-katauhan = Personification (tao ang bagay)
Bugtong = Riddle (Filipino literary form)
Tanaga = 4-line poem (7 syllables each, Filipino)
CET Reading Tips para sa Filipino Students:
Basang-takbo (skimming) muna bago basahing mabuti
Salungguhitan (underline) ang mahahalagang puntos
Tanggalin (eliminate) ang mga maliwanag na mali
Bumalik sa teksto (go back to text) — huwag umasa sa memorya
Resources
Free Resources for Reading Comprehension: - Khan Academy - Reading
comprehension practice (all levels) - Read Theory.org - Adaptive reading
comprehension (free, great for CET) - Common Lit.org - Reading passages with
questions - Newsela.com - News articles at different reading levels - Project
Gutenberg - Free classic books (practice reading) - BBC Learning English - Reading
comprehension videos
Literary Devices: - Literary Devices.net - Comprehensive device list with examples -
Poetry Foundation - Poetry analysis and devices - Shakespeare Online -
Understanding Shakespeare’s language
Practice Tests: - Quizlet - “Reading comprehension”, “Literary devices” - Test-
Questions.com - Reading comprehension practice - Khan Academy - Practice
exercises for each skill
Chapter Summary: - BIG 5 Skills: Main Idea, Supporting Details,
Inference, Tone/Purpose, Vocabulary in Context - SQ3R: Survey, Question,
Read, Recite, Review (active reading method) - Inference = reading
BETWEEN lines (combine facts + background knowledge) - Tone =
author’s attitude (optimistic, pessimistic, objective, sarcastic) - Author’s
Purpose = PIE (Persuade, Inform, Entertain) - Literary Devices: Simile
(like/as), Metaphor (is), Personification (human traits) - CET Strategy:
Read QUESTIONS first, then passage (know what to look for!) - Trap
words: “always”, “never”, “all”, “none” (too extreme = usually wrong)
Chapter Summary: - BIG 5 Skills: Main Idea, Supporting Details,
Inference, Tone/Purpose, Vocabulary in Context - SQ3R: Survey, Question,
Read, Recite, Review (active reading method) - Inference = reading
BETWEEN lines (combine facts + background knowledge) - Tone =
author’s attitude (optimistic, pessimistic, objective, sarcastic) - Author’s
Purpose = PIE (Persuade, Inform, Entertain) - Literary Devices: Simile
(like/as), Metaphor (is), Personification (human traits) - CET Strategy:
Read QUESTIONS first, then passage (know what to look for!) - Trap
words: “always”, “never”, “all”, “none” (too extreme = usually wrong)
End Chapter Checkpoint
Review these key questions before moving on:
Can you explain the core concepts from this chapter in your own words?
Which worked example was most challenging — and why?

english.title: Verbal Reasoning

blocks.value: 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 22.1 Word Analogies before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 22.1 Word Analogies is key to performing well on the CET. Focus on core
concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up
A : B :: C : D (A is to B as C is to D)
Common Analogy Relationships: Relationship
Logic Synonym
Hot : Warm :: Cold : Cool
are synonyms, like Cold and
Antonym
Hot : Cold :: Happy : Sad
is opposite of Cold, like Happy is
opposite of Sad Part to Whole
Wheel : Car :: Page : Book
is PART of Car, like Page is PART of
Type/Kind
Rose : Flower :: Dog :
Mammal
is a TYPE of Flower, like Dog is a TYPE
of Mammal Function/Use
Pen : Write :: Knife : Cut
is USED to Write, like Knife is USED to
Worker/ Workplace
Doctor : Hospital ::
Teacher : School
Doctor WORKS at Hospital, like Teacher WORKS at School Cause/Effect
Fire : Burn :: Rain : Wet
Fire CAUSES Burn, like Rain CAUSES Wet Degree
Warm : Hot :: Cool : Cold
is LESS Hot, like Cool is LESS Cold
Symbol
Dove : Peace :: Red : Danger
Dove SYMBOLIZES Peace, like Red SYMBOLIZES Danger Sequence
Seed : Tree :: Egg : Bird
Seed GROWS INTO Tree, like Egg GROWS INTO Bird How to Solve Analogies (4 Steps): Find relationship between A and B Put in sentence: “A [relationship] B” Apply to C: “C [same relationship] D?”
Test answer: Does it fit the SAME pattern?
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 22.1 Word Analogies. Practice with sample
questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Hot : Cold :: Happy : ? - A) Joyful - B) Sad - C) Angry - D)
Excited Answer**: B) Sad
Analogy Bridge 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 22.2 Syllogisms (Deductive Reasoning) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Logic puzzles follow strict rules. If the premises are true and the logic is valid, the
conclusion must be true — no matter how surprising.
3. The Build-Up
Syllogism: Deductive reasoning with 2 premises + 1 conclusion.
Structure:
Premise 1: All A are B. Premise 2: All C are A.
Conclusion: Therefore, all C are B.
Example:
Premise 1: All humans are mortal. Premise 2: Socrates is human.
Conclusion: Therefore, Socrates is mortal.
Valid vs. True: - Valid: Logic is correct (even if factually wrong) - True: Factually
correct (even if logic is bad)
Valid Syllogism (Factually FALSE, but logic valid):
Premise 1: All cats are dogs. (FALSE) Premise 2: Tom is a cat. (TRUE)
Conclusion: Therefore, Tom is a dog. (LOGIC VALID, but FALSE)
Common Syllogism Patterns: Pattern
All A are B
All A → B All birds have feathers.
Some A are B
A ∩ B ≠ ∅ Some students like math.
No A are B
A ∩ B = ∅
are mammals.
Most A are B
Majority of A → B Most Filipinos love rice. 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Confusing valid vs. true - Valid = logic correct; True = factually correct
“Some” ≠ “Most” - “Some” = at least one (could be just one!)
“All A are B” ≠ “All B are A” - All dogs are mammals, but NOT all mammals are
Filipino Context
Syllogism = Silogismo / Panghuhusga
Premise = Premis / Pangangatwiran
Conclusion = Konklusyon / Pinal na palagay
Deductive = Deduktibo / Panghuhusgang-ibaba
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Which is VALID (logic correct)? Premise 1: All birds can fly.
Premise 2: Penguin is a bird. - A) Penguin can fly. - B) Penguin cannot fly. - C) All
birds are penguins. - D) Some birds are not penguins. Answer: A) Penguin can
fly. (Logic is valid: ALL birds → fly, penguin → bird, ∴ penguin → fly. Factually
FALSE, but logic VALID!
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 22.3 Deductive vs. Inductive Reasoning before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Logic puzzles follow strict rules. If the premises are true and the logic is valid, the
conclusion must be true — no matter how surprising.
3. The Build-Up
Deductive Reasoning (Top-Down): - Start with GENERAL rule → Apply to SPECIFIC
case - Example: All humans are mortal (general). Socrates is human (specific). ∴
is mortal. - Result: VALID deduction = 100% certain (if premises true)
Inductive Reasoning (Bottom-Up): - Start with SPECIFIC observations → Build
GENERAL conclusion - Example: I saw 100 Filipino students, all love rice. ∴ All
Filipinos love rice. - Result: NEVER 100% certain (maybe next Filipino hates rice!)
Comparison Table: Feature Deductive Inductive Direction General → Specific Specific → General Certainty 100% (if valid) Probable (never 100%) Start with Rule/Law Observations
Math proofs Scientific theories
Abductive Reasoning (Guess best explanation): - Start with observation → Guess
MOST LIKELY explanation - Example: Floor is wet → MOST LIKELY: rain came in
(could also be: someone spilled water) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 22.3 Deductive vs. Inductive Reasoning.
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “I saw 50 jeepneys, all colorful. ∴ All jeepneys are colorful.”
What type? - A) Inductive - B) Deductive - C) Abductive - D) Fallacy Answer: A)
Inductive (Specific observations → General conclusion = inductive
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 22.4 Logical Fallacies before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Logic puzzles follow strict rules. If the premises are true and the logic is valid, the
conclusion must be true — no matter how surprising.
3. The Build-Up
Fallacy: Error in reasoning (logic flaw).
Common Fallacies (CET Tested): Fallacy Definition

Ad Hominem Attack person, not argument “You’re stupid, so you’re wrong.” Straw Man Misrepresent opponent’s argument “He wants to ban ALL cars!” (He only wants fewer cars) False Dilemma Only 2 choices (when more exist)
“Either eat rice or starve!” (Can eat bread
Slippery Slope Small step → Disaster
“If we allow jeepneys, soon there’ll be no
roads left!” Circular Reasoning Conclusion in premise
“The Bible is true because it says it’s true.”
Hasty Generalization Too small sample
“I met 2 Filipinos, both like adobo. ∴ All
Filipinos like adobo.” Post Hoc “After this, therefore BECAUSE of this”
“Rooster crowed, then sun rose. ∴ Rooster
causes sunrise!” Appeal to Authority “Expert” says so (but not real expert)
“Actor says this pill works, so it must!”
Bandwagon “Everyone else does it” “Everyone cheats, so you should too!” Red Herring
Distract from main issue
“Why worry about poverty when we have great malls?” 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 22.4 Logical Fallacies. Practice with sample
questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “Either build this mall or our economy dies!” What fallacy? -
A) Slippery Slope - B) False Dilemma - C) Straw Man - D) Red Herring Answer: B)
False Dilemma (Only 2 choices given, but more options exist
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 22.5 CET Verbal Reasoning Practice before moving on.
2. The Intuition
The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,
managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly.
3. The Build-Up CET Verbal Reasoning Question Types:
Analogies (A:B :: C:?) - Find relationship
Syllogisms - Valid/invalid conclusion?
Logical Sequences - What comes next in pattern?
Statement-Argument - Identify premise/conclusion
Inference - What can be logically concluded?
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 22.5 CET Verbal Reasoning Practice.
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Premise 1: All teachers are patient. Premise 2: Ms. Cruz is a
teacher. Valid conclusion? - A) Ms. Cruz is patient. - B) All patient people are
teachers. - C) Ms. Cruz is not patient. - D) Some teachers are not patient. Answer:
A) Ms. Cruz is patient. (Deductive: All teachers → patient, Ms. Cruz → teacher, ∴
patient 6. Practice Problems
Basic:
1. Review core concepts and definitions from this section.
Standard:
2. Apply concepts to sample problems. Exam-Level:
3. Timed practice under exam conditions.
7. One-Page Lock-In Core Definitions & Key Facts: Concept Key Information
Key CET topic — focus on understanding
One Thing to Never Forget: Master the core concepts of 22.5 CET Verbal Reasoning
Practice for CET success.
8. Section Reminders (Error Prevention)
Understand the fundamentals of 22.5 CET Verbal Reasoning Practice thoroughly
Practice with CET-style questions regularly
Review examples step by step
Focus on understanding, not just memorization
Use this section for quick review before the exam
Filipino Context
Pattern = Pattern / Tulaan
Sequence = Pagkasunod-sunod / Sunod-sunod
Valid = Wasto / Epektibo (sa lohika)
Invalid = Mali / Hindi epektibo
Chapter Practice Questions
1. Knife : Cut :: Pen : ? - A) Write - B) Book - C) Read - D) Paper
2. All A are B. All C are A. Conclusion? - A) All B are C - B) All C are B - C) Some C
are B - D) No C are B
3. “I met 3 rich people, so all people are rich.” What fallacy? - A) Ad Hominem - B)
Hasty Generalization - C) False Dilemma - D) Slippery Slope
4. Seed : Tree :: Caterpillar : ? - A) Butterfly - B) Leaf - C) Garden - D) Insect
5. “Either vote for me or this city dies!” What fallacy? - A) Ad Hominem - B) Straw
Man - C) False Dilemma - D) Red Herring
7. “You’re not an expert, so your opinion doesn’t count.” What fallacy? - A) Ad
Hominem - B) Appeal to Authority - C) Bandwagon - D) Red Herring
8. Hot : Cold :: Fast : ? - A) Quick - B) Slow - C) Speed - D) Car
9. All dogs have tails. Buddy is a dog. Conclusion? - A) Buddy has a tail. - B) All
dogs are Buddy. - C) Buddy has no tail. - D) Some dogs have no tails.
10. “After I washed my car, it rained. My washing caused rain!” What fallacy? -
A) Post Hoc - B) Slippery Slope - C) Circular Reasoning - D) Bandwagon
Answer Key
1. A - Knife is USED TO Cut, Pen is USED TO Write 2. B - All A→B, All C→A, ∴ All
C→B (Deductive logic) 3. B - Hasty Generalization (too small sample = 3 people) 4. A -
Seed GROWS INTO Tree, Caterpillar GROWS INTO Butterfly 5. C - False Dilemma
(only 2 choices, but more exist) 6. C - Pattern: +3 each time (3, 6, 9, 12, 15) 7. A - Ad
Hominem (attacks person = “not expert”, not argument) 8. B - Hot is OPPOSITE Cold,
is OPPOSITE Slow 9. A - Deductive: All dogs → tails, Buddy → dog, ∴ Buddy →
tail 10. A - Post Hoc (“After this, therefore BECAUSE of this”)
Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas: Common Fallacies in Filipino Arguments:
Ad Hominem: “Bobo ka, kaya mali ka!” (You’re stupid, so you’re wrong!)
Bandwagon: “Lahat ng kapitbahay natin bumoto sa kanya, bumoto ka na
rin!” (Everyone voted for him, you should too!)
False Dilemma: “Mag-aral ka o maging basura collector ka!” (Study or become
trash collector!)
Hasty Generalization: “Nakita ko 2 lang ng mga Pilipino, tamad. Kaya lahat ng
Pilipino tamad!” (Saw 2 lazy Filipinos, so all Filipinos are lazy!)
Analogies sa Filipino:
Guro : Paaralan :: Doktor : Ospital (Teacher:School :: Doctor:Hospital)
Buto : Katawan :: Pahina : Aklat (Bone:Body :: Page:Book)
Mainit : Malamig :: Mabilis : Mabagal (Hot:Cold :: Fast:Slow)
Syllogisms sa Filipino:
Premis 1: Lahat ng Pilipino ay kumakain ng kanin.
Premis 2: Si Juan ay Pilipino.
Konklusyon: Si Juan ay kumakain ng kanin.
Verbal Reasoning Tips para sa Filipino Students:
Hanapin ang relasyon (find the relationship) sa analogies
Suriin ang lohika (check logic) sa syllogisms
Wag madaling maglapat (don’t rush to conclude) sa inductive reasoning
Kilalanin ang fallasiya (recognize fallacies) sa arguments

Practice sets - Syllogism Practice (Purdue OWL) - Logic exercises
Practice Tests: - Quizlet - “Analogies”, “Syllogisms”, “Logical fallacies” - Test-
Questions.com - Verbal reasoning practice - Khan Academy - Logic and reasoning

sentence, apply to C) - Syllogisms: 2 premises + 1 conclusion (deductive
reasoning) - Valid = logic correct; True = factually correct (different!) -
Deductive: General → Specific (100% certain if valid) - Inductive: Specific
→ General (never 100% certain) - Common Fallacies: Ad Hominem, Straw
Man, False Dilemma, Slippery Slope - CET Verbal: Analogies, Syllogisms,
Patterns, Inferences - Remember: “Some” = at least one (could be just
one!), NOT “most”

blocks.value: Essay Structure 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 23.1 Diction (Word Choice) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 23.1 Diction (Word Choice) is key to performing well on the CET. Focus
on core concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up
Diction: Choice of words (affects tone, clarity, precision).
Levels of Diction: Level
Formal Academic, professional “The results demonstrate significance.” Informal Casual, conversational “The results show it’s important.” Colloquial Regional/dialect
“The results show it’s imporant, ‘di ba?”

Slang Very casual, trendy “The results are lit!” Archaic Old-fashioned “The results doth show import.” Precise vs. Vague Words: Vague (Avoid) Precise (Use) Excellent, beneficial, advantageous Detrimental, harmful, adverse Enormous, vast, expansive Small Minuscule, tiny, negligible Stride, amble, trudge
Denotation vs. Connotation: - Denotation: Dictionary definition (literal meaning) -
Connotation: Emotional association (positive/negative)
Example: - “Slim” vs. “Skinny” vs. “Slender” - All mean “thin” (denotation same) -
“Slim” = positive (fit), “Skinny” = negative (too thin), “Slender” = neutral/elegant
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 23.1 Diction (Word Choice). Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “The politician is thrifty” (denotation = careful with money).
What connotation? - A) Positive (smart with money) - B) Negative (cheap, stingy) -
C) Neutral - D) Depends on context Answer: D) Depends on context (Could be
positive “smart” or negative “stingy”
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 23.2 Syntax and Sentence Patterns before moving on.
2. The Intuition
are everywhere once you train your eye to see them. The CET tests your ability
to spot these patterns quickly — a skill you can develop with practice.
3. The Build-Up
Syntax: Arrangement of words to create well-formed sentences.
5 Basic Sentence Patterns (Review): 1. S + V (Birds fly.) 2. S + V + C (She is happy.) 3.
S + V + O (I eat rice.) 4. S + V + IO + DO (I gave him a book.) 5. S + V + O + OC (We

elected her president.) Common Syntax Errors: Error Wrong Correct Dangling Modifier “Running fast, the tree was seen.” “Running fast, I saw the tree.” Misplaced Modifier “I saw a dog with a telescope.” “With a telescope, I saw a dog.” Faulty Parallelism “I like swimming, to jog, and biking.” “I like swimming, jogging, and biking.” Incomplete Sentence (Fragment) “Because I was happy.” “I smiled because I was happy.” Run-on Sentence “I went to Manila I saw Rizal Park.” “I went to Manila, and I saw Rizal Park.”
Subordination (Complex Sentences): - Use subordinating conjunctions: because,
although, since, while, if, when - Example: “I smiled because I was happy.”
Coordination (Compound Sentences): - Use coordinating conjunctions: and, but, or,
so, for, nor, yet (FANBOYS) - Example: “I smiled, and you laughed.”
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 23.2 Syntax and Sentence Patterns. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Fix: “Walking down the hall, the painting caught my eye.” -
A) “Walking down the hall, I noticed the painting.” - B) “The painting, walking
down the hall, caught my eye.” - C) “Walking down the hall, my eye caught the
painting.” - D) “The painting caught my eye walking down the hall.” Answer: A)
“Walking down the hall, I noticed the painting.” (Now “walking” modifies “I”
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 23.3 Media and Information Literacy before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 23.3 Media and Information Literacy is key to performing well on the
CET. Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Media Literacy: Ability to access, analyze, evaluate, and create media.
Types of Media:

Purpose Print Books, newspapers, magazines In-depth information Broadcast TV, radio, podcasts Mass audience, audio/visual Digital/Social Facebook, Twitter, Tik Tok Instant, interactive New Media Blogs, vlogs, podcasts User-generated content Evaluating Sources (C.R.A.P. Test): Criterion
Questions to Ask
Currency
Is it up-to-date? When was it published?
Relevance
Does it relate to your topic? Is it appropriate?
Authority
Who wrote it? Are they experts? Is it credible?
Accuracy
Is it factually correct? Can it be verified?
Purpose
Why was it written? Is it to persuade, inform, or sell?
Bias and Propaganda Techniques: Technique Definition

Bandwagon “Everyone else is doing it!” “Join 1 million users!” Glitering Generalities Vague, positive words “The best product ever!” Testimonial Famous person endorses “Celebrity X uses this!” Plain Folks “Just like you” “Ordinary people use this!” Fear Appeal Threaten bad outcome “Buy or you’ll regret it!” Technique Definition
Loaded Language Emotionally charged words “Disgusting, miracle, freedom” 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 23.3 Media and Information Literacy.
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “Join 1 million users!” uses what technique? - A)
Testimonial - B) Bandwagon - C) Fear Appeal - D) Plain Folks Answer: B)
Bandwagon (“Everyone else is doing it!”
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 23.4 Oral Communication before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 23.4 Oral Communication is key to performing well on the CET. Focus on
core concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up Types of Speech: Purpose

Informative Teach facts Lecture, report, news Persuasive Convince audience Debate, campaign speech Entertaining Amuse audience Speech, toast, comedy Impromptu No preparation Answering questions, introducing Extemporaneous Some preparation, notes Class presentation Manuscript Read word-for-word News anchor, formal address Memorized No notes, memorized Oratorical contest
Parts of a Speech: 1. Introduction - Hook, background, thesis 2. Body - Main points
with evidence 3. Conclusion - Summary, call to action
Delivery Techniques: - Eye contact - Connect with audience - Gestures - Emphasize
points - Volume/Pitch/Speed - Vary for emphasis - Pause - Let important points sink
in - Visual aids - Help audience understand
Active Listening Skills: - Focus - Give full attention - Clarify - Ask questions if
unclear - Summarize - Repeat main points - Empathize - Understand speaker’s
feelings 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 23.4 Oral Communication. Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “I have a dream…” is what part of speech? - A)
Introduction (hook) - B) Body (main point) - C) Conclusion (call to action) - D)
None of the above Answer: A) Introduction (hook) (Famous opening line that grabs
attention
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 23.5 Practical Research before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 23.5 Practical Research is key to performing well on the CET. Focus on
core concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up
Research Process (5 Steps): 1. Identify Problem - What do you want to know? 2.
Review Literature - What did others say? 3. Formulate Hypothesis - What do you
predict? 4. Collect Data - Surveys, experiments, observations 5. Analyze & Conclude -
What does the data say? Types of Research: Purpose
Descriptive Describe characteristics “What are students’ study habits?” Correlational Relationship between 2+ variables “Is there a link between sleep and grades?” Purpose

Experimental Cause-and-effect “Does more sleep → better grades?” Qualitative Non-numerical (interviews, observations) “How do students feel about exams?” Quantitative Numerical (surveys, tests) “85% of students fear exams.” Sampling Techniques: Technique Definition
Random Everyone has equal chance Pick names from a hat Stratified Divide into groups, then random Random from Grade 10, 11, 12 Systematic Every nth person Survey every 5th student Convenience
Whoever is available
Survey friends in class 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 23.5 Practical Research. Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Surveying only your classmates. What sampling? - A)
Random - B) Stratified - C) Systematic - D) Convenience Answer: D) Convenience
(Whoever is available = classmates 6. Practice Problems

Filipino Context
Research = Pananaliksik / Pagsasaliksik
Hypothesis = Hipotesis / Palagay
Qualitative = Kwalitatibo (salita/deskripsyon)
Quantitative = Kwantitatibo (numero/estadistika)

Chapter Practice Questions
1. Which is MOST formal diction? - A) “The food is yum!” - B) “The food is delicious.”
- C) “The food is palatable.” - D) “The food is bomb!”
2. Which is a DANGLING modifier? - A) “I saw a dog running fast.” - B) “Running
fast, the dog barked at me.” - C) “Running fast, I was barked at by the dog.” - D) “The dog
was running fast.”
3. C.R.A.P. test: “C” stands for? - A) Clarity - B) Currency - C) Creativity - D) Content
4. “Join 1 million users!” uses what propaganda? - A) Testimonial - B) Bandwagon -
C) Fear Appeal - D) Plain Folks
5. Which speech type is a “lecture”? - A) Informative - B) Persuasive - C)
Entertaining - D) Impromptu
6. “Does sleep affect grades?” What research type? - A) Descriptive - B)
Correlational - C) Experimental - D) Qualitative
7. Surveying every 5th person. What sampling? - A) Random - B) Stratified - C)
Systematic - D) Convenience
8. Which is a RUN-ON sentence? - A) “I study hard, and I pass.” - B) “I study hard. I
pass.” - C) “I study hard I pass.” - D) “I study hard; I pass.”
9. “The politician is thrifty” (positive). What connotation? - A) Positive - B)
Negative - C) Neutral - D) Depends on context
10. “I have a dream…” is what part of speech? - A) Introduction (hook) - B) Body - C)
Conclusion - D) None Answer Key
1. C - “Palatable” is most formal (academic word) 2. B - “Running” doesn’t modify
“dog” (dangling); should be “Running fast, I was barked at…” 3. B - C.R.A.P. = Currency,
Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, Purpose 4. B - “Everyone else is doing it!” = Bandwagon
5. A - Lecture = Informative (teach facts) 6. B - “Is there a relationship?” = Correlational
7. C - Every nth person = Systematic 8. C - Two independent clauses with no
conjunction = run-on 9. A - “Thrifty” = positive (smart with money) in this context 10. A
- Famous hook/attention-grabber = Introduction
Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas: Media Literacy sa Pilipinas:
Fake News = Maling balita (madalas sa Facebook)
Trolls = Mga troll (paid to spread misinformation)

“Damdamin ng mga Estudyante sa Modular Learning” (Students’ Feelings
on Modular Learning)
Oral Communication Tips para sa Filipino Students:
Gumamit ng mata (eye contact) - Connect with audience
Huwag magbasa nang direkta (don’t read directly) - Use notes, not manuscript
Habang nagsasalita, mag-gesture (gesture while speaking) - Emphasize points
Huwag kabahan (don’t be nervous) - Practice makes perfect!

blocks.value: Exam Weight Pie 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 24.1 SAT Writing and Language Strategies before moving
2. The Intuition
Understanding 24.1 SAT Writing and Language Strategies is key to performing well on
the CET. Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
SAT Writing Section (44 questions, 35 minutes): - 4 passages (narrative, argument,
informative, nonfiction) - Questions on: Grammar, Rhetoric, Words in Context
Big 5 SAT Writing Rules:
Concise Expression - Choose SHORTEST correct answer
❌ “The fact that she is happy is known.”
✅ “She is happy.”
Parallel Structure - Same form for related ideas
❌ “I like swimming, to jog, and biking.”
✅ “I like swimming, jogging, and biking.”
Modifier Placement - Modifier NEAR word it describes
❌ “I saw a dog running to school with a broken leg.”
✅ “I saw a dog with a broken leg running to school.”
Pronoun Clarity - Clear antecedent, no ambiguity
❌ “When Juan told Jose he was happy.” (Who is “he”?)
✅ “Juan told Jose, ‘I am happy.’”
Logical Transitions - Choose correct connector
Addition: furthermore, moreover, in addition
Contrast: however, although, on the other hand
Cause/Effect: therefore, thus, consequently
Time: meanwhile, subsequently, earlier
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 24.1 SAT Writing and Language Strategies.
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “I like swimming, to jog, and biking.” Best revision? - A) I
like swimming, to jog, and biking. - B) I like swimming, jogging, and to bike. -
C) I like swimming, jogging, and biking. - D) I like to swim, jog, and biking.
Answer: C) I like swimming, jogging, and biking. (All -ing = parallel
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 24.2 ACT English Section before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 24.2 ACT English Section is key to performing well on the CET. Focus on
core concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up
ACT English Section (75 questions, 45 minutes): - 5 passages with underlined
portions - Questions on: Usage/Mechanics (40%), Rhetorical Skills (60%)
ACT vs. SAT Writing: | Feature | SAT Writing | ACT English |
sec/Q | ~36 sec/Q (faster!) | | Pictures | Has charts/graphs | Text only | | Difficulty | Gets
harder | Uniform difficulty | | Scoring | 200-800 | 1-36 |
Top ACT English Rules:
Commas (Use SPACED):
Series: apples, oranges, and bananas
The book, which is red, is mine.
Appositive: My brother, a doctor, lives here.
A hot, humid day.
Equal adjectives: Happy and excited, she left.
Direct address: Mom, can I go? Semicolons/Colons:
Semicolon (;): Joins 2 independent clauses (no conjunction)
Colon (:): Introduces list, explanation, or quote
Apostrophes:
The girl’s book (singular), The girls’ books (plural)
Contractions: Don’t, won’t, it’s (it is), its (possessive = NO apostrophe!)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 24.2 ACT English Section. Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Correct? “The girls books are on the table.” - A) The girl’s
books are on the table. - B) The girls’ books are on the table. - C) The girls books
are on the table. - D) The girls’s books are on the table. Answer: B) The girls’
books are on the table. (Plural “girls” + apostrophe at END = girls’
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 24.3 Error Identification (CET Style) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,
managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly.
3. The Build-Up
CET Error ID Format: - Sentence with 4 underlined parts (A, B, C, D) - Choose the part
with ERROR (or “No error”) Common Error Types: Error Type
Subject-Verb
The box of books are heavy.

is (subject = box) Pronoun-Antecedent Everyone brought their book. his or her Verb Tense I have went to Manila. have gone Double Negative I don’t have no money. don’t have any Parallelism I like swimming, to jog. jogging Modifiers Running to school, the tree saw me. I saw the tree running… Pronoun Case Between you and I. me (object = me) Who/Whom Who did you give it to? Whom (object)
Strategy (3 Steps): 1. Read sentence for meaning 2. Check each underlined part
systematically 3. Eliminate obviously correct parts
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
“Each/Every/Everyone” - ALWAYS singular (“Everyone has”, NOT “have”)
“None” - Usually singular in formal writing (“None has arrived”)
“Data” - Plural in formal writing (“The data are conclusive”)
“Who vs. Whom” - Who = subject, Whom = object
Filipino Context Error Identification = Pagtukoy sa Mali Subject-Verb = Simuno-Panaguri
Pronoun = Panghalip
Parallelism = Pagkakapareho ng Anyo
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Find error: “Between you and (A) I, (B) the secret (C) is safe
(D).” - A) A - B) B - C) C - D) D Answer: A) A (“Between you and me” - object = me, not
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 24.4 Sentence Completion before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 24.4 Sentence Completion is key to performing well on the CET. Focus
on core concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up
Sentence Completion Format: - Sentence with 1-2 blanks - Choose word(s) that BEST
fit - Key: Read sentence for CLUES Clue Types: Clue Type Signal Words
Contrast but, however, although, unlike “She is ___ but kind.” (mean) Support and, also, furthermore “He is smart and ___.” (talented) Cause/Effect because, since, therefore
“It rained, ___ the game was canceled.” (so)
such as, for example “She likes fruits, ___ apples.” (like) Definition means, is defined as “A philanthropist is ___.” (generous)
2-Blank Strategy: 1. Predict BOTH words from context 2. Check if they FIT the
sentence 3. Eliminate pairs that don’t match
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 24.4 Sentence Completion. Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “Unlike his gregarious sister, Juan is ___.” (opposite of
gregarious = sociable) - A) outgoing - B) shy - C) happy - D) tall Answer: B) shy
(Unlike = contrast, gregarious = sociable, so Juan = shy
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 24.5 Time Management and Test Strategies before moving
2. The Intuition
Understanding 24.5 Time Management and Test Strategies is key to performing well on
the CET. Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up General CET Test Strategy:
Skip and Return - Hard questions → skip, answer easy first
Eliminate Obvious Wrong - Cross out 2 choices immediately
Watch for Traps - “All of the above”, “None of the above”
Guess if Necessary - No penalty for wrong answers (usually)
Manage Time - Allocate time per question
Time Allocation (CET English ~60 questions, 45 min): - Easy questions: 30 sec
each (30 Qs = 15 min) - Medium questions: 45 sec each (20 Qs = 15 min) - Hard
questions: 60 sec each (10 Qs = 10 min) - Buffer: 5 min for review
Reading Comprehension Strategy (Review): 1. Read QUESTIONS first (know what
to look for) 2. Skim passage (first/last sentences) 3. Read carefully (underline key
points) 4. Answer questions (go BACK to text) 5. Check answers (eliminate traps)
Math Strategy (Review): 1. Read problem carefully (twice if needed) 2. Identify
what’s asked (what is unknown?) 3. Choose formula/method 4. Solve step-by-step
5. Check answer (does it make sense?) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 24.5 Time Management and Test Strategies.
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Strategy for hard questions? - A) Spend 5 minutes each -
B) Skip, return later - C) Guess immediately - D) Leave blank Answer: B) Skip,
return later (Don’t waste time; answer easy ones first!

Chapter Practice Questions
1. SAT: Best revision? “Due to the fact that she is happy, everyone knows.” - A)

blocks.value: is happy, everyone knows. - B) Because she is happy, everyone

happy because.
2. ACT: Correct punctuation? “I bought apples; oranges, and bananas.” - A) I
bought apples; oranges, and bananas. - B) I bought apples, oranges, and bananas. - C) I
bought apples: oranges, and bananas. - D) I bought apples oranges and bananas.
3. Find error: “Each (A) of the boys (B) have (C) a pencil (D).” - A) A - B) B - C) C -
4. “The philanthropist gave ___ to charity.” (clue: generous) - A) nothing - B) little -
C) millions - D) some
5. 90 questions, 60 minutes. Time per Q? - A) 30 sec - B) 45 sec - C) 60 sec - D) 90 sec
6. “Unlike his talkative brother, Juan is ___.” - A) chatty - B) quiet - C) loud - D)
happy
7. Find error: “The girls (A) books (B) are (C) on the table (D).” - A) A - B) B - C) C -
8. SAT: Best revision? “In the event that it rains, cancel.” - A) In the event that it
rains, cancel. - B) If it rains, cancel. - C) The event of rain cancels. - D) Rain event
cancels.
9. “Between you and (A) I, (B) the secret (C) is safe (D).” - A) A - B) B - C) C - D) D
10. Strategy for time management? - A) Answer in order, no skipping - B) Skip hard,
return later - C) Spend 5 min per question - D) Guess all answers
Answer Key
1. B - Shortest correct = “Because she is happy, everyone knows.” 2. B - Series needs
commas: “apples, oranges, and bananas.” 3. C - “Each” = singular → “has”, not “have” 4.
C - Philanthropist = generous → gives millions 5. B - 60 min × 60 sec = 3600 sec ÷ 90 Qs
= 40 sec (closest to B) 45 sec) 6. B - Unlike = contrast, talkative → quiet 7. B - Plural
“girls” + apostrophe at END = girls’ 8. B - Shortest correct = “If it rains, cancel.” 9. A -
“Between you and me” (object = me, not “I”) 10. B - Skip hard questions, return later
(time management) Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas: Common Filipino English Test Mistakes:
❌ “Open the light” → ✅ “Turn on the light”
❌ “The food is eatable” → ✅ “The food is edible”
❌ “My sister is more better” → ✅ “My sister is better”
❌ “I am here for 5 years” → ✅ “I have been here for 5 years”
CET Test-Taking Tips para sa Filipino Students:
Simulan sa madali (Start with easy questions)
Laktawan ang mahirap (Skip hard questions), balikan mamaya (return later)
Tanggalin ang hindi pwede (Eliminate wrong answers)
Huwag mag-overtime (Don’t spend too long on one question)
Hulaan kung kinakailangan (Guess if necessary - no penalty!)
Time Management sa CET:
Math (~60 Qs, 45 min) = ~45 sec/Q
English (~60 Qs, 45 min) = ~45 sec/Q
Science (~80 Qs, 60 min) = ~45 sec/Q
Filipino (~40 Qs, 30 min) = ~45 sec/Q
Abstract Reasoning (~30 Qs, 20 min) = ~40 sec/Q
Common CET Traps (Filipino Context):
“All of the above” = Piliin kung lahat tama (Choose if ALL correct)
“None of the above” = Piliin kung lahat mali (Choose if ALL wrong)
“Except” = Hinuhat (Exception - pick the one that DOESN’T fit!)
“Not” = Hindi (Negative - pick the FALSE statement)
Resources
Free Resources for Test-Taking: - Khan Academy - SAT Practice (official
partnership with College Board) - ACT Academy - Free ACT prep (official) - Eric
Educaton - CET practice tests (Philippines) - Magoosh - SAT/ACT strategies (You Tube,
blog) - College Board - Official SAT practice tests (8 free) - ACT.org - Official ACT
practice questions
SAT Writing/Language: - Khan Academy - SAT Writing practice (personalized) -
Erica Meltzer - SAT Grammar Rules (free PDF) - College Panda - SAT Writing guide
(free excerpts)
ACT English: - ACT Academy - Official practice - The Princeton Review - ACT
English strategies - Barron’s ACT - Practice tests
Practice Tests: - Quizlet - “SAT grammar”, “ACT English”, “Error identification” -
CrackSAT.net - SAT practice tests (free PDFs) - ACT.org - Official ACT practice test
(free)
Chapter Summary: - SAT Writing: 44 Qs/35 min, loves CONCISE
(shortest correct), parallel structure, modifier placement - ACT English: 75
Qs/45 min (faster pace), commas (SPACED), semicolons/colons,
apostrophes - Error ID: Subject-verb, pronoun-antecedent, verb tense,
double negatives, parallelism - Sentence Completion: Read for CLUES
(contrast, support, cause/effect), predict BOTH words - Time Management:
Skip hard → return later, eliminate wrong, ~45 sec/Q - CET Traps: “All of
the above”, “None of the above”, “Except”, “Not” - Filipino Tips: Simulan sa
madali, laktawan ang mahirap, huwag mag-overtime
End Chapter Checkpoint
Review these key questions before moving on:

blocks.value: Exam Strategies 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 42.1 CET Format and Structure before moving on.
2. The Intuition
The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,
managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly.
3. The Build-Up
Typical CET Structure (Varies by School):
Section
Questions

Time Limit Topics Covered 40-60 45-60 mins Arithmetic, Algebra, Geometry, Trig Science 40-60 45-60 mins Bio, Chem, Physics, Earth Science English 50-70 45-60 mins Grammar, Vocab, Reading, Verbal Filipino 30-50 30-45 mins Balarila, Pagbasa, Talasalitaan Abstract Reasoning 30-50 30-45 mins Patterns, Logic, Spatial, Mechanical Section
Questions
Time Limit Topics Covered General Info 30-50 30-45 mins History, Gov’t, Geo, Current Events
Popular CETs in Philippines: - UPCAT (University of the Philippines College
Admission Test) - ACET (Ateneo College Entrance Test) - USTET (University of Santo
Tomás Entrance Test) - DLSUCET (De La Salle University College Entrance Test) -
PUPCET (Polytechnic University of the Philippines College Entrance Test)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 42.1 CET Format and Structure. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: UPCAT is for? - A) Ateneo - B) University of the
Philippines - C) UST - D) La Salle Answer: B) University of the Philippines (UP
College Admission Test ✓
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 42.2 Time Management Techniques before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 42.2 Time Management Techniques is key to performing well on the
CET. Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
The 50-30-20 Rule: - 50% of time: Answer easy questions (secure points) - 30% of time:
Tackle medium questions - 20% of time: Attempt hard questions (if time permits)

Per-Question Time Budget: Section Total Time Per Q Budget Strategy 50 mins 1 min/Q Skip if >2 mins Science 50 mins 50 sec/Q Read passage first English 50 mins 45 sec/Q Grammar faster, Reading slower Filipino 35 mins 50 sec/Q Balarila faster Abstract 35 mins 45 sec/Q
Patterns = 30 sec, Logic = 1 min
Section Total Time Per Q Budget Strategy Gen Info 35 mins 45 sec/Q
Know = instant, Skip unknown
Skip Strategy: 1. Read question (5 sec) 2. If you don’t know instantly → SKIP (mark
for review) 3. Come back if time permits 4. Never spend >2 mins on one question
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 42.2 Time Management Techniques.
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: What to do if stuck >2 mins? - A) Keep trying - B) Skip
and come back - C) Guess randomly - D) Leave blank Answer: B) Skip and come
back (Don’t waste time ✓
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 42.3 Question Analysis Strategies before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 42.3 Question Analysis Strategies is key to performing well on the CET.
Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
READ Method: 1. Read the question carefully (underline keywords) 2. Eliminate
obvious wrong answers 3. Analyze remaining options 4. Decide on best answer
Keyword Spotting: - “EXCEPT” → Look for the one that does NOT fit - “NOT” →
Negative question (reverse logic) - “MOST LIKELY” → Probable answer (not certain) -
“ALWAYS/NEVER” → Usually FALSE (extreme words)
Common Trap Words: - “All”, “Always”, “Never”, “Only” → Usually wrong (too
extreme) - “Some”, “Often”, “Rarely” → Often correct (less extreme) - “EXCEPT” →
Changes question to reverse logic 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 42.3 Question Analysis Strategies. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Which word makes statement FALSE? - A) Often - B)
Sometimes - C) Always - D) Usually Answer: C) Always (Too extreme = usually
FALSE ✓
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 42.4 Elimination Techniques before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 42.4 Elimination Techniques is key to performing well on the CET. Focus
on core concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up
Process of Elimination (POE): 1. Read all options (A, B, C, D) 2. Eliminate obvious
wrong (cross out in test booklet) 3. Compare remaining (which is MOST correct?) 4.
Guess from remaining (if down to 2, guess!)
Common Elimination Patterns: - Grammar: Eliminate wrong tense/agreement →
pick correct - Math: Eliminate impossible answers (too big/small) → solve - Science:
Eliminate factually wrong → pick correct - Abstract: Eliminate shapes/numbers that
don’t fit → find pattern
When Down to 2: - Go with gut (first instinct is usually right) - Pick the “less
extreme” (“often” > “always”) - Pick the “more specific” (specific > general)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 42.4 Elimination Techniques. Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Math: 2+2×3 = ? Options: A) 8, B) 10, C) 12, D) 6 - Eliminate
D) 6 (too small, 2+2=4 minimum) - Eliminate C) 12 (too big, 2×3=6, +2=8) -
Remaining: A) 8, B) 10 - Answer: A) 8 (2 + (2×3) = 2+6 = 8 ✓) Answer**:
Example 2 (Standard): Q: Which is NOT a prime? A) 2, B) 3, C) 5, D) 6 - Eliminate A,
B, C (all primes) - Answer: D) 6 (2×3, NOT prime ✓) Answer**:
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 42.5 Guessing Strategies (When Stuck) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 42.5 Guessing Strategies (When Stuck) is key to performing well on the
CET. Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Smart Guessing (When Time is Running Out):
Section Best Guess Strategy Pick middle number (not extreme) Science Pick “most scientific” answer English Pick grammatically correct option Filipino
Pick “pormal” (formal) over “balbal” (slang)
Abstract
Look for pattern in options (pick similar)
Gen Info Pick “most recent” date/event
Letter of the Day (LOTD): - Pick ONE letter (A, B, C, or D) for ALL guesses -
Statistically: 25% chance vs. random = 20% chance - Never change LOTD mid-exam!
Extreme Word Rule: - “Always/Never/All” → Usually FALSE (pick opposite) -
“Some/Often/Sometimes” → Usually TRUE 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 42.5 Guessing Strategies (When Stuck).
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Extreme word “ALWAYS” in option? - A) Most likely
TRUE - B) Most likely FALSE - C) 50-50 chance - D) Depends on question
Answer: B) Most likely FALSE (Too extreme = usually wrong ✓
6. Practice Problems
Basic:
1. Review core concepts and definitions from this section.
Standard:
2. Apply concepts to sample problems. Exam-Level:
3. Timed practice under exam conditions.
7. One-Page Lock-In Core Definitions & Key Facts: Concept Key Information
Key CET topic — focus on understanding
One Thing to Never Forget: Master the core concepts of 42.5 Guessing Strategies
(When Stuck) for CET success. 8. Section Reminders (Error Prevention)
Understand the fundamentals of 42.5 Guessing Strategies (When Stuck)
thoroughly
Practice with CET-style questions regularly
Review examples step by step
Focus on understanding, not just memorization
Use this section for quick review before the exam
Filipino Context
Guessing Strategy = Istratehiya sa Pag-hula
Letter of the Day = Letra ng Araw / LOTD
Extreme Words = Mga Susing Salitang Sobra
Chapter Practice Questions
1. Longest section in most CETs? - A) Math - B) Science - C) English - D) Abstract
Reasoning
2. Per-Q budget for Math (50 Qs, 50 mins)? - A) 30 seconds - B) 1 minute - C) 1.5
minutes - D) 2 minutes
3. ALL are prime EXCEPT? - A) 2 - B) 3 - C) 4 - D) 5
4. Which word makes statement FALSE? - A) Often - B) Sometimes - C) Always - D)
Usually
5. UPCAT is for? - A) Ateneo - B) University of the Philippines - C) UST - D) La Salle
6. 10 seconds left, 5 blanks. Best strategy? - A) Leave blank - B) Pick random letters -
C) Use LOTD (same letter for all) - D) Guess C for all
8. Extreme word “NEVER” in option? - A) Most likely TRUE - B) Most likely FALSE -
C) 50-50 chance - D) Depends on question
9. Down to 2 options. Strategy? - A) Keep thinking - B) Go with gut (first instinct) - C)
Always pick A - D) Always pick C
10. ACET is for? - A) Ateneo - B) University of the Philippines - C) UST - D) La Salle
Answer Key
1. C) English (50-70 questions, longest ✓) 2. B) 1 minute (50 mins ÷ 50 Qs = 1 min/Q
✓) 3. C) 4 (4 = 2×2, NOT prime ✓) 4. C) Always (Too extreme = usually FALSE ✓) 5.
B) University of the Philippines (UP College Admission Test ✓) 6. C) Use LOTD (25%
chance vs. random 20% ✓) 7. A) 11 (3 + (4×2) = 3+8 = 11 ✓) 8. B) Most likely FALSE
(Too extreme = usually wrong ✓) 9. B) Go with gut (first instinct usually right ✓) 10.
A) Ateneo (Ateneo College Entrance Test ✓)
Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas:
Common CET Mistakes (Filipino Students):
Spending >2 mins on one Q → Skip and come back!
Not using LOTD → Pick one letter for all guesses
Falling for “EXCEPT” → Reverse logic (eliminate correct, pick wrong)
Extreme words (“always/never”) → Usually FALSE
CET Timeline (Test Day):
Arrive 1 hour early (parking, lines, stress)
First hour: Math + Science (hardest, do first)
Second hour: English + Filipino (longest, steady pace)
Last hour: Abstract + Gen Info (fast pace, use LOTD if needed)
Popular CETs:
UPCAT (UP): Hardest, most competitive (10% pass rate)
ACET (Ateneo): Holistic, essay + exam
USTET (UST): Traditional, all sections
DLSUCET (La Salle): Fast-paced, time pressure
Tips para sa CET:
Simulate exam (praktis under timed conditions)
Skip hard Qs (huwag malunod sa mahirap)
Use LOTD (letter of the day for guesses)
Watch extreme words (pansin sa “always/never”)
Resources
Free CET Resources: - UPCAT Reviewer (PDF) - Many online (search “UPCAT
reviewer”) - ACET Practice Tests - Ateneo website (sample tests) - CET Sample
Questions - Various websites - You Tube (CET Tips) - “How to pass UPCAT”, “CET
strategies” - College Entrance Exam Reviewer (PDF) - Comprehensive

Questions.com - Timed practice tests - Past CET exams - Look for leaked/sample tests

- Reddit (r/Philippines) - CET discussions

Flashcards for test strategies - Mga pormal na pagsusulit - Basahin ang “CET
Strategies” by various authors

Management: 50-30-20 Rule (50% easy, 30% medium, 20% hard) - Question
Analysis: READ Method (Read, Eliminate, Analyze, Decide) - Elimination:
Cross out wrong, compare remaining, guess from 2 - Guessing: LOTD
(pick one letter), avoid extremes (“always/never” = FALSE) - Filipino Tips:
Simulate exam, Skip hard Qs, Use LOTD, Watch extreme words
Chapter Summary: - CET Format: Math (40-60 Qs), Science (40-60),

examspec.title: Stress Management & Last-Minute Tips

blocks.value: Exam Timeline 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 43.1 Stress Management Techniques before moving on.
2. The Intuition
is a skill. The students who master exam-day stress
consistently score higher than those who know more content but panic.
3. The Build-Up
Pre-Exam Stress (1-2 Weeks Before): - Sleep: 7-8 hours/night (brain consolidation) -
Exercise: Light exercise (releases endorphins) - Review: Light review only (NO
cramming!) - Diet: Avoid too much caffeine (increases anxiety)
Exam Day Stress (Morning): - Breakfast: Eat light, protein-rich (NOT heavy!) -
Arrive Early: 1 hour before exam (parking, lines) - Breathe: Deep breathing (4-7-8
technique) - Positive Self-Talk: “I am prepared!” NOT “I will fail!”
During Exam Stress: - Skip Hard Qs: Don’t panic if stuck (skip, come back) -
Breathe: Deep breaths if anxious - Focus: One question at a time (forget past, ignore
future) - Positive: “I can do this!” (mantra)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 43.1 Stress Management Techniques.
Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Breathing technique for stress? - A) 2-3-4 - B) 4-7-8 - C)
10-10-10 - D) 1-2-3 Answer: B) 4-7-8 (Inhale 4 sec, hold 7, exhale 8 ✓

The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,

yourself (45 sec/Q, don’t rush!)
CET Filipino Tips: - Balarila rules (NG vs. NANG, aspects) - Read passage first
(main idea, konklusyon) - Underline keywords (EXCEPT, HINDI, PALAGI) - Pace
yourself (50 sec/Q, Filipino = faster!) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

1. Pre-exam sleep recommendation? - A) 4-5 hours - B) 7-8 hours - C) 10-12 hours -
D) All-nighter
2. Hardest CET (lowest pass rate)? - A) UPCAT - B) ACET - C) USTET - D) DLSUCET
3. Week before CET, BEST strategy? - A) Cram new topics - B) Light review, sleep
well - C) All-nighter - D) Skip sleep
4. REQUIRED to bring to CET? - A) Ballpen - B) No. 2 Pencil - C) Calculator - D)
Smartwatch
5. Holistic review (essay + interview)? - A) UPCAT - B) ACET - C) USTET - D)
DLSUCET
6. Breathing technique for stress? - A) 2-3-4 - B) 4-7-8 - C) 10-10-10 - D) 1-2-3
7. Most fatal mistake? - A) Spending >2 mins on Q - B) NOT bringing pencil - C)
Changing answers - D) Reading too fast
8. CET Math - stuck on Q >2 mins? - A) Keep trying - B) Skip, come back - C) Guess
randomly - D) Leave blank
9. Day before CET, what to do? - A) Cram all night - B) NO CRAMMING, sleep early -
C) Party with friends - D) Pull all-nighter
10. CET Science - read passage? - A) Skip passage, go to Qs - B) Read passage first! -
C) Read Qs first - D) Guess all answers Answer Key
1. B) 7-8 hours (Brain consolidation ✓) 2. A) UPCAT (~10-15% pass rate ✓) 3. B) Light
review, sleep well (Brain needs rest ✓) 4. B) No. 2 Pencil (NOT pen! Pencil only ✓) 5.
B) ACET (Ateneo, holistic review ✓) 6. B) 4-7-8 (Inhale 4, hold 7, exhale 8 ✓) 7. B)
NOT bringing pencil (Exam not accepted ✓) 8. B) Skip, come back (Don’t waste time
✓) 9. B) NO CRAMMING, sleep early (Brain needs rest ✓) 10. B) Read passage first!
(Science = passage-based ✓) Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas:
Common Stress Mistakes (Filipino Students):
Cramming (lutang) → Light review only! Not sleeping (puyat) → 7-8 hours/night! Arriving late (mahuhi) → 1 hour early!
Forgetting pencil (lapis) → Check twice!
CET Comparison:
UPCAT = Hardest (10-15% pass, ~90+ percentile)
ACET = Holistic (essay + interview)
USTET = Traditional (all sections)
DLSUCET = Fast-paced (time pressure)
Day-of Tips: Maagang punta (arrive 1 hour early)
Komportableng damit (comfortable clothing)
Lapis (No. 2) (NOT ballpen!) Positive mindset (“Kaya ko ‘to!”) Tips para sa CET:
Simulate exam (praktis under timed conditions)
Skip hard Qs (huwag malunod sa mahirap)
Use LOTD (letter of the day for guesses)
Breathe (malalim na paghinga when stressed)

examspec.title: Subject-Specific Tips & Tricks

blocks.value: Subject Tips 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 44.1 CET Math Specific Tips before moving on.
2. The Intuition
The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,
managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly.
3. The Build-Up
Math Topics (Most Tested): 1. Arithmetic: Fractions, decimals, percentages, ratios 2.
Algebra: Linear equations, quadratics, inequalities 3. Geometry: Area, perimeter,
volume, Pythagorean theorem 4. Trigonometry: SOH-CAH-TOA, unit circle 5. Word
Problems: Age, motion, work, mixture
Math Strategy (Step-by-Step): 1. Read carefully (underline what’s asked!) 2.
Identify topic (algebra? geometry?) 3. Write formula (write it down!) 4. Solve step-
by-step (show work on scratch!) 5. Check units (convert if needed!)
Common Math Traps: - Extraneous solutions (√x = -2? NO!) - Wrong units (meters
vs. centimeters) - Forgetting formulas (memorize! Pythagorean, Area, Volume)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 44.1 CET Math Specific Tips. Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: 30% of 200 = ? - A) 50 - B) 60 - C) 70 - D) 80 Answer: B) 60
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 44.2 CET Science Specific Tips before moving on.
2. The Intuition
The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,
managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly.
3. The Build-Up
Science Topics (Most Tested): 1. Biology: Cells, genetics, ecology, human body 2.
Chemistry: Elements, compounds, reactions, pH 3. Physics: Motion, energy, waves,
electricity 4. Earth Science: Rocks, weather, solar system
Science Strategy (Passage-Based): 1. Read passage FIRST (2-3 mins!) 2. Underline
keywords (dates, names, numbers) 3. Answer Qs in order (usually passage order) 4.
Eliminate wrong (cross out in booklet) 5. Watch units (convert if needed!)
Common Science Traps: - Contradictory passages (old info vs. new info) -
“EXCEPT” questions (reverse logic!) - “According to the passage” (NOT your
knowledge!) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 44.2 CET Science Specific Tips. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: ALL are organelles EXCEPT? - A) Mitochondria - B)
Nucleus - C) Water - D) Chloroplast Answer: C) Water (NOT an organele ✓)
[EXCEPT = reverse!]
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 44.3 CET English Specific Tips before moving on.
2. The Intuition
The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,
managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly.
3. The Build-Up
English Topics (Most Tested): 1. Grammar: Subject-verb agreement, tenses,
prepositions 2. Vocabulary: Synonyms, antonyms, context clues 3. Reading
Comprehension: Main idea, inference, tone 4. Verbal Reasoning: Analogies,
syllogisms
English Strategy (Grammar): 1. Find subject/verb (underline them!) 2. Check
agreement (singular/plural) 3. Watch tense (consistent past/present?) 4. Eliminate
wrong (cross out in booklet)
English Strategy (Reading): 1. Read passage FIRST (2-3 mins!) 2. Underline topic
sentence (first/last sentence) 3. Answer main idea Q FIRST (easiest!) 4. Inference =
“most likely” (NOT certain!) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 44.3 CET English Specific Tips. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Reading: Main idea of passage about dogs? - A) Dogs are
loyal pets ✓ - B) Dogs have fur - C) Dogs eat meat - D) Dogs run fast Answer: A)
are loyal pets (Covers whole passage ✓
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 44.4 CET Filipino Specific Tips before moving on.
2. The Intuition
The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,
managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly.
3. The Build-Up
Filipino Topics (Most Tested): 1. Balarila: Mga bahagi ng pananalita, aspekto ng
pandiwa 2. Pagbasa: Pangunahing ideya, paghuhulo, tono 3. Talasalitaan: Mga salitang
Filipino, idioma 4. Pagpapaliwanag: Analogiya, silogismo
Filipino Strategy (Balarila): 1. Hanapin ang paksa/pandiwa (underline!) 2. Tignan
ang aspekto (nakaraan/kasalukuyan/hinaharap) 3. NG vs. NANG (pag-aari vs.
pamaraan!) 4. Eliminate mali (tanggalin sa booklet)
Filipino Strategy (Pagbasa): 1. Basahin ang pasahe UNA (2-3 mins!) 2.
Salungguhitan ang topic sentence (unang/huling pangungusap) 3. Sagutin ang
pangunahing ideya UNA (pinakamadali!) 4. Paghuhulo = “malamang” (HINDI
sigurado!) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 44.4 CET Filipino Specific Tips. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Pagbasa: Pangunahing ideya ng tungkol sa aso? - A) Ang
aso ay tapat na alaga ✓ - B) Ang aso ay may balahibo - C) Ang aso ay kumakain
ng karne - D) Ang aso ay tumatakbo Answer: A) Ang aso ay tapat na alaga (Sakop
ang buong talata ✓
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 44.5 CET Abstract Reasoning Tips before moving on.
2. The Intuition
The CET is as much about strategy as it is about knowledge. Knowing the format,
managing time, and using elimination techniques can boost your score significantly.
3. The Build-Up
Abstract Topics (Most Tested): 1. Patterns: Visual, number, geometric 2. Logic:
Syllogisms, analogies, sequences 3. Spatial: Mental rotation, 2D→3D, orientation 4.
Mechanical: Simple machines, forces, motion
Abstract Strategy (Patterns): 1. Identify changing element (shape? color? number?)
2. Find the rule (how does it change?) 3. Predict next (apply rule!) 4. Match to
options (eliminate wrong!)
Abstract Strategy (Logic): 1. Read premises carefully (underline keywords!) 2.
Check validity (logic correct?) 3. Eliminate wrong (cross out in booklet) 4. Watch
for “EXCEPT” (reverse logic!) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 44.5 CET Abstract Reasoning Tips. Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Syllogism: All A are B. All C are A. Therefore? - A) All C
are B ✓ (Deductive: A→B, C→A, ∴ C→B) - B) All B are C - C) Some C are B - D)
are B Answer: A) All C are B (Deductive logic ✓
6. Practice Problems
Basic:
1. Review core concepts and definitions from this section.
Standard:
2. Apply concepts to sample problems. Exam-Level:
3. Timed practice under exam conditions.
7. One-Page Lock-In Core Definitions & Key Facts: Concept Key Information
Key CET topic — focus on understanding
One Thing to Never Forget: Master the core concepts of 44.5 CET Abstract Reasoning
Tips for CET success. 8. Section Reminders (Error Prevention)
Understand the fundamentals of 44.5 CET Abstract Reasoning Tips thoroughly
Practice with CET-style questions regularly
Review examples step by step
Focus on understanding, not just memorization
Use this section for quick review before the exam
Filipino Context Abstract Tips = Mga Tip sa Abstract
Patterns = Mga Pattern / Tulaan
Logic = Lohika
Spatial = Spisyal / Pang-espasyo
Chapter Practice Questions
1. Math: Right triangle, legs=5,12. Hypotenuse? - A) 13 ✓ (5²+12²=25+144=169,
2. Science: Passage: “Mitochondria produce ATP.” Which produces ATP? - A)
Nucleus - B) Mitochondria ✓ - C) Ribosome - D) Chloroplast
3. English Grammar: “The list of books ___ heavy.” - A) is ✓ (subject = list, singular)
- B) are - C) were - D) have
4. Filipino Balarila: “Ang listahan ng mga libro ay ___ mabigat.” - A) ay ✓ (simuno
= listahan, isahan) - B) ay - C) ay - D) ay
7. Science: ALL are organs EXCEPT? - A) Heart - B) Brain - C) Blood ✓ (NOT an
organ!) - D) Liver
8. English Reading: Main idea of passage about jeepneys? - A) Jeepneys are
colorful Filipino transport ✓ - B) Jeepneys have wheels - C) Jeepneys use gasoline - D)
Jeepneys are loud
9. Filipino Pagbasa: Pangunahing ideya tungkol sa dyipni? - A) Ang dyipni ay
makulay na sasakyan ✓ - B) Ang dyipni ay may gulong - C) Ang dyipni ay gumagamit
ng gasolina - D) Ang dyipni ay maingay
10. Abstract: Syllogism: All X are Y. All Z are X. Therefore? - A) All Z are Y ✓
(Deductive: X→Y, Z→X, ∴ Z→Y) - B) All Y are Z - C) Some Z are Y - D) No Z are Y
Answer Key
1. A) 13 (Pythagorean: 5²+12²=169, √169=13 ✓)) 2. B) Mitochondria (According to
passage ✓)) 3. A) is (Subject = list, singular ✓)) 4. A) ay (Simuno = listahan, isahan ✓))
5. C) ●●●● (+1 dot each step ✓)) 6. B) 20 (0.25 × 80 = 20 ✓)) 7. C) Blood (NOT an organ
✓) [EXCEPT = reverse!]) 8. A) Jeepneys are colorful Filipino transport (Covers
whole passage ✓)) 9. A) Ang dyipni ay makulay na sasakyan (Covers whole passage
✓)) 10. A) All Z are Y (Deductive logic ✓))
Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas:
Common Subject-Specific Mistakes (Filipino Students):
Math: Forgetting formulas (pormula → memorise! Pythagorean, Area, Volume)
Science: Using own knowledge (gumamit ng sariling alam) → Use passage only!
English: Subject-verb agreement (paksa-pandiwa) → Find subject first!
Filipino: NG vs. NANG (pag-aari vs. pamaraan) → Tandaan!
Abstract: Spending >2 mins (gugol ng >2 minuto) → Skip, come back!
Subject Tips (Mga Tip sa Bawat Subject):
Math: Memorise formulas (isaisip ang mga pormula!)
Science: Read passage FIRST (basahin ang pasahe UNA!)
English: Grammar = find subject/verb (hanapin ang paksa/pandiwa!)
Filipino: Balarila = NG vs. NANG (pag-aari vs. pamaraan!)
Abstract: Patterns = find rule (hanapin ang tuntunin!)
Test Day Strategies (Estratehiya sa Araw ng Eksam):
Math: Spend max 1 min/Q (gastos max 1 min/bawat tanong!)
Science: Passage first (pasahe muna!), then Qs
English: Grammar faster (balarila = mas mabilis!), Reading slower
Filipino: Balarila faster (mas mabilis!), Pagbasa slower
Abstract: Skip hard patterns (laktawan ang mahirap!), come back
Tips para sa CET:
Simulate exam (praktis under timed conditions)
Subject-specific review (pagsusuri sa bawat subject)
Use LOTD (letter of the day for guesses)
Breathe (huminga nang malalim when stressed!)
Resources
Free Subject-Specific Resources: - Khan Academy - Math, Science, English (all
levels) - Purde OWL - English grammar, writing - Quizlet - “CET Math”, “CET
Science”, “CET English” - Test-Questions.com - Subject-specific practice Qs - You Tube
(Subject Tutorials) - “CET Math Tips”, “CET Science Strategies”
CET Practice Tests (Subject-Specific): - UPCAT Reviewer (PDF) - Search online
(“UPCAT reviewer 2024”) - ACET Practice Tests - Ateneo website (sample tests) -
USTET Reviewer - Various websites - DLSUCET Practice - La Salle website
Subject-Specific Tips (You Tube): - Math Tips - “CET Math Formulas”, “CET Math
Strategies” - Science Tips - “CET Science Passage Strategy” - English Tips - “CET
English Grammar”, “CET Reading Comprehension” - Filipino Tips - “CET Filipino
Balarila”, “CET Filipino Pagbasa” - Abstract Tips - “CET Abstract Patterns”, “CET
Abstract Logic”
Practice Tests: - Quizlet - “CET Subject Tips”, “CET Math Formulas” -
StudyStack.com - Flashcards for each subject - Mga pormal na pagsusulit - Basahin
ang “CET Strategies” by various authors
Chapter Summary: - Math Tips: Memorise formulas (Pythagorean, Area,
Volume), 1 min/Q max - Science Tips: Read passage FIRST, Answer in
order, Eliminate wrong - English Tips: Grammar = find subject/verb,
Reading = main idea FIRST - Filipino Tips: Balarila = NG vs. NANG,
Pagbasa = passage FIRST - Abstract Tips: Patterns = find rule, Logic =
check validity - Filipino Tips: Simula exam, Subject-specific review, Use
LOTD, Breathe End Chapter Checkpoint

examspec.description: Covers subject-specific tips and tricks for Math, Science, English, Filipino, and Abstract Reasoning.

filipino.title: Balarila I: Bahagi ng Pananalita

blocks.value: Ang pangngalan (noun) ay salitang tumutukoy sa tao, bagay, lugar, o pangyayari. Ito ang isa sa mga pinakapangunahing bahagi ng pananalita sa Filipino. Sa CET, madalas itanong ang pagkakaiba ng mga uri ng pangngalan at tamang paggamit ng mga ito sa pangungusap.
Tumutukoy sa natatanging tao, lugar, o bagay. Karaniwang nagsisimula sa malaking titik. Halimbawa: Manila, Maria, Rizal, Enero.
Tumutukoy sa pangkalahatang tao, bagay, o lugar. Halimbawa: lungsod, babae, bayani, buwan.
Mga bagay na nahahawakan o nakikita. Halimbawa: mesa, tubig, libro, bola.
Mga ideya, damdamin, o katangiang hindi nahahawakan. Halimbawa: pag-ibig, katarungan, kaligayahan, talino.
Tumutukoy sa grupo ng tao, hayop, o bagay bilang isa. Halimbawa: pangkat (group), kawan (flock), tropa (troop), bayan (nation).

Sa Filipino, may dalawang paraan ng pagbuo ng maramihang pangngalan. Una, ang pagdaragdag ng unlaping 'mga' — halimbawa, 'bata' ay naging 'mga bata.' Ito ang pinakakaraniwang paraan. Pangalawa, ang pag-uulit ng salita — halimbawa, 'araw-araw' (every day) mula sa 'araw' (day). Tandaan na ang 'mga' ay hindi bahagi ng pangngalan mismo kundi isang marker ng plural.

Ang panghalip (pronoun) ay salitang pumapalit sa pangngalan. Sa Filipino, ang mga panghalip ay may iba't ibang anyo depende sa relasyon sa pandiwa (kung sino ang gumagawa, tinutukoy, o pinag-uusapan). Ito ang tinatawag na 'case' ng panghalip.
Ako/si/akin (I/me), ikaw/ka/mo (you), siya/niya (he/she/it), kami/tayo (we), sila/nila (they). May tatlong 'case': absolutive (paksa), ergative (may-ari/ng), at oblique (sa/para sa).

blocks.title: Panghalip Panao sa Pangungusap
blocks.value: Absolutive (paksa): AKO ang kumain. ERGATIVE (may-ari/ng): Kinain KO ang mansanas. OBLIQUE (sa/para): Ibinigay sa AKIN ang libro. — Ipinakikita ng tatlong halimbawa na iba ang anyo ng 'ako' depende sa kanyang papel sa pangungusap.
Sino (who), ano (what), alin (which), saan (where), kailan (when), bakit (why), paano (how). Ginagamit sa pagtatanong.
