geninfo.title: PH Geography & Resources

blocks.value: The Philippines is divided into 17 administrative regions, each grouping provinces with shared geographic, cultural, or economic characteristics. Knowing these regions is essential for CET questions on governance, geography, and current events.
An administrative division of the Philippines grouping several provinces for coordinated governance and development planning.
The three main island groups are Luzon (northern), Visayas (central), and Mindanao (southern). Luzon has 8 regions, Visayas has 3, and Mindanao has 6 (including BARMM).
Luzon Regions (8): NCR, CAR, Region I (Ilocos), Region II (Cagayan Valley), Region III (Central Luzon), Region IV-A (CALABARZON), Region IV-B (MIMAROPA), Region V (Bicol)
Visayas Regions (3): Region VI (Western Visayas), Region VII (Central Visayas), Region VIII (Eastern Visayas)
Mindanao Regions (6): Region IX (Zamboanga Peninsula), Region X (Northern Mindanao), Region XI (Davao), Region XII (SOCCSKSARGEN), Region XIII (Caraga), BARMM
NCR (National Capital Region) is the smallest region by land area but the most densely populated. It contains 16 cities including Manila, Quezon City, Makati, Taguig (BGC), Pasig, and Mandaluyong.

CALABARZON stands for Cavite, Laguna, Batangas, Rizal, and Quezon. It is the most populous region and a major industrial hub south of Metro Manila. Tagaytay in Cavite offers a famous view of Taal Volcano.

CAR (Cordillera Administrative Region) is the only landlocked region in the Philippines. It includes Baguio City (the Summer Capital) and the provinces of Benguet, Ifugao, Mountain Province, Abra, Kalinga, and Apayao. The Banaue Rice Terraces are in Ifugao.

Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao — the newest autonomous region, replacing ARMM in 2019. Provinces: Basilan, Lanao del Sur, Maguindanao del Norte, Maguindanao del Sur, Sulu, Tawi-Tawi. Capital: Cotabato City.

MIMAROPA stands for Mindoro, Marinduque, Romblon, and Palawan. Palawan is known as the 'last ecological frontier' of the Philippines and home to the Puerto Princesa Underground River, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
SOCCSKSARGEN stands for South Cotabato, Cotabato, Sultan Kudarat, Sarangani, and General Santos. General Santos City is the 'Tuna Capital of the Philippines.'
Example: Q: Which region is the only landlocked region in the Philippines? A) CAR — The Cordillera Administrative Region is entirely mountainous with no coastline.

CET Tip: Memorize the region numbers and their acronyms. Questions often ask which provinces belong to CALABARZON, MIMAROPA, or SOCCSKSARGEN. The 2019 Bangsamoro Organic Law created BARMM — a frequent current events question.
A city with a minimum population of 200,000 and annual income of at least ₱50 million. HUCs are independent of their parent provinces. Examples: Cebu City, Davao City, Zamboanga City.
Practice: Match the region acronym to its component provinces. List all 17 regions from north to south. Identify which island group each region belongs to.

Manila is the official capital of the Philippines and the seat of the national government. It is one of the 16 cities of Metro Manila (NCR). Quezon City is the largest city by population and was the capital from 1948 to 1976.
The National Capital Region — a metropolitan area of 16 cities and 1 municipality (Pateros). It is the political, economic, and cultural center of the Philippines.
Makati and Taguig (Bonifacio Global City) serve as the financial centers of the Philippines. The Philippine Stock Exchange, major banks, and multinational corporations are headquartered here.

Baguio City in Benguet province (CAR) is the 'Summer Capital of the Philippines' due to its cool mountain climate at ~1,500 meters elevation. It was designed by American architect Daniel Burnham in 1905.

Major Cities by Island Group: Luzon — Manila, Quezon City, Makati, Baguio, Angeles, Olongapo, Dagupan, Laoag, Vigan, Legazpi. Visayas — Cebu City, Iloilo City, Bacolod, Tacloban, Dumaguete, Tagbilaran. Mindanao — Davao City, Cagayan de Oro, Zamboanga City, General Santos, Cotabato, Butuan, Iligan, Marawi.
Davao City is the largest city in the Philippines by land area (2,444 km²). It is the economic center of Mindanao and home to Mount Apo, the country's highest peak.
Cebu City is the 'Queen City of the South' and the oldest city in the Philippines. It is a major shipping hub, IT-BPO center, and tourist gateway to the Visayas.

Vigan City in Ilocos Sur is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It preserves Spanish colonial architecture with cobblestone streets (Calle Crisologo) and is the best-preserved example of a Spanish colonial town in Asia.
A city that is part of a province and subject to provincial governance. Example: Tagaytay (Cavite), San Fernando (La Union). Contrast with Highly Urbanized Cities (HUCs) which are independent.

Iloilo City is known as the 'City of Love' and is a major food destination. The Iloilo River Esplanade and heritage houses attract tourists. Bacolod City in Negros Occidental is the 'City of Smiles' and hosts the MassKara Festival.
Example: Q: What is the Summer Capital of the Philippines? A) Baguio — Located in Benguet, CAR, known for its cool climate and pine trees.

CET Tip: Know the difference between Manila (capital city) and Metro Manila (the metropolitan region). Questions may ask about the former capital (Quezon City), the financial center (Makati/BGC), or the largest city by area (Davao City).
Practice: Identify whether each city is an HUC or component city. List 5 cities from Visayas and their provinces. What was the capital of the Philippines before Manila?

The Philippine archipelago consists of 7,641 islands, making it the second largest archipelago in the world after Indonesia. The three main island groups — Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao — are surrounded by the Pacific Ocean, Philippine Sea, South China Sea (West Philippine Sea), Sulu Sea, and Celebes Sea.
A group of islands. The Philippines is an archipelagic state — its territory is primarily composed of islands surrounded by water.
Highest Peaks: Mt. Apo (2,954m, Davao) — highest in PH; Mt. Pulag (2,922m, Benguet) — highest in Luzon; Mt. Dulang-Dulang (2,938m, Bukidnon) — 2nd highest; Mt. Kitanglad Range — important watershed.

The Sierra Madre is the longest mountain range in the Philippines, stretching approximately 540 km along the eastern coast of Luzon from Cagayan province to Quezon province. It serves as a natural barrier against typhoons from the Pacific.

The Cordillera Central is the largest mountain range in the Philippines, located in the northern Luzon highlands. It contains the headwaters of major river systems and is home to the Igorot indigenous peoples.

Major Rivers: Cagayan River (520 km, longest) — flows through Cagayan Valley to the Babuyan Channel; Pampanga River (260 km) — drains Central Luzon plain; Agno River (220 km) — flows through Pangasinan; Rio Grande de Mindanao (373 km) — longest in Mindanao.

Laguna de Bay is the largest lake in the Philippines (900 km²), located between Laguna and Rizal provinces near Metro Manila. The Pasig River connects it to Manila Bay. It provides water supply and fishery resources but faces pollution challenges.
The Philippine Trench (also called the Philippine Deep) is one of the deepest ocean trenches in the world, reaching approximately 10,540 meters. It lies east of the Philippines in the Pacific Ocean.

The official Philippine name for the eastern part of the South China Sea that falls within the Philippines' exclusive economic zone (EEZ). The 2016 Permanent Court of Arbitration ruling upheld the Philippines' sovereign rights in this area.

Major Volcanoes: Mt. Mayon (Albay) — perfect cone shape, most active volcano; Taal Volcano (Batangas) — one of the smallest active volcanoes, located on an island within a lake; Mt. Pinatubo (Zambales) — famous 1991 eruption that cooled global temperatures; Mt. Kanlaon (Negros) — highest point in the Visayas.

The Philippine archipelago lies on the Pacific Ring of Fire, making it prone to earthquakes and volcanic eruptions. The country has about 20 active volcanoes and experiences frequent seismic activity.
The sea zone extending 200 nautical miles from a country's coast where it has exclusive rights to explore and exploit marine resources. The Philippines' EEZ covers approximately 2.2 million km².
Example: Q: What is the longest river in the Philippines? A) Cagayan River — 520 km, flowing through the Cagayan Valley in northern Luzon.

CET Tip: Memorize superlatives — longest river (Cagayan), highest peak (Mt. Apo), largest lake (Laguna de Bay), longest mountain range (Sierra Madre), deepest trench (Philippine Trench). These are frequent CET questions.
Practice: Name 3 active volcanoes and their locations. What body of water connects Laguna de Bay to Manila Bay? Explain why the Philippines is in the Pacific Ring of Fire.

The Philippines is rich in natural resources including minerals, forests, marine life, geothermal energy, and agricultural land. However, managing these resources sustainably remains a major challenge.
Materials or substances that occur in nature and can be used for economic gain. They include renewable resources (forests, fish, water) and non-renewable resources (minerals, fossil fuels).

The Philippines is one of the most biodiverse countries in the world. It is identified as a 'mega-diverse' country, home to about 5% of the world's flora and fauna despite having only 0.2% of the world's land area.

Key Mineral Resources: Gold — 2nd largest gold reserve (after South Korea); Copper — major deposits in Luzon and Mindanao; Nickel — 2nd largest producer worldwide; Chromite — found in Zambales and Dinagat Islands.

The Philippines is the world's second-largest producer of nickel ore, a key component in stainless steel and electric vehicle batteries. Major nickel mining areas are in Surigao, Palawan, and Zambales.

Geothermal energy: The Philippines is the 3rd largest geothermal energy producer in the world (after the USA and Indonesia). Major geothermal plants are in Leyte (Tongonan), Albay (Tiwi), and Negros Oriental (Bacon-Manito).
Energy derived from heat within the Earth's crust. The Philippines' location on the Ring of Fire gives it abundant geothermal resources, providing about 10-12% of the country's electricity.

Marine resources: The Philippines has one of the longest coastlines in the world (~36,000 km). The Coral Triangle — the global center of marine biodiversity — includes Philippine waters. Tubbataha Reefs Natural Park in the Sulu Sea is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Agricultural regions: Central Luzon (Region III) is the 'Rice Granary of the Philippines.' The Cagayan Valley is another major rice-producing area. Coconut production is highest in Quezon, Davao, and Zamboanga. Sugar is grown in Negros Occidental ('Sugar Bowl of the Philippines').

Environmental issues: Deforestation — Philippine forest cover dropped from 70% in 1900 to about 20% today due to logging, agriculture, and urbanization. Air pollution — Metro Manila has some of the worst air quality in Southeast Asia. Water pollution — the Pasig River is one of the most polluted rivers in the world.

Species at risk of extinction. Philippine examples: Philippine Eagle (national bird, critically endangered), Tarsier (world's smallest primate), Pawikan (sea turtles), Tamaraw (dwarf buffalo found only in Mindoro).

Climate: The Philippines has a tropical maritime climate with two seasons — dry (November to April) and wet (May to October). The country is visited by an average of 20 typhoons per year, about 5-6 of which are destructive. It lies in the 'typhoon belt' of the western Pacific.

Typhoon classifications by PAGASA: Tropical Depression (<63 kph), Tropical Storm (63-88 kph), Severe Tropical Storm (89-117 kph), Typhoon (118-184 kph), Super Typhoon (>185 kph). Typhoon Haiyan (Yolanda, 2013) was the strongest to make landfall globally.
Example: Q: Why is the Philippines a major geothermal energy producer? A) Because it sits on the Pacific Ring of Fire, giving it abundant volcanic heat that can be converted to electricity.
CET Tip: Questions on Philippine resources often link to environmental issues. Know the country's mega-diverse status, major agricultural products by region, and the typhoon classification system.
Practice: List 3 renewable and 3 non-renewable resources of the Philippines. What is the Coral Triangle? Explain how the typhoon belt affects Philippine agriculture.

Economic geography examines how location, resources, and geography influence economic activity. The Philippines' archipelagic nature creates both opportunities (marine resources, tourism) and challenges (transportation costs, regional inequality).
The study of how economic activities are distributed across space and how geographic factors influence production, trade, and development.

The Philippines has a service-oriented economy, with services (BPO, tourism, finance) contributing about 60% of GDP. Industry (manufacturing, construction) contributes about 30%, and agriculture about 10%. The IT-BPO sector makes the Philippines the 'Call Center Capital of the World.'

Philippine Nearest Neighbors: Taiwan (north, across Bashi Channel); China (northwest, across South China Sea); Vietnam (west); Malaysia (south, via Sabah); Indonesia (south, via Sulawesi/Mindanao Sea); Japan (northeast).

ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations): The Philippines is a founding member (1967) along with Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. ASEAN promotes regional economic cooperation, political stability, and cultural exchange. The ASEAN headquarters is in Jakarta, Indonesia.

Association of Southeast Asian Nations — a regional organization of 10 member states (Philippines, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam, Brunei, Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar) founded in 1967 to promote regional cooperation.

The Sabah dispute: The Philippines has a historical claim over Sabah (North Borneo), based on the Sultanate of Sulu's historical sovereignty. Malaysia also claims Sabah. This remains a sensitive diplomatic issue.

Key trade partners: China is the Philippines' largest trading partner (imports and exports). Japan, the USA, South Korea, and Hong Kong are also major partners. OFW remittances (about $30+ billion/year) are a critical source of foreign exchange.
The Philippines' strategic location in the western Pacific makes it important for global shipping routes. The Malacca Strait and South China Sea carry about one-third of global shipping traffic.

Special Economic Zones: PEZA (Philippine Economic Zone Authority) manages economic zones that attract foreign investment with tax incentives. Major zones include those in Clark (Pampanga), Subic (Zambales), and Cebu.
Example: Q: Which organization was the Philippines a founding member of in 1967? A) ASEAN — along with Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, and Thailand.

CET Tip: Know the ASEAN member states and founding date (August 8, 1967). Questions may ask about Philippine territorial disputes, major trade partners, or the economic significance of OFW remittances.
Practice: Name all 10 ASEAN member states. What is the significance of the West Philippine Sea dispute? How do OFW remittances affect the Philippine economy?

geninfo.description: Covers the 17 regions, physical features, natural resources, and economic geography of the Philippines.

blocks.value: Current Events 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 39.1 Ancient Civilizations before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 39.1 Ancient Civilizations is key to performing well on the CET. Focus on
core concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up Major Ancient Civilizations: Civilization Timeline Key Contributions Mesopotamia (Tigris- Euphrates) 3500-500 BCE
Cuneiform (1st writing), Code of Hammurabi,

Wheel Ancient Egypt (Nile River) 3100-332 BCE Pyramids, Hieroglyphics, Calendar, Mummification Ancient Greece (Aegean Sea) 800-146 BCE Democracy, Philosphy (Socrates, Plato, Aristotle), Olympics Civilization Timeline Key Contributions Roman Empire (Italy/ Mediterranean) 753 BCE-476
Republic, Law, Engineering (roads, aqueducts),
Latin Ancient China (Yellow River) 2100 BCE-220
Great Wall, Silk Road, Paper, Gunpowder,
Compass Indus Valley (India/Pakistan) 3300-1300 BCE Urban planning (grid cities), Plumbing, Standard weights
Key Dates: - 776 BCE: 1st Olympic Games (Greece) - 509 BCE: Roman Republic
founded - 450 BCE: 1st Roman Law (Twelve Tables) - 323 BCE: Death of Alexander the
Great (Hellenistic period) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 39.1 Ancient Civilizations. Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Philosphy (Socrates, Plato, Aristotle)? - A) Mesopotamia -
B) Egypt - C) Greece - D) Rome Answer: C) Greece (Golden Age of Athens ✓
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 39.2 World War I (1914-1918) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 39.2 World War I (1914-1918) is key to performing well on the CET. Focus
on core concepts and their application. 3. The Build-Up
Causes of WWI (MANIA): - Militarism (arms race) - Alliances (Triple Alliance vs.
Triple Entente) - Nationalism (ethnic tensions) - Imperialism (colonial competition) -
Assassination (Archduke Franz Ferdinand, June 28, 1914 — trigger)
Major Events: - 1914: War begins (Austria-Hungary declares war on Serbia) - 1917:
USA enters war (Zimmerman Telegram, unrestricted submarine warfare) - 1918:
Armistice (November 11, 11AM — “Eleventh hour of the eleventh day”)
Treaty of Versailles (1919): - Germany: Lost territory, disarmed, pay reparations ($33
billion) - League of Nations: Founded (but USA never joined!)
Major Powers: - Allies: France, UK, Russia (until 1917), USA (1917+) - Central
Powers: Germany, Austria-Hungary, Ottoman Empire
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 39.2 World War I (1914-1918). Practice with
sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: “Eleventh hour of the eleventh day” (1918)? - A) War
begins - B) USA enters war - C) Armistice (end of fighting) - D) Treaty signed
Answer: C) Armistice (Nov 11, 11AM, 1918 ✓
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 39.3 World War II (1939-1945) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 39.3 World War II (1939-1945) is key to performing well on the CET.
Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Causes of WWII: - Treaty of Versailles (harsh on Germany → Hitler’s rise) - Rise of
Fascism (Hitler in Germany, Mussolini in Italy, Hirohito in Japan) - Policy of
Appeasement (UK/France gave Sudetenland to Hitler) - Nazi-Soviet Pact (1939:
Germany + USSR agreed not to fight)
Major Events: - 1939: War begins (Germany invades Poland, Sept 1) - 1941: Pearl
Harbor (Dec 7 — USA enters war) - 1944: D-Day (June 6, Normandy landings —
liberation of France) - 1945: Germany surrenders (May 8), Japan surrenders (Sept 2, after
atomic bombs)
Holocaust (Shoah): - 6 million Jews killed by Nazis (extermination camps: Auschwitz,
Treblinka) - Other victims: Roma, disabled, homosexuals, political prisoners (total ~11
million)
Atomic Bombs (1945): - Hiroshima (Aug 6, 1945): “Little Boy” (140,000+ killed) -
Nagasaki (Aug 9, 1945): “Fat Man” (70,000+ killed)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 39.3 World War II (1939-1945). Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: First atomic bomb (Aug 6, 1945)? - A) Nagasaki - B) Tokyo
- C) Hiroshima - D) Okinawa Answer: C) Hiroshima (“Little Boy” bomb ✓
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 39.4 Cold War Era (1947-1991) before moving on.
2. The Intuition
Understanding 39.4 Cold War Era (1947-1991) is key to performing well on the CET.
Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Cold War (USA vs. USSR): - Ideology: Capitalism (USA) vs. Communism (USSR) -
Nuclear Arms Race: Hydrogen bomb (1952 USA, 1953 USSR) - Space Race: Sputnik
(1957 USSR), Apollo 11 (1969 USA — Moon landing) - Berlin Wall: Built 1961 (East vs.
West Berlin), fell 1989
Major Events: - 1947: Truman Doctrine (contain communism), Marshall Plan (rebuild
Europe) - 1948: Berlin Airlift (USA supplies West Berlin during Soviet blockade) - 1962:
Cuban Missile Crisis (closest to nuclear war) - 1989: Berlin Wall falls (symbol of Cold
War’s end) - 1991: USSR dissolves (official end of Cold War)
Proxy Wars (fought by surrogates): - Korean War (1950-1953): North (USSR/China)
vs. South (USA/UN) - Vietnam War (1955-1975): North (USSR/China) vs. South (USA) -
Afghanistan (1979-1989): Mujahideen (USA) vs. USSR)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 39.4 Cold War Era (1947-1991). Practice
with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Symbol of Cold War’s end (1989)? - A) Cuban Missile
Crisis - B) Berlin Wall falls - C) USSR dissolves - D) Moon landing Answer: B)
Berlin Wall falls (1989 — symbol of Cold War’s end ✓
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 39.5 Contemporary World Issues (1991-Present) before
moving on. 2. The Intuition
Understanding 39.5 Contemporary World Issues (1991-Present) is key to performing well
on the CET. Focus on core concepts and their application.
3. The Build-Up
Post-Cold War Era: - 1991: Gulf War (USA liberates Kuwait from Iraq) - 2001: 9/11
attacks → War on Terror (Afghanistan, Iraq) - 2008: Global Financial Crisis (housing
bubble, Lehman Brothers) - 2020: COVID-19 pandemic (global lockdown)
Issues (2024-2026): - Climate Change: Global warming, rising sea levels,
extreme weather - AI Revolution: Artificial Intelligence (ChatGPT, AGI race) -
Geopolitics: USA-China rivalry, Russia-Ukraine war (2022+), Israel-Gaza conflict
(2023+) - Technology: Social media, misinformation, cybersecurity threats
International Organizations: - UN (United Nations): 193 member states (founded
1945) - NATO: Military alliance (USA + Europe, founded 1949) - EU (European Union):
27 member states (single currency: Euro) - ASEAN: 10 Southeast Asian nations (PH is
founding member, 1967) 4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)
Focus on understanding the core concepts of 39.5 Contemporary World Issues (1991-
Present). Practice with sample questions to reinforce your learning.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Current AI revolution (ChatGPT, etc.)? - A) 1990s - B)
2000s - C) 2010s - D) 2020s Answer: D) 2020s (ChatGPT launched Nov 2022 ✓
6. Practice Problems
Basic:
1. Review core concepts and definitions from this section.
Standard:
2. Apply concepts to sample problems. Exam-Level:
3. Timed practice under exam conditions.

thoroughly

Filipino Context
Issues = Kasalukuyang Isyu
Climate Change = Pagbabago ng Klima AI Revolution = Rebolusyong AI

Chapter Practice Questions
1. 1st writing system (Mesopotamia)? - A) Hieroglyphics - B) Cuneiform - C)
Alphabet - D) Latin
2. Trigger for WWI (1914)? - A) Sinking of Lusitania - B) Assassination of Archduke
Franz Ferdinand - C) Zimmerman Telegram - D) Invasion of Poland
3. “Eleventh hour of the eleventh day” (1918)? - A) War begins - B) USA enters war -
C) Armistice (end of fighting) - D) Treaty signed
4. Philosophy (Socrates, Plato, Aristotle)? - A) Mesopotamia - B) Egypt - C) Greece -
D) Rome
5. USA enters WWII (1941)? - A) Invasion of Poland - B) Pearl Harbor (Dec 7, 1941) -
C) D-Day (June 6, 1944) - D) Battle of Stalingrad
6. 1st man on Moon (1969)? - A) USSR (Yuri Gagarin) - B) USA (Neil Armstrong) - C)
China - D) UK
7. Symbol of Cold War’s end (1989)? - A) Cuban Missile Crisis - B) Berlin Wall falls -
C) USSR dissolves - D) Moon landing
8. 1st atomic bomb (Aug 6, 1945)? - A) Nagasaki - B) Tokyo - C) Hiroshima - D)
Okinawa
9. War on Terror (after 9/11 2001)? - A) Gulf War - B) Afghanistan, Iraq - C) Cold War
- D) Vietnam War
10. Current AI revolution (ChatGPT)? - A) 1990s - B) 2000s - C) 2010s - D) 2020s
Answer Key
1. B) Cuneiform (Mesopotamia, 3200 BCE ✓) 2. B) Assassination of Archduke Franz
Ferdinand (June 28, 1914 ✓) 3. C) Armistice (Nov 11, 11AM, 1918 ✓) 4. C) Greece
(Golden Age of Athens ✓) 5. B) Pearl Harbor (Dec 7, 1941 ✓) 6. B) USA (Neil
Armstrong, Apollo 11, 1969 ✓) 7. B) Berlin Wall falls (1989 ✓) 8. C) Hiroshima (“Little
Boy” bomb ✓) 9. B) Afghanistan, Iraq (War on Terror ✓) 10. D) 2020s (ChatGPT
launched Nov 2022 ✓)** Filipino Context Applications Mga Halimbawa sa Pilipinas:
Common World History Mistakes (Filipino Students):
WWI trigger: Assassination of Franz Ferdinand (NOT sinking of Lusitania!)
USA enters WWII: Pearl Harbor 1941 (NOT D-Day 1944!)
1st writing: Cuneiform (NOT Hieroglyphics!)
Cold War end: Berlin Wall falls 1989 (NOT USSR dissolves 1991!)
History Timeline (Important Dates): 776 BCE: 1st Olympics (Greece) 1914-1918: World War I (MANIA causes)
1939-1945: World War II (Pearl Harbor 1941, Atomic bombs 1945)
1947-1991: Cold War (Space Race, Berlin Wall)
2020: COVID-19 pandemic Ancient vs. Modern:
Ancient: Mesopotamia (Cuneiform), Egypt (pyramids), Greece (democracy),
Rome (law)
Modern: Cold War (USA vs. USSR), AI Revolution (ChatGPT), Climate Change
Tips para sa CET:

Know causes + effects (kilalanin sanhi at bunga) — WWI → WWII → Cold
Timeline matters (mahal ang timeline) — Ancient → WWI → WWII → Cold
War → Present
Don’t confuse (huwag malito) — Franz Ferdinand (WWI) vs. Pearl Harbor
