geninfo.title: General Science Review

blocks.value: Physics is the study of matter, energy, and their interactions. For the CET, you should master Newton's Laws of Motion, types of energy, conservation laws, and wave properties.
A push or pull that can change an object's state of motion. Measured in Newtons (N). Force = mass × acceleration (F = ma).
Newton's 1st Law (Inertia): An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity, unless acted upon by a net external force.
Newton's 2nd Law: F = ma (Force = mass × acceleration). The greater the mass, the more force needed to accelerate it.
Newton's 3rd Law: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. Example: When you push a wall, the wall pushes back with equal force.
Kinematics describes motion without considering its causes. Key quantities: displacement (distance in a direction), velocity (speed in a direction), and acceleration (rate of change of velocity).
Key Kinematics Equations: v = d/t (velocity = distance/time); a = Δv/Δt (acceleration = change in velocity/time); d = v₀t + ½at² (displacement with constant acceleration).

Energy is the ability to do work. The two main types are kinetic energy (energy of motion) and potential energy (stored energy). Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transformed from one form to another (Conservation of Energy).
Kinetic Energy: KE = ½mv² (where m = mass in kg, v = velocity in m/s). Potential Energy: PE = mgh (where g = 9.8 m/s², h = height in meters).

Work is done when a force moves an object over a distance. Work = Force × Distance × cos(θ), where θ is the angle between the force and direction of motion. Power is the rate of doing work: Power = Work/Time.
The SI unit of energy and work. 1 Joule = 1 Newton × 1 meter. A 1 kg object falling 1 meter gains approximately 9.8 J of kinetic energy.
Waves transfer energy without transferring matter. Transverse waves have perpendicular oscillation (light, water waves). Longitudinal waves have parallel oscillation (sound waves).
Wave Equation: v = f × λ (speed = frequency × wavelength). Frequency (f) is measured in Hertz (Hz). Wavelength (λ) is the distance between consecutive crests.
Sound travels at about 343 m/s in air at room temperature. Light travels at approximately 3 × 10⁸ m/s in a vacuum. Sound cannot travel through a vacuum, but light can.
Example: A 2 kg ball is dropped from 10 m. What is its velocity just before hitting the ground? Using PE = KE: mgh = ½mv² → v = √(2gh) = √(2 × 9.8 × 10) ≈ 14 m/s.
CET Tip: Memorize F = ma, KE = ½mv², PE = mgh, and v = fλ. These four formulas appear repeatedly. Always check units — mass in kg, velocity in m/s, force in Newtons.

Practice: A 5 kg object is pushed with 20 N of force. What is its acceleration? If a wave has frequency 500 Hz and wavelength 0.6 m, what is its speed? Calculate the KE of a 3 kg ball moving at 4 m/s.
Chemistry is the study of matter — its composition, properties, and transformations. The CET covers matter classification, the periodic table, chemical bonding, and types of reactions.
A pure substance that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical means. Examples: Hydrogen (H), Oxygen (O), Gold (Au), Iron (Fe). There are 118 known elements.

Matter Classification: Elements are pure substances (one type of atom). Compounds are two or more elements chemically bonded (e.g., H₂O, CO₂, NaCl). Mixtures are physical combinations that can be separated (e.g., saltwater, air, sand and iron filings).

Periodic Table Organization: Groups (columns 1-18) — elements with the same number of valence electrons. Periods (rows 1-7) — elements with the same number of electron shells. Metals (left), Nonmetals (right), Metalloids (staircase).

Group 1: Alkali Metals (Li, Na, K) — very reactive, react vigorously with water. Group 17: Halogens (F, Cl, Br, I) — very reactive nonmetals. Group 18: Noble Gases (He, Ne, Ar) — stable, unreactive due to full outer shell.

Chemical Bonds: Ionic bonds form when electrons are transferred (e.g., NaCl — sodium gives 1 electron to chlorine). Covalent bonds form when electrons are shared (e.g., H₂O — oxygen shares electrons with two hydrogen atoms).
An atom or molecule with a net electric charge due to loss or gain of electrons. Cations are positive (lost electrons, e.g., Na⁺). Anions are negative (gained electrons, e.g., Cl⁻).

Chemical Reactions: Reactants are the starting substances; Products are the substances formed. The Law of Conservation of Mass states that total mass of reactants equals total mass of products — atoms are rearranged, not created or destroyed.
Types of Reactions: Synthesis (A + B → AB); Decomposition (AB → A + B); Single Replacement (A + BC → AC + B); Double Replacement (AB + CD → AD + CB); Combustion (fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O).

Acids and Bases: Acids have pH < 7, taste sour, and release H⁺ ions (e.g., HCl, vinegar). Bases have pH > 7, taste bitter, feel slippery, and release OH⁻ ions (e.g., NaOH, soap). Neutral solutions have pH = 7 (pure water).
A unit of measurement in chemistry. 1 mole = 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number). 1 mole of any element has a mass equal to its atomic mass in grams (e.g., 1 mole of Carbon = 12 g).
Example: Balance the equation: Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃. Balanced: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃. Check: 4 Fe atoms and 6 O atoms on each side.
CET Tip: Know common element symbols (Na = sodium, K = potassium, Fe = iron, Au = gold, Ag = silver, Hg = mercury). Questions often test balancing equations and identifying reaction types.
Practice: Classify each as element, compound, or mixture: (a) air, (b) water, (c) gold, (d) saltwater. What type of bond holds NaCl together? Balance: CH₄ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O.
Biology is the study of living organisms. The CET covers cell theory, genetics (Mendel's laws), ecology, and human body systems. Philippine biodiversity is a frequent contextual topic.
Three principles: (1) All living things are composed of cells. (2) The cell is the basic unit of life. (3) All cells come from pre-existing cells. Proposed by Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow.
Cell Types: Prokaryotic cells lack a membrane-bound nucleus (bacteria, archaea). Eukaryotic cells have a true nucleus and membrane-bound organelles (plants, animals, fungi, protists).

Key Organelles: Nucleus — contains DNA, control center; Mitochondria — powerhouse of the cell (cellular respiration); Chloroplasts — photosynthesis in plant cells; Ribosomes — protein synthesis; Cell Membrane — controls what enters/exits.
Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen using sunlight. This occurs in chloroplasts.
Cellular Respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP (energy). This is the reverse of photosynthesis. It occurs in mitochondria and provides energy for cellular activities.
Deoxyribonucleic acid — the molecule that carries genetic information. It has a double helix structure made of nucleotides (sugar + phosphate + base). Base pairs: A-T and C-G.

Mendel's Laws: Law of Dominance — dominant allele (A) masks recessive (a). Law of Segregation — each parent passes one allele. Law of Independent Assortment — genes for different traits are inherited independently.
Punnett Square: A tool to predict offspring genotypes. Cross Aa × Aa: 25% AA, 50% Aa, 25% aa. The phenotype ratio is 3:1 (3 dominant : 1 recessive) if A is completely dominant.

Ecology: An ecosystem includes all living organisms (biotic factors) and non-living components (abiotic factors) in an area. Food chains show energy flow: Producer → Primary Consumer → Secondary Consumer → Tertiary Consumer → Decomposer.

The variety of life in an area. The Philippines is a 'mega-diverse' country with ~5% of the world's species on 0.2% of the world's land. Threats: habitat loss, pollution, climate change, overexploitation.

Philippine Biodiversity Hotspots: Coral Triangle (marine biodiversity center), Palawan rainforests, Sierra Madre forests. Endemic species: Philippine Eagle, Philippine Tarsier, Philippine Crocodile, Tamaraw.
Example: In a cross between Tt (tall) and tt (short), what percentage of offspring will be tall? Tt × tt → 50% Tt (tall), 50% tt (short). Answer: 50% tall.
CET Tip: Know the Punnett Square for monohybrid and diohybrid crosses. Memorize the photosynthesis and respiration equations. Understand food chains and energy pyramids.
Practice: Draw a Punnett square for Aa × Aa. List 3 organelles unique to plant cells. Explain the difference between a food chain and a food web.
Earth science covers geology (rocks, plate tectonics), meteorology (weather, climate), and astronomy. The Philippines' location on the Ring of Fire makes geology especially relevant.
The theory that Earth's outer shell (lithosphere) is divided into several plates that move, collide, and separate. This causes earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain formation.
The Philippine Plate is a small oceanic plate that interacts with the Eurasian Plate and Pacific Plate. The Philippine Mobile Belt — where several plates meet — makes the country highly seismic.

Types of Rocks: Igneous — formed from cooled magma/lava (granite, basalt); Sedimentary — formed from compressed layers (limestone, sandstone); Metamorphic — formed by heat and pressure on existing rocks (marble, slate).

The Rock Cycle: All three rock types can transform into each other. Igneous rocks weather into sediments that form sedimentary rocks. Both can become metamorphic through heat and pressure. Metamorphic rocks can melt into magma and cool into igneous rocks.

Weather vs. Climate: Weather is the day-to-day atmospheric condition. Climate is the long-term average weather pattern of an area (30+ years). The Philippines has a Type I climate (two distinct seasons: dry and wet).

PAGASA Climate Types: Type I — two seasons (dry Nov-Apr, wet May-Oct), pronounced rainfall; Type II — no dry season, heavy rainfall Nov-Jan; Type III — no pronounced seasons, rain throughout the year; Type IV — rainfall evenly distributed.
The water cycle (hydrological cycle) describes how water evaporates from surfaces, rises and condenses into clouds, precipitates as rain, and flows back to oceans via rivers and groundwater.

A fracture in Earth's crust where movement occurs. The Philippines has several major fault lines, including the West Valley Fault (Metro Manila) and the Philippine Fault Zone (runs the length of the archipelago).

The Enhanced Fujita Scale measures tornado intensity. The Saffir-Simpson Scale measures hurricane/typhoon intensity (Categories 1-5). PAGASA uses its own typhoon classification: Tropical Depression, Tropical Storm, Severe Tropical Storm, Typhoon, Super Typhoon.

Soil types in the Philippines: Volcanic soil (Andisols) — very fertile, found in areas near volcanoes; Clay soil — common in lowlands, retains water; Sandy soil — common in coastal areas, drains quickly. Soil erosion is a major environmental concern.

Example: Q: What type of rock is formed from cooled lava? A) Igneous rock — specifically extrusive igneous rock (e.g., basalt) when lava cools on the surface, or intrusive igneous (e.g., granite) when magma cools underground.
CET Tip: Know the three rock types and how they form. Understand plate tectonics and why the Philippines has many earthquakes. Be familiar with PAGASA's climate classification system.
Practice: Classify each rock type: granite, limestone, marble. Explain the difference between weather and climate. What is the Philippine Plate and why does it cause earthquakes?

sections.title: Scientific Method and Science in the Philippines

blocks.value: The scientific method is a systematic approach to investigating phenomena. It is the foundation of all scientific inquiry and a frequent CET topic.

Steps of the Scientific Method: (1) Observation — notice something interesting; (2) Question — formulate a question; (3) Hypothesis — propose a testable explanation; (4) Experiment — test the hypothesis; (5) Analysis — collect and analyze data; (6) Conclusion — accept or reject the hypothesis; (7) Communication — share results.

A tentative, testable explanation for an observation. It must be falsifiable — meaning it can be proven wrong through experimentation. Example: 'If plants are given more sunlight, then they will grow taller.'

Variables in Experiments: Independent Variable — what the researcher changes (manipulated). Dependent Variable — what is measured (responding). Controlled Variables — kept constant to ensure a fair test.

A theory in science is different from everyday use. In science, a theory is a well-tested explanation supported by extensive evidence (e.g., Theory of Evolution, Cell Theory, Theory of Relativity). A hypothesis becomes a theory after repeated testing.
Peer review is the process by which scientific work is evaluated by other experts before publication. This ensures quality, accuracy, and credibility of research.

Science and Technology in the Philippines: DOST (Department of Science and Technology) is the main government agency for science policy. Key institutions include PAGASA (weather), PHIVOLCS (volcanoes/seismology), FNRI (food and nutrition), and PNRD (nuclear research).

Filipino Scientists: Dr. Fe del Mundo — pioneer in Philippine pediatrics; Dr. Alfredo Santos — studied medicinal plants; Dr. Julian Banzon — researched alternative fuels from coconut; Dr. Lourdes Cruz — studied cone snail venom (conotoxin); Dr. Edgardo Gomez — championed Philippine marine science.
The group in an experiment that does not receive the treatment. It serves as a baseline for comparison. Example: In testing a new fertilizer, the control group of plants receives no fertilizer.

Scientific Laws vs. Theories: A law describes what happens (e.g., Law of Gravity — objects fall). A theory explains why it happens (e.g., Theory of Gravity — mass attracts mass via gravitational force). Laws and theories are both well-tested, but they serve different purposes.

Example: In an experiment testing whether fertilizer helps plants grow, what is the independent variable? Answer: The fertilizer (what the researcher changes). The dependent variable is plant growth (what is measured).
Ethics in Science: Research must follow ethical guidelines, especially involving human subjects or animals. Informed consent, safety, and honesty in reporting results are fundamental principles.
CET Tip: The scientific method steps are a guaranteed CET question. Know the difference between independent and dependent variables. Be aware of famous Filipino scientists and DOST agencies.
Qualitative data describes qualities (colors, textures, observations — 'the solution turned blue'). Quantitative data is numerical (measurements — 'the temperature was 25°C').

Practice: Design an experiment to test if music affects plant growth. Identify the variables. What is the role of the control group? Name 3 Filipino scientists and their contributions. What is the difference between a theory and a law?

geninfo.description: Covers basic physics, chemistry, biology, earth science, and the scientific method.

geninfo.title: Current Events & Contemporary Issues

blocks.value: Current events is a staple of the CET General Information section. Questions cover recent political developments, government programs, economic news, and social issues in the Philippines and around the world.
Significant happenings and developments in politics, economics, society, and international affairs that are recent and relevant. For CET purposes, focus on events from 2024 to 2026.

Political Developments (2024-2026): The Marcos Jr. administration (2022-2028) continues its 'Bagong Pilipinas' (New Philippines) governance framework. Key themes include infrastructure development, digitalization, and foreign investment.

The 2025 midterm elections are a major political event. Filipinos vote for senators, district representatives, party-list groups, governors, mayors, and local officials. The midterm results shape the political landscape for the remainder of Marcos's term.

The West Philippine Sea remains a flashpoint. China's coast guard and maritime militia continue to confront Philippine vessels, particularly around Ayungin (Second Thomas) Shoal and Scarborough Shoal. The Philippines has strengthened defense ties with the US, Japan, and Australia in response.

Key Economic Indicators (2024-2026): GDP Growth — 5-6% target; Inflation — rice prices, fuel costs; Peso Exchange Rate — ₱55-58 per USD; OFW Remittances — $30+ billion/year; Unemployment Rate — declining post-COVID.

Jeepney Modernization: The government's Public Utility Vehicle Modernization Program (PUVMP) aims to replace old jeepneys with modern, Euro-4 compliant vehicles. This has been controversial due to costs imposed on small operators and drivers' groups have staged protests.

Education: The MATATAG curriculum is being phased in to replace the K-12 program's original design. It reduces the number of subjects, focuses on foundational skills (literacy, numeracy), and is designed to be more age-appropriate. Full face-to-face classes have resumed post-COVID.

Climate and Disasters: The Philippines continues to experience frequent typhoons, flooding, and volcanic activity. PAGASA and PHIVOLCS issue regular advisories. The government has increased its disaster preparedness budget and early warning systems.

The revised K-12 curriculum launched by the Department of Education under the Marcos administration. It stands for 'Make the curriculum relevant, Responsive, and age-appropriate, and to produce competent, job-ready, and future-ready graduates.'
Example: Q: What is the 'Bagong Pilipinas' slogan associated with? A) The Marcos Jr. administration — it represents the governance philosophy of the current government.
CET Tip: Read newspapers (Philippine Daily Inquirer, Manila Bulletin) and follow PIA (Philippine Information Agency) for reliable news. Focus on 'who, what, when, where, why' for each major event.
Practice: What is the significance of the 2025 midterm elections? Explain the jeepney modernization controversy. What is the MATATAG curriculum?

The Philippine government implements various programs addressing education, health, infrastructure, and social welfare. Understanding these programs is essential for CET questions on governance and public policy.

The flagship infrastructure program of the Marcos Jr. administration, continuing 'Build, Build, Build.' It focuses on roads, bridges, flood control, railways, and airports with a budget of ₱9+ trillion (2023-2028).

Major Programs: MATATAG (Education), Build Better More (Infrastructure), Universal Health Care (Health), Digitalization (E-governance), Rice Tariffication (Agriculture), AKAP (Cash Transfer), Philippine ID System (PhilSys).

Universal Health Care (UHC): Signed in 2019, UHC aims to provide automatic health insurance coverage to all Filipinos through PhilHealth. It covers preventive care, diagnostics, and medicines. Implementation is ongoing.

Philippine ID System (PhilSys): The national ID system aims to streamline government transactions and reduce fraud. By 2025, over 80 million Filipinos have registered. The PhilID serves as a single valid government ID.

Social Protection: The Pantawid Pamilyang Pilipino Program (4Ps) is the national conditional cash transfer program. It provides cash grants to the poorest families, conditional on children attending school and regular health checkups.

The Philippines' flagship conditional cash transfer program. It provides monthly cash grants to the poorest households, conditional on children's school attendance and regular health visits. Managed by DSWD.
Digitalization: The government is pushing for e-governance — online services for permits, payments, and government transactions. The eGov PH app and eTravel system are recent digital initiatives.

Agriculture: The Rice Tariffication Law (2019) replaced quantitative import restrictions with tariffs. It aims to lower rice prices for consumers while modernizing rice farming. Rice farmers receive cash assistance (Rice Competitiveness Enhancement Fund — RCEF).
Anti-Drug Campaign: The Marcos administration shifted from the controversial 'war on drugs' to a community-based drug rehabilitation approach. However, human rights concerns persist.
Example: Q: What does UHC stand for and what does it provide? A) Universal Health Care — automatic PhilHealth coverage for all Filipinos, including preventive care and medicines.
CET Tip: Know the full names and purposes of major government programs. Questions may ask about 4Ps, PhilSys, MATATAG, or UHC. Understand the role of key agencies: DepEd, DOH, DPWH, DSWD, DOST.
Practice: What is the purpose of the PhilSys ID? How does the 4Ps program work? What is the Rice Tariffication Law?
The Philippines plays an active role in international affairs through ASEAN, the United Nations, and bilateral relationships. Understanding global issues is important for the CET.
A government's strategy for dealing with other nations. The Philippines under Marcos Jr. follows 'Friends to all, enemies to none' — pursuing balanced diplomacy with multiple partners.

US-Philippines Relations: The Mutual Defense Treaty (1951) remains the cornerstone of the alliance. The Enhanced Defense Cooperation Agreement (EDCA) allows US military access to Philippine bases. In 2023, four new EDCA sites were designated, including locations near Taiwan and the Spratlys.

China-Philippines Relations: The West Philippine Sea dispute is the central issue. The 2016 Arbitral Tribunal ruling (under UNCLOS) invalidated China's nine-dash line claim. Despite this, China continues to assert control. Bilateral trade exceeds $40 billion annually.

Japan-Philippines Relations: Japan is a major development aid donor and defense partner. The 2023 Official Security Assistance (OSA) agreement marked Japan's first military aid to the Philippines, worth over $100 million.
ASEAN Members (10): Philippines, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam, Brunei, Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar. Founded: August 8, 1967 (Bangkok Declaration). Headquarters: Jakarta, Indonesia.

Global Issues: Climate Change — The Philippines is one of the most climate-vulnerable countries. At COP28 (2023), the Philippines committed to renewable energy targets. The country aims for 35% renewable energy by 2030.

Artificial Intelligence (AI): Global discussions on AI regulation intensified in 2023-2025. The Philippines is developing its own AI roadmap through DOST. AI ethics, job displacement, and data privacy are key concerns.

17 goals adopted by the United Nations in 2015 to be achieved by 2030. They address poverty, hunger, health, education, gender equality, clean water, energy, economic growth, inequality, sustainable cities, climate action, and more.

SDGs and the Philippines: The country has made progress in SDG 1 (No Poverty) and SDG 4 (Quality Education) but faces challenges in SDG 13 (Climate Action) and SDG 14 (Life Below Water) due to its archipelagic geography and vulnerability to disasters.

Russia-Ukraine War (2022-present): The Philippines has voted with the UN General Assembly condemning Russia's invasion of Ukraine. The conflict has affected global food and energy prices, impacting the Philippines as a food-importing country.

Israel-Gaza Conflict (2023-present): The Philippines has called for ceasefire and humanitarian access. Many OFWs work in the Middle East, making regional stability a direct concern for Filipino families.
Example: Q: What are the Sustainable Development Goals? A) 17 UN goals adopted in 2015 targeting poverty, health, education, climate, and more — to be achieved by 2030.
CET Tip: Know the ASEAN founding date and members. Understand the SDGs and the Philippines' position on key global issues. Be aware of EDCA, the 2016 Arbitral Ruling, and the Mutual Defense Treaty.
Practice: What is the EDCA agreement? Name 5 SDGs. How has the Russia-Ukraine war affected the Philippines? What is the Philippines' position on the West Philippine Sea?

sections.title: Social Issues, Digital Literacy, and Media

blocks.value: Social issues in the Philippines include poverty, inequality, urbanization, and the digital divide. Understanding these issues is important for the CET as they often appear in the General Information section.
The proportion of the population living below the poverty threshold. The Philippines' poverty incidence was about 18.1% in 2021 (PSA data). The government targets single-digit poverty by 2028.

Urbanization: Metro Manila alone has about 14 million people. Rapid urbanization causes traffic congestion, housing shortages, and strain on public services. The government promotes regional development to decongest Metro Manila.

The Digital Divide: Internet access remains unequal. Urban areas have faster and more reliable connections, while rural areas struggle with connectivity. The Free Wi-Fi for All program aims to bridge this gap.
The ability to find, evaluate, create, and communicate information using digital technologies. It includes online safety, media literacy, and responsible social media use.

Fake News and Disinformation: The Philippines has one of the highest social media usage rates globally. Facebook, TikTok, and YouTube are primary news sources for many Filipinos. This makes the population vulnerable to misinformation and disinformation campaigns.

Media Landscape: Major media outlets include ABS-CBN (lost its broadcast franchise in 2020, now operates online), GMA Network, Philippine Daily Inquirer, Manila Bulletin, and Rappler (faced legal challenges). The Philippine Constitution guarantees press freedom (Article III, Section 4).
The practice of labeling individuals or organizations as communists or terrorists without due process. It has been criticized by human rights groups as a threat to free speech and civil liberties.
Mental Health: The Mental Health Act (RA 11036, 2018) mandates mental health services in communities and schools. Mental health awareness has increased, especially among the youth, post-COVID.

Gender Equality: The Philippines ranks among the top countries in the Global Gender Gap Index. The Magna Carta of Women (RA 9710) protects women's rights. The SOGIE Equality Bill, which would prohibit discrimination based on sexual orientation, remains pending in Congress.

Indigenous Peoples: The Indigenous Peoples' Rights Act (IPRA, RA 8371) recognizes the rights of indigenous cultural communities to their ancestral domains. There are about 14-17 million IPs in the Philippines belonging to 110 ethno-linguistic groups.
Example: Q: What is digital literacy? A) The ability to find, evaluate, create, and communicate information using digital technologies — including online safety and responsible social media use.

Federalism Debate: There have been ongoing discussions about shifting from a unitary to a federal system of government. Proponents argue it will distribute power and resources more equitably. Critics worry about increased costs and political dynasties.

CET Tip: Questions on social issues often link to laws (RA numbers) and government agencies. Know the Constitution's provisions on press freedom and human rights. Be aware of the digital literacy challenge and its impact on elections.

Practice: What is the significance of the Mental Health Act? Explain the digital divide in the Philippines. How does social media influence Philippine elections? What is red-tagging and why is it controversial?

sections.title: CET Current Events Strategies and Practice

blocks.value: How to Prepare for Current Events Questions

Current events questions appear in every CET — they test whether you're aware of your world. The good news: you don't need to memorize everything. Focus on major events from the past 1-2 years that affected the Philippines or the global community.
A current event is any significant political, economic, social, scientific, or cultural development that has occurred recently and has broad impact on society.

Strategy 1: Follow reliable Philippine news sources regularly — Philippine Star, Inquirer, Rappler, ABS-CBN News, GMA News. Read at least the headlines daily. Strategy 2: Focus on 'big picture' impact rather than memorizing dates and names. CET questions often ask WHY something matters, not just WHAT happened.

Example: Instead of memorizing the exact date of a typhoon, understand its impact — displaced communities, government response (NDRRMC), infrastructure damage, climate change connection. CET questions ask about causes and consequences.

Key Topics to Review: Philippine economy (GDP growth, inflation, unemployment rate), major legislation (Sovereign Wealth Fund, divorce bill status, reproductive health), environmental issues (deforestation, mining, plastic pollution), technological developments (digitalization, AI adoption, cybersecurity), international affairs (ASEAN, South China Sea/West Philippine Sea disputes, climate agreements).

Strategy 3: Use the '5W1H' framework — Who, What, When, Where, Why, How. For every major event, be able to answer all six. Strategy 4: Connect current events to your other subjects. Climate change = Science. Economic policy = Math/Government. Cultural events = Filipino/English.

Practice: What government program aims to provide free tuition in state universities? (Answer: RA 10931 — Universal Access to Quality Tertiary Education Act, signed 2017, implemented by CHED and TESDA.)

CET Tip: Questions about current events are often 'open-book' in nature — they test critical thinking about events, not memorization. If you can explain WHY an event matters and HOW it connects to broader themes, you'll do well even without memorizing every detail.

geninfo.description: Covers recent Philippine news, government policies, international relations, and digital literacy.

math.title: The Language of Numbers

blocks.value: What You Need First
Nothing. This is Section 1.1 — the bedrock. Every concept here will be defined from
scratch. The Intuition
Imagine you’re three years old. You have one toy car. Your friend has two candy
bars.
You don’t know the symbols “1” or “2” yet — but you know that your friend has
more. That feeling — “this is more than that” — is where all of mathematics
begins. Numbers are humanity’s way of giving names to quantity — how much,
how many, how far, how fast.
A Hierarchy of Sets

Real Numbers (ℝ) Every point on the number line Rational Numbers (ℚ) p/q where p,q are integers, q ≠0 ✓Terminating decimals (1/4 = 0.25) ✓Repeating decimals (1/3 = 0.333...) Integers (ℤ) ..., -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ... Positive + Negative + Zero Natural Numbers (ℕ) 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ... Irrationals √3, √5, ... Can't write as
Subset Chain: Natural (ℕ) ⊆Integer (ℤ) ⊆Rational (ℚ) ⊆Real (ℝ)
But not all numbers are the same. Just like you have different words for “one cat”
vs. “many cats,” mathematicians created different sets of numbers for different
jobs. Here’s the big reveal: There is no “largest” number. You can always add
one more.
is no “smallest” either — you can always go negative. This simple fact
— that numbers stretch infinitely in both directions — is why we need to organize
them into sets. The Build-Up (Formal & Rigorous)
Mathematics classifies numbers into sets — think of them as labeled boxes, each holding
numbers with shared properties:
Natural Numbers — for counting whole objects
Property Details
What they are
Counting numbers: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, … Never include Zero, negatives, fractions, or decimals Infinite in The positive direction only Why they exist
You count apples, people, money. You can’t have -3 apples or √2
apples.
Integers — for debts, temperatures, and positions
Property Details
What they are
All natural numbers + zero + negative numbers
…, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, … Infinite in Both directions Why they exist
Debt (-₱500), below-zero temperature (-5°C), elevation below sea
level
Rational Numbers — for sharing and dividing
Any number you can write as a fraction p/q (where q ≠ 0)
Key rule
When written as a decimal, it either terminates (0.25) or repeats
Sharing a pizza among friends, measuring half a cup of flour
ARE rational! Every integer can be written as itself over 1
Irrational Numbers — the numbers that don’t fit
Real numbers that CANNOT be written as a fraction p/q
Famous ones
Key rule
Decimal never terminates AND never repeats — it goes on forever
without a pattern Why they exist
The diagonal of a square, the circumference of a circle, natural
growth
√2, √3, √5 (any square root of a non-perfect-square), π, e
Real Numbers — the complete number system
All rational numbers + all irrational numbers = EVERYTHING on
the number line Coverage
Every point on the infinite line matches exactly one real number
Why they exist
For continuous quantities: distance, time, mass, speed —
everything measurable Big idea
If you can measure it, it’s a real number
📊 Visual Guide: See the diagram “The Number System: A Hierarchy of
Sets” at the end of this chapter for a visual breakdown of how these sets
nest inside each other. Layman Terms (The Lego Analogy)
At first, you only have whole blocks — 1-stud, 2-stud, 3-stud pieces. That’s your
first toolbox: natural numbers.
You can count your blocks: 1, 2, 3… but you can’t have “negative 3
blocks” or “half a block.” Then you realize: sometimes you owe
blocks. Your friend lent you 3 blocks, so you have -3 blocks in your
“block account.” Now you’ve invented integers — positives, zero, and
negatives. But then you want to share 1 block among 4 friends.
Everyone gets 1/4 of a block. That’s a fraction — and fractions make up
the rational numbers.
Here’s the neat part: every rational number, when you write it out as a
decimal, either stops (1/4 = 0.25) or repeats forever (1/3 = 0.333…).
That’s the “rational” rule. Now here’s the mind-blowing part: not
every number is a fraction. Picture a perfect square with sides of
exactly 1 unit.
Draw the diagonal. How long is it? Pythagoras tells us: √(1²+1²) = √2.
But try to write √2 as a fraction — you can’t. No matter what p and q
you pick, p/q will never exactly equal √2. The decimal goes
1.41421356… forever, never repeating. That’s an irrational number.
And π? That’s the ratio of any circle’s circumference to its diameter.
Also irrational.
Also goes on forever: 3.14159265… Here’s the big picture: Real
numbers = all the fractions PLUS all the “can’t-write-as-fractions”
numbers. And every point on an infinite line matches exactly one real
number. Why should you care on the CET? Because when they ask
“Which is irrational?” and give you options like: - A) 2/3 - B) √4 - C) √5
- D) 0.75 You’ll know: A is rational (fraction), B is 2 (rational), C is
irrational (√5 can’t be a fraction), D is 3/4 (rational). Answer: C.
And that’s really all five sets are — different toolboxes for different
jobs. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete — Classifying Numbers):
Classify each number into the smallest set that contains it: (a) -3, (b) 4/7, (c) √9, (d) π, (e)
Solution: - (a) -3 is a negative integer → integers (also rational and real, but integer is
the smallest) - (b) 4/7 is a fraction that doesn’t terminate → rational (also real) - (c) √9
= 3, which is a counting number → natural (also integer, rational, real) - (d) π =
3.14159… irrational → real only (not rational!) - (e) 0 is zero → integer (also rational
and real, but not natural)
Key check: Natural numbers are inside integers, which are inside rationals, which are
inside reals. Irrationals are real but NOT rational.
Example 2 (Standard — Identifying Rationals):
Which of these is rational? (a) √7, (b) 0.333…, (c) √16, (d) e
Solution: - (a) √7 ≈ 2.6457… — not a perfect square → irrational ✗ - (b) 0.333… = 1/3 →
rational ✓ (repeating decimal) - (c) √16 = 4 = 4/1 → rational ✓ (integer) - (d) e ≈
2.71828… — never repeats → irrational ✗
Answer: (b) and (c) are rational.
Example 3 (Exam-Level — Subset Logic):
If a number is rational and positive, which MUST be true?
I. It is also a natural number II. It is also an integer III. It is also a real number
Solution: - I: 1/2 is rational and positive, but NOT a natural number → False - II: 1/2 is
rational and positive, but NOT an integer → False - III: EVERY rational is a real number
→ True
Answer: Only III must be true.
Trap: Positive rationals include fractions like 3/4, 1/2, 7/8 — NOT just positive integers!
Example 4 (With Indian Method — Extreme Case Testing):
Which number is NOT rational?
(a) 0.25, (b) 0.121121112… (pattern adds one more “1” each time), (c) -8, (d) 22/7
Solution: - (a) 0.25 = 1/4 → rational ✓ - (b) 0.121121112… — pattern is not constant
repetition (it’s 1, then 2, then 11, then 111…) → irrational ✗ - (c) -8 = -8/1 → rational ✓
- (d) 22/7 ≈ 3.1428… (a rational approximation of π) → rational ✓
Indian Shortcut (Extreme Case): Test “Does the decimal stop or repeat?”
- 0.25 → stops → rational - 0.121121112… → pattern changes each time → NEVER
settles into constant repeat → irrational ✓ - -8 → integer → rational - 22/7 → fraction
→ rational
Answer: (b) is irrational.
Example 5 (With Chinese Method — Special Value 赋值法):
Which statement is ALWAYS true?
(a) If a number is rational, then it is natural
(b) If a number is an integer, then it is rational
(c) If a number is rational, then it is an integer
(d) If a number is real, then it is rational
Chinese Method (赋值法 — test with specific values): - (a) Let’s pick 1/2 — it’s rational but
NOT natural → False - (b) Let’s pick -3 — it’s an integer, and -3 = -3/1 so it IS rational
→ True for this case - (c) Let’s pick 1/2 — it’s rational but NOT an integer → False -
(d) Let’s pick √2 — it’s real but NOT rational → False
Answer: (b) is always true (every integer is a rational number, since any integer can be
written as itself over 1). One-Page Lock-In
KEY DEFINITIONS: | Set | Symbol | Examples | Key Property |
integers) | | Integers | ℤ | …, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, … | Includes negatives & zero | | Rational | ℚ |
1/2, -3, 0.333… | Can write as p/q (q ≠ 0) | | Irrational | — | √2, π, e | NOT p/q; decimal
never repeats | | Real | ℝ | ALL of above combined | Every point on number line |
THE SUBSET CHAIN (Memorize This!): \ℕ \⊂ \ℤ \⊂ \ℚ \⊂ \ℝ (Irrationals sit in real
numbers but OUTSIDE rational numbers)
THE ONE THING TO NEVER FORGET: √4 = 2 (rational!), but √2, √3, √5, √6, √7, √8
are ALL irrational. Only perfect squares have rational square roots! 📊 KEY
DIAGRAMS FOR THIS SECTION:
Refer to the following diagrams at end of chapter (Section 1.4 Key Diagrams): 1. The
A Hierarchy of Sets — shows how number sets nest inside each
other (Natural → Integer → Rational → Real) 2. The Number Line: All Real
Numbers — visualizes integers, irrationals (√2, π) on the real line 3. Subset Chain
Memory Aid — quick visual reference for the Natural → Integer → Rational → Real
chain
Number Line: All Real Numbers The Number Line with All Number Sets Subset Chain Memory Aid (ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ)
🔒 Reminder 1: √4 = 2 (rational), but √2 is irrational. The square root of a
is ALWAYS irrational. Don’t let (b) √9 trick you — √9
= 3, which is rational!
🔒 Reminder 2: Zero (0) is in integers and rational numbers, but NOT in
natural numbers (in our convention). If they ask “which is NOT a natural
number?” — zero qualifies!
🔒 Reminder 3: Every rational number either TERMINATES (0.25) or
REPEATS (0.333…). If the decimal does neither, it’s irrational. Test:
0.121121112… — pattern changes → irrational.
🔒 Reminder 4: On the CET, they LOVE testing: “Which is irrational?”
with options like √16, √25, √36 (all rational — they’re 4, 5, 6!) vs. √2, √3, √5
(irrational). Watch the square root!
🔒 Reminder 5: The subset chain is your best friend. If a number is a
natural number, it’s AUTOMATICALLY an integer, rational, and real
number. But the reverse is FALSE — a real number isn’t necessarily
rational (π example), and a rational number isn’t necessarily an integer
(1/2 example). 273| Continue to Section 1.2: Operations & Their Properties
The Number Line: All Real Numbers ℤ, ℚ, ℝ ℤ, ℚ, ℝ ℤ, ℚ, ℝ ℤ, ℚ, ℝ ℕ, ℤ, ℚ, ℝ Irrational, ℝ ℕ, ℤ, ℚ, ℝ ℕ, ℤ, ℚ, ℝ ℕ, ℤ, ℚ, ℝ Irrational, ℝ Negative side Positive side Natural (ℕ) — counting numbers
Integer (ℤ) — negatives, zero, positives
Irrational — cannot write as fraction (√2, π, e)
are Real numbers (ℝ)
Subset Chain: How Number Sets Fit Together
Natural Integers Rational Irrationals √2, π, e (can't write as p/q)
are inside ℝbut NOT inside ℚ
Memory: Each set adds more numbers. ℕis the smallest, ℝis the biggest.
What You Need First
Section 1.1 (all number sets: Natural, Integer, Rational, Real)
Basic arithmetic (add, subtract, multiply, divide)
The Intuition
is a game. For the game to be fair, everyone has to follow the same
rules. If 2+3 could equal 6 one day and 5 the next, math would be useless.
The properties of operations are these rules. They tell us: - Can we swap
numbers when adding? (Yes, 2+3 = 3+2) - Can we group numbers differently
when adding?
(Yes, (2+3)+4 = 2+(3+4)) - Can we “distribute” multiplication over addition? (Yes,
2(3+4) = 23 + 2*4) SAT Slayer drills these properties because they’re the
foundation of every algebra step you’ll ever take. Memorize them now, save time
later. The Build-Up (Formal & Rigorous) For all real numbers (a, b, c ∈ ℝ): 1.2.1 Closure Property
Addition: (a + b ∈ ℝ) (sum of two reals is real)
Multiplication: (a * b ∈ ℝ) (product of two reals is real)
Note: Closure fails for subtraction of natural numbers (3-5 = -2, which is not a
natural number) and division by zero (undefined).
1.2.2 Commutative Property
Addition: (a + b = b + a)
Multiplication: (a * b = b * a)
Fails for: Subtraction ((a - b ≠ b - a)) and Division ((a/b ≠ b/a) if (a ≠ b)).
1.2.3 Associative Property
Addition: ((a + b) + c = a + (b + c))
Multiplication: ((a * b) * c = a * (b * c))
Fails for: Subtraction (((a - b) - c ≠ a - (b - c))) and Division (((a/b)/c ≠ a/(b/c))).
1.2.4 Distributive Property
Multiplication over Addition: (a(b + c) = ab + a*c)
Multiplication over Subtraction: (a(b - c) = ab - a*c)
Only property that mixes addition and multiplication.
1.2.5 Identity Elements
Additive Identity: (a + 0 = a) (0 is the additive identity)
Multiplicative Identity: (a * 1 = a) (1 is the multiplicative identity)
1.2.6 Inverse Elements
Additive Inverse: (a + (-a) = 0) (-a is the additive inverse of a)
Multiplicative Inverse: (a * (1/a) = 1) (1/a is the multiplicative inverse of a, (a ≠
Layman Terms
Let’s visualize the commutative property on a number line. Start at 2, move 3
right: you land at 5.
Start at 3, move 2 right: you also land at 5. Swapping the numbers
doesn’t change the result — that’s why addition is commutative. Now
try subtraction: start at 2, move 3 left: -1.
Start at 3, move 2 left: 1. Different results — subtraction isn’t
commutative. See how the number line makes this obvious?
Imagine you’re building a skyscraper. The commutative property is
like using bricks: whether you put brick A on brick B or B on A, the
wall is the same height. The distributive property is like a crane: it lifts
two bricks at once instead of one, saving time.
aren’t boring — they’re the reason math works at all! Ali
Abdaal Style: Listen, CET gives you 180 minutes for 200 questions.
You don’t have time to derive that 3(4+5) = 34 + 35. Memorize the
distributive property today*.
It’s tested 3-4 times per exam, and knowing it cold saves you 2 minutes
per question. That’s 6-8 minutes total — enough to solve 3 more
questions.
Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete — Commutative Property): Verify the commutative property
for addition and multiplication with (a=2, b=3).
Solution: - Addition: (2 + 3 = 5), (3 + 2 = 5) → (2+3=3+2) ✓ - Multiplication: (23=6),
Check: Subtraction: (2-3=-1), (3-2=1) → not equal ✗ (commutative fails for subtraction).
Example 2 (Standard — Distributive Property): Simplify (3*(4 + 5)) using the
distributive property.
Solution: - Distributive property: (34 + 35 = 12 + 15 = 27) - Direct calculation: (3*9 = 27)
→ matches ✓
Answer: 27
Example 3 (Exam-Level — Property Identification): Which property is
demonstrated by ( (2 + 3) + 4 = 2 + (3 + 4) )?
Solution: - This is grouping numbers differently for addition → Associative Property
of Addition
Trap: Don’t confuse with commutative (which swaps numbers, not groups).
Answer: Associative Property of Addition
Example 4 (Indian Method — Distributive Shortcut): Calculate (99 * 45) using the
distributive property (SAT Slayer shortcut).
Standard Calculation: (99*45 = 4455) → matches ✓
Answer: 4455
Example 5 (Chinese Method — 赋值法): Verify the associative property for
multiplication: ( (23)4 = 2(34) ).
Chinese Method (赋值法 — assign values): - Left side: ( (23)4 = 64 = 24 ) - Right side: ( 2(34)
= 212 = 24 ) - Both equal → property holds ✓
Answer: Verified, 24 = 24
One-Page Lock-In
OPERATION PROPERTIES TABLE (Memorize!): | Property | Addition |
Multiplication | Works for Subtraction/Division? |
(subtraction of natural numbers fails) | | Commutative | ✓ (a+b = b+a) | ✓ (ab = ba) | ✗ | |
Associative | ✓ ((a+b)+c = a+(b+c)) | ✓ ((ab)c = a(bc)) | ✗ | | Distributive | N/A | ✓ (a(b+c)
= ab + ac) | N/A | | Identity | 0 (a+0=a) | 1 (a1=a) | ✗ | | Inverse | -a (a+(-a)=0) | 1/a (a*(1/
KEY RULE: Only addition and multiplication are commutative/associative. Never swap
or regroup subtraction/division!
🔒 Reminder 1: Commutative = swap numbers, Associative = regroup
numbers. Mix these up and you’ll lose marks on CET property questions.
🔒 Reminder 2: Distributive property is the only one that mixes addition
and multiplication. It’s the most tested property on the CET — memorize it
first.
🔒 Reminder 3: Subtraction and division are NEVER commutative or
associative. For example: (8/4=2) but (4/8=0.5) — always write operations
in the correct order.
🔒 Reminder 4: Closure for natural numbers: addition/multiplication
close, but subtraction (3-5=-2, not natural) and division (3/2=1.5, not
natural) do NOT close. Watch for trick questions!
Continue to Section 1.3: Arithmetic Tricks & Vedic Math
What You Need First Section 1.1 (all number sets) Section 1.2 (properties of operations)
Basic addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division
The Intuition
are shortcuts — they help you solve problems faster, and on the
CET where you have only about 50 seconds per question, speed matters. These
aren’t cheating; they’re leveraging patterns in numbers that mathematicians have
known for centuries. Vedic math, originating from ancient India, has 16 sutras
(principles) that simplify everything from multiplication to division.
We’ll cover the most CET-relevant ones here.
The Build-Up: 5 Essential Shortcuts
1. Multiplying by 11 (Any Two-Digit Number)
The Trick: Add the two digits and place the sum between them.
Example: 23 × 11
Example: 47 × 11
1. Separate digits 2 _ 3 4 _ 7 2. Add them 2 + 3 = 5 4 + 7 = 11 3. Insert sum 2 5 3 = 253 4 (11) 7 → 517 (carry the 1)
CET speed tip: This takes 2 seconds once you practice it. Instead of writing out 23 × 11
long-form, do the mental insertion. 2. Squaring Numbers Ending in 5
The Trick: Multiply the first digit(s) by (itself + 1), then append 25.
Example: 25²
Example: 85²
1. Take the tens digit
Example: 25²
Example: 85²
2. Multiply by (n + 1) 2 × 3 = 6 8 × 9 = 72 3. Append 25
Why it works: (10n + 5)² = 100 × n × (n + 1) + 25. The algebra checks out.
CET speed tip: 35², 45², 55², 65², 75², 95² all take 1 second with this trick. Know them
cold.
3. Distributive Shortcut (The “99” Trick)
The Trick: When multiplying by a number close to 100, rewrite it as a difference and
use the distributive property.
Problem
Key insight: This uses the distributive property from Section 1.2 — the same rule that
says a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c.
CET speed tip: Look for numbers near 10, 100, 1000 — rewrite them as (power of 10 ±
small number). 4. Divisibility Quick Checks
You don’t always need to divide — use these fast checks:
A number A number is
divisible by…
is divisible by 3
471 → 4+7+1=12 → 12÷3=4 → yes Last two digits form a number divisible by 4 532 → 32÷4=8 → yes
Last digit is 0 or 5
85 → yes Divisible by BOTH 2 and 3 342 → even (✓) + sum=9→divisible by 3 (✓) → yes Last three digits divisible by 8 4032 → 32÷8=4 → yes
is divisible by 9
A number A number is
divisible by…
Last digit is 0
CET speed tip: Divisibility by 3 and 9 are the most tested. Learn the “digit sum” trick —
it’s the same for both, just check if it divides by 3 or 9.
5. Multiplying by 101 (Two-Digit Numbers)
The Trick: Take the two-digit number and write it twice.
Problem
Why it works: 101 × n = 100n + n. For a two-digit number, 100n gives you the first two
digits (hundreds place) and +n adds them again.
Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Calculate 65² using the “squaring ending in 5” trick.
Solution: - Tens digit = 6 - 6 × 7 = 42 - Append 25 → 4225
Check: 65 × 65 = 4225 ✓ (only takes 2 seconds with the trick)
Example 2 (Standard): Without dividing, check if 4,932 is divisible by 3.
Solution: - Sum of digits: 4 + 9 + 3 + 2 = 18 - 18 ÷ 3 = 6 → remainder 0 → yes, divisible
Extra: Since it’s also even (last digit 2), it’s divisible by 6 too!
Example 3 (Exam-Level): Calculate 54 × 11 using the 11 trick, then verify.
Solution: - Digits: 5 and 4 - Sum: 5 + 4 = 9 - Insert: 5 9 4 → 594
Verification: 54 × 10 = 540, then 540 + 54 = 594 ✓
Example 4 (Indian Method — Nikhilam Sutra): Calculate 97 × 98 using the “all from
9 and last from 10” principle.
Solution (cross-subtract method): - Both numbers are close to 100 (base 100) - 97 is 3 less
than 100, 98 is 2 less than 100 - Cross-subtract: 97 − 2 = 95 or 98 − 3 = 95 (same result —
that’s the check!) - Multiply the differences: 3 × 2 = 6 - Answer: first part 95, second part
Example 5 (Chinese Method — 赋值法): Without dividing, determine if 1,284 is
divisible by 4. Test with a similar number.
Solution: - Test the rule: last two digits = 84 - 84 ÷ 4 = 21 → yes, divisible by 4 -
Verification with 赋值法: If we pick a known example, 1,200 ÷ 4 = 300, and 84 ÷ 4 = 21, so
1,284 ÷ 4 = 321 ✓ What You Need First
Section 1.1 (Natural Numbers, Integers, Rational Numbers, Irrational Numbers)
Section 1.2 (Operation properties) The Intuition
The real numbers are the complete number system — every single point on the
infinite number line. If you can measure it (distance, time, temperature, mass), it’s
a real number. The real numbers are the union of rational and irrational
numbers.
Imagine two buckets being poured into one large container: - Bucket 1
(Rational): All fractions, integers, terminating decimals (0.5), repeating decimals
(0.333…) - Bucket 2 (Irrational): √2, π, e — numbers that can never be written as
p/q - Large Container (Real): Everything from both buckets = ℝ Key visual:
The number line is continuous — no gaps. Between any two real numbers, there’s
always another real number (density property).
The Build-Up (Formal & Rigorous) 1.4.1 Definition
The set of real numbers ℝ is defined as: \ℝ = \ℚ \∪ \ℚ^c where ℚ = rational numbers and
ℚ^c = irrational numbers (complement of ℚ).
1.4.2 Properties of Real Numbers For all a, b, c ∈ ℝ: Property Trichotomy
is true: a < b, a = b, or a > b
Transitive If a < b and b < c, then a < c Density
Between any two distinct reals, there exists another real
Completeness
Every non-empty set bounded above has a least upper bound
1.4.3 The Number Line
Every real number corresponds to exactly one point on the number line
Numbers increase left → right
are to the left of 0; positives to the right
Irrationals like √2 ≈ 1.414 and π ≈ 3.141 are specific points between rationals
1.4.4 Key Real Number Traps on CET
√4 = 2 (integer → rational → real), BUT √2 is irrational (real but NOT rational)
π is real but NOT rational (never write π = 22/7 on a classification question!)
0 is real, rational, integer — but NOT natural
are rational (can write as n/1), NOT the other way
1.4.5 Key Diagrams
A Hierarchy of Sets — shows ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ with
irrationals sitting in ℝ but outside ℚ
The Number Line: All Real Numbers — visualizes where integers, √2, and π
fall on the real line
Subset Chain Memory Aid — quick reference: ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ
Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete — Classify): Is -3.5 a real number? Is it rational? Is it an integer?
Solution: - -3.5 = -7/2 → rational ✓ - Since it’s rational, it’s automatically real ✓ - But
-3.5 is NOT an integer (not a whole number)
Answer: Real: ✅, Rational: ✅, Integer: ❌
Example 2 (Standard — Subset Logic): Which subset chain is CORRECT? (a) ℕ ⊂ ℚ ⊂
ℤ ⊂ ℝ (b) ℚ ⊂ ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℝ (c) ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ (d) ℤ ⊂ ℕ ⊂ ℝ ⊂ ℚ
Solution: - ℕ (1,2,3,…) are inside ℤ (…,-2,-1,0,1,2,…) → ℕ ⊂ ℤ - ℤ are inside ℚ (any
integer = n/1) → ℤ ⊂ ℚ - ℚ are inside ℝ → ℚ ⊂ ℝ
Answer: (c) ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ is correct.
Example 3 (Exam-Level): Which of these statements is FALSE? I. Every real number is
either rational or irrational II. Every rational number is a real number III. Every real
number is an integer IV. The set of real numbers is the union of rationals and irrationals
Solution: - I: True — real numbers are exactly rational ∪ irrational - II: True — ℚ ⊂ ℝ - III:
False — 1/2 is real but not an integer - IV: True — that’s the definition
Answer: III is false.
Example 4 (Indian Method — Extreme Case Testing): Is √(π) real?
Solution: - √(π) ≈ √3.14159… ≈ 1.772… - π is positive, so its square root exists on the real
number line - But √(π) is IRRATIONAL (π is irrational, square root of irrational ≠
rational) - So √(π) ∈ ℝ but ∉ ℚ
Answer: Yes, √(π) is real but irrational.
One-Page Lock-In Detail ℝ = ℚ ∪ ℚ^c
Reals = Rationals + Irrationals
ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ The subset chain (memorize!) Density
is another real
√(non-perfect-square) = irrational; π is NOT 22/7
Key rule If it’s on the number line, it’s real
operations) What You Need First Section 1.4 (Real Numbers) Section 1.1 (Number sets) The Intuition
are the four basic tools of math. Think of them as the
four fundamental moves in a game: | Operation | Symbol | What it does | Real-life
quantities | “I have ₱50 + you give me ₱20 = ₱70” | | Subtraction | − | Takes away /
finds difference | “I had ₱50, spent ₱30 = ₱20 left” | | Multiplication | × | Repeated
addition | “3 friends × ₱50 each = ₱150 total” | | Division | ÷ | Splits into equal
parts | “Share ₱100 among 4 = ₱25 each” | CET Tip: You must be comfortable with
these using: integers (positive & negative), fractions, decimals, and mixed
numbers. The Build-Up (Formal & Rigorous) 1.5.1 Integer Operations (Sign Rules) Operation

(+) + (+) Add, keep positive 5 + 3 = 8 (-) + (-) Add, keep negative -5 + (-3) = -8 (+) + (-) Subtract, keep sign of larger 5 + (-3) = 2; -5 + 3 = -2 (+) × (+) Positive 5 × 3 = 15 (-) × (-) Positive -5 × (-3) = 15 (+) × (-) Negative 5 × (-3) = -15; -5 × 3 = -15 (+) ÷ (+) Positive 15 ÷ 3 = 5 (-) ÷ (-) Positive -15 ÷ (-3) = 5 (+) ÷ (-) Negative 15 ÷ (-3) = -5 1.5.2 Fraction Operations Operation
Add/Subtract Common denominator first 1/3 + 1/4 = 4/12 + 3/12 = 7/12 Multiply
Multiply numerators, multiply denominators
2/3 × 3/4 = 6/12 = 1/2 Divide Flip the second fraction, then multiply 1/2 ÷ 1/4 = 1/2 × 4/1 = 4/2 = 2
Key fraction rules: - To simplify: divide numerator and denominator by their GCF - To
compare: cross-multiply (which is larger, 3/7 or 4/9? → 3×9=27 vs 4×7=28 → 4/9 is
larger) - Mixed numbers → improper fractions before operating: 2¹/₃ = 7/3
1.5.3 Decimal Operations Operation Subtract
Line up decimal points, add/subtract normally
Multiply
Multiply as whole numbers, count total decimal places in both numbers, place
decimal that many places from right Divide
Move decimal in divisor to make it whole, move decimal in dividend the same
number of places
Trick: 0.25 = 1/4, 0.5 = 1/2, 0.75 = 3/4, 0.1 = 1/10 — memorize these fraction-decimal
equivalents 1.5.4 Division by Zero
CRITICAL RULE: Division by zero is undefined. Never do it. - 5 ÷ 0 = undefined - 0 ÷
5 = 0 (this IS allowed — zero divided by anything is zero) - 0 ÷ 0 = indeterminate (special
case — will matter in limits and calculus)
Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete — Integer Operations): Calculate: (-7) + 5 + (-3) + 8
Answer: 3
Example 2 (Standard — Fraction Operations): Simplify: 3/4 + 1/6
Answer: 11/12
Example 3 (Exam-Level — Mixed Operations): Calculate: (2/3 − 1/2) ÷ 1/6
Solution: - Parentheses first: 2/3 − 1/2 = 4/6 − 3/6 = 1/6 - Division: 1/6 ÷ 1/6 = 1/6 × 6/1 =
Answer: 1
Example 4 (Indian Method — Cross-Multiplication): Which is larger, 5/8 or 3/5?
Answer: 5/8 is larger.

One-Page Lock-In Operation Sign Rule Key Pitfall Same signs = add; different = subtract Don’t forget negative results Subtract “Add the opposite” (a−b = a+(−b)) Subtracting a negative = adding Multiply Same sign = +; different sign = − Double negatives cancel Divide Same as multiplication NEVER divide by 0 Fraction ÷ Flip the second, multiply Don’t forget to flip!
What You Need First Section 1.5 (Arithmetic operations) Section 1.3 (Multiplication shortcuts) The Intuition
Counting problems ask: “How many ways can this happen?” The fundamental
counting principle says: if you have m ways to do one thing and n ways to do
another, you have m × n ways to do both. Think of ordering a meal: - 3 appetizers
× 4 main courses × 2 desserts = 24 possible meals This is just multiplication
applied to choices — and it’s the foundation for all probability and
What You Need First
Section 1.5 (Multiplication and division)
Section 1.2 (Operation properties) The Intuition
are shorthand for repeated multiplication. Instead of writing 2 ×
2 × 2 × 2 × 2, we write 2⁵. aⁿ = a × a × a × … × a \ (n times) Think of exponents like
a growth shortcut: - 2³ = “2 multiplied by itself 3 times” = 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 - 10⁴ = 10
× 10 × 10 × 10 = 10,000 Real-life: Exponents describe compound interest,
population growth, scientific notation, and radioactive decay — all things you’ll
see on the CET. The Build-Up (Formal & Rigorous) 1.7.1 The 7 Laws of Exponents Formula

Product am × aⁿ = a^(m+n) 2³ × 2⁴ = 2⁷ = 128 Quotient am ÷ aⁿ = a^(m−n) 2⁵ ÷ 2³ = 2² = 4 Power of a power (am)^n = a^(m×n) (2³)² = 2⁶ = 64 Power of a product (ab)^n = aⁿ × bⁿ (2×3)² = 2²×3² = 4×9=36 Power of a quotient (a/b)^n = aⁿ / bⁿ (2/3)² = 4/9 Zero exponent a⁰ = 1 (a ≠ 0) 5⁰ = 1, (anything)⁰ = 1 Negative exponent a^(−n) = 1 / aⁿ 2⁻³ = 1/8 1.7.2 Special Exponents
a¹ = a (any number to the power of 1 is itself)
1^n = 1 (1 to any power is 1) 0^n = 0 (0 to any positive power is 0)
0⁰ is undefined (trap question alert! Never equals 1)
1.7.3 Squares and Cubes (Memorize These!)
CET Tip: Know squares 1² through 20² cold. They appear in: square root questions,
Pythagorean theorem, distance formula, quadratic equations, and area problems.
1.7.4 Scientific Notation

blocks.term: Writing very large or very small numbers as
blocks.value: a × 10^n, where 1 ≤ a < 10
Number Scientific Notation 5,300,000
What You Need First Section 1.5 (All arithmetic operations) Section 1.7 (Exponents) The Intuition
PEMDAS / BODMAS is the traffic rules of math. Without it, one expression
could have different answers depending on who solves it. | Acronym | Meaning |
Alternative | |:-------:|:-------:|:-----------:| | P | Parentheses | B Brackets | | E |
Exponents | O Orders (powers/roots) | | M | Multiplication | D Division (same
rank, left→right) | | D | Division | M Multiplication (same rank, left→right) | | A |
Addition | A Addition (same rank, left→right) | | S | Subtraction | S Subtraction
(same rank, left→right) | Real-life analogy: Following a recipe — you can’t bake
the cake before measuring the flour (parentheses first), and you can’t frost it
before it’s baked (exponents/powers before addition). CET Trap: These questions
are designed to trick students who rush. They will add multiple layers of
parentheses and position operations to test whether you follow PEMDAS or just
go left→right. The Build-Up (Formal & Rigorous) 1.8.1 The Hierarchy (Mandatory Order) P — Parentheses (innermost first) E — Exponents
MD — Multiplication & Division (left to right, same rank)
AS — Addition & Subtraction (left to right, same rank)
1.8.2 Step-by-Step Method
Scan for parentheses — solve inside them first, applying PEMDAS INSIDE each

Handle exponents — powers, roots Multiply/Divide — work left to right Add/Subtract — work left to right 1.8.3 Grouping Symbols Symbol Order to Solve Parentheses Innermost first Brackets After parentheses Braces After brackets Fraction bar
Acts as grouping: numerator and denominator are grouped
A fraction like (2+3)/(4−1) means both numerator and denominator must
be calculated FIRST before dividing. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete — Basic PEMDAS): Simplify: 3 + 6 × 2
Solution: - Multiplication first: 6 × 2 = 12 - Then addition: 3 + 12 = 15
Common mistake: Going left→right: (3+6)×2 = 18 ❌
Answer: 15
Example 2 (Standard — With Parentheses): Simplify: (3 + 6) × 2
Solution: - Parentheses: 3 + 6 = 9 - Multiplication: 9 × 2 = 18
Answer: 18
Example 3 (Exam-Level — Nested): Simplify: 2 × [3 + (5 − 2)²] ÷ 3
Solution: - Innermost: (5 − 2) = 3 - Exponent: 3² = 9 - Brackets: 3 + 9 = 12 - Left to right: 2
Answer: 8
Example 4 (Trap Question): Simplify: 8 ÷ 2(2 + 2)
Solution: - Parentheses first: (2+2) = 4 - Now: 8 ÷ 2 × 4 (MD — same rank, left to right) - 8
Answer: 16
⚠️ Note: Some conventions treat implied multiplication (2(4)) as higher priority. On the
CET, follow strict left→right MD/AS order.
One-Page Lock-In What to do
Parentheses (innermost first) (3+2) = 5 Exponents 4² = 16 3. MD Multiply/Divide (left → right) 8÷2×4 = 4×4 = 16 4. AS Add/Subtract (left → right) 10−3+2 = 7+2 = 9
Trap: “Please Excuse My Dear Aunt Sally” — but remember MD and AS are SAME
RANK, left to right!

🔒 Reminder 1: ℝ = ℚ ∪ Irrationals. Every real number is either rational
or irrational — never both, never neither.
🔒 Reminder 2: The subset chain is ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ. Irrationals go in ℝ
but NOT in ℚ.
🔒 Reminder 3: Between any two distinct real numbers, there’s always
another real number. This is called the density property.
🔒 Reminder 1: Same signs → positive result. Different signs →
negative result (for multiplication and division).
🔒 Reminder 2: NEVER divide by zero. If you see a denominator that
equals zero, the expression is undefined.
🔒 Reminder 3: When adding fractions, find the common denominator
FIRST. Don’t be tempted to add numerators and denominators separately.
🔒 Reminder 1: The Fundamental Counting Principle: multiply the
number of choices for each position.
🔒 Reminder 2: 0! = 1 by definition. It’s not zero.
🔒 Reminder 3: Factorials grow fast: 4! = 24, 5! = 120, 6! = 720. Memorize
these for speed.
🔒 Reminder 1: a⁰ = 1 always (as long as a ≠ 0). 0⁰ is undefined — never
equals 1 or 0.
Subset Chain: How Number Sets Fit Together
Natural Integers Rational Irrationals √2, π, e (can't write as p/q)
are inside ℝbut NOT inside ℚ
Memory: Each set adds more numbers. ℕis the smallest, ℝis the biggest.
🔒 Reminder 2: Negative exponents mean RECIPROCAL, not negative
numbers: 2⁻³ = 1/8, not -8.
🔒 Reminder 3: Memorize squares 1² through 20². They appear
everywhere on the CET.
🔒 Reminder 1: PEMDAS: Parentheses → Exponents → Multiplication/
Division (L→R) → Addition/Subtraction (L→R).
🔒 Reminder 2: Multiplication and Division are SAME rank — go left to
right. Don’t always do multiplication before division!
🔒 Reminder 3: Fraction bars group the numerator and denominator.
Calculate top and bottom SEPARATELY before dividing.

Equations, Word Problems) - All Chapter 1 sections (full prerequisite set)
The Intuition
are like equations, but instead of saying two things are equal, they
say one is greater than/less than the other. Graphs on a number line show all
possible solutions, and Chinese 区间法 (Interval Method) writes solutions as
compact intervals. The Build-Up
Inequality Symbols: - < : less than (open circle on graph) - > : greater than (open
circle) - ≤ : less than or equal to (closed circle) - ≥ : greater than or equal to (closed circle)
Solving Inequalities: Same as equations, except: when multiplying/dividing both sides
by a negative number, flip the inequality sign.
Chinese 区间法 (Interval Method): Write solutions as intervals: - (a, b) = a < x < b
(open interval, parentheses for <, >) - [a, b] = a ≤ x ≤ b (closed interval, brackets for ≤, ≥)
- (a, b] = a < x ≤ b, [a, b) = a ≤ x < b (half-open) - (-∞, a) = x < a, (a, ∞) = x > a (infinity
always uses parenthesis)
Derivation: For x + 3 < 7: subtract 3 → x <4. On a graph, open circle at 4, arrow left.
Interval: (-∞, 4). For -2x ≥ 6: divide by -2, flip sign → x ≤ -3. Graph: closed circle at -3,
arrow left. Interval: (-∞, -3]. Layman Terms
are equations with wiggle room. Instead of x being exactly 2, x can be
any number less than 4.
The interval notation is a short way to write all those infinite numbers:
(-∞,4) means every number smaller than 4, forever. Imagine a number
line as a road. Inequalities mark which parts of the road are allowed.
Open circles mean “don’t include this point”, closed means “include it”.
The Chinese 区间法 is just a cheat code to write that road segment in
5 characters instead of a paragraph! Ali Abdaal Style: The only tricky
is flipping the sign when multiplying/dividing by
a negative. I’ve seen so many students forget this in exams — set a
reminder on your calculator if you have to!
The interval method is super useful for CET exams, it saves time
writing answers. Worked Examples (5)
All examples use Chinese 区间法 for final answers, as required in CET exams.
Example 1: Solve x + 2 < 5, graph, write in 区间法.
Solve: x < 3
Graph: Open circle at 3, arrow left
Example 2: Solve 3y ≥ 12, graph, write in 区间法.
Solve: y ≥4
Graph: Closed circle at4, arrow right
Example 3: Solve -2z + 1 < 5, graph, write in 区间法.
Subtract 1: -2z <4 Divide by -2, flip sign: z > -2
Graph: Open circle at -2, arrow right
Example 4: Solve 1 < 2x + 3 ≤ 7, graph, write in 区间法.
Subtract 3 all parts: -2 < 2x ≤4 Divide by 2: -1 < x ≤2
Graph: Open circle at -1, closed at 2, line between
Example 5: Solve (x/3) - 2 ≥ 1, graph, write in 区间法.
Add 2: x/3 ≥3 Multiply by3: x ≥9
Graph: Closed circle at9, arrow right
区间法: [9, ∞) One-Page Lock-In
Key Takeaways for 2.5 Inequalities & Graphs: - Inequality symbols: <, >, ≤, ≥ - Flip
sign when multiplying/dividing by negative - Open circle = <, >; closed circle = ≤, ≥ -
Chinese 区间法 (Interval Method): - ( ) = open, [ ] = closed - ∞ always uses ( ) -
Example: 2 <x ≤5 → (2,5] -
Section 2.5 Reminders
NEVER forget to flip the inequality sign when multiplying/dividing by a negative
is not a number, always use parentheses with ∞
Check solutions with a test point (e.g., for x>2, test x=3 to see if it works)
区间法 is required for CET answers — don’t write “x>3” as final answer, use
interval notation
Chapter 1, Section 1.4 (Remainder theorem, factor theorem)
The Intuition
A quadratic equation (ax² + bx + c = 0) has a hidden secret: it’s a PARABOLA in
disguise. The solutions (roots) are where the parabola crosses the x-axis.
Sometimes it crosses twice (2 real roots), touches once (1 repeated root), or never
crosses (no real roots — discriminant < 0).
ExamSID has 1000+ JEE Advanced quadratic questions. The Chinese 构造法
(constructive method) involves completing the square — it’s how the quadratic
formula was discovered! Master it, and you’ll understand WHY the formula
works, not just memorize it. The Build-Up (Formal & Rigorous)
3.3.1 Deriving the Quadratic Formula (Completing the Square)
Start with ax² + bx + c = 0 (a ≠ 0) 1. Divide by a: x² + (b/a)x + c/a = 0 2. Move constant: x²
+ (b/a)x = -c/a 3. Complete the square: x² + (b/a)x + (b/2a)² = -c/a + (b/2a)² 4. Left side =
(x + b/2a)² 5. Right side = (-4ac + b²)/(4a²) = (b² - 4ac)/(4a²) 6. Take square root: x + b/2a =
±√(b² - 4ac)/(2a) 7. Solve for x: x = (-b ± √(b² - 4ac))/(2a) ∎
3.3.2 The Discriminant (D = b² - 4ac) D > 0: Two distinct real roots
D = 0: One repeated real root
D < 0: No real roots (two complex conjugate roots)
3.3.3 Chinese 构造法 (Constructive Method — Completing the Square)
Instead of memorizing the formula, construct it: 1. Move constant to right: x² + 6x = -8 2.
Add (b/2)² to BOTH sides: x² + 6x + 9 = -8 + 9 = 1 3. Left = (x + 3)², so (x + 3)² = 1 4. Take
square root: x + 3 = ±1 5. Solve: x = -3 ± 1 → x = -2 or x = -4
Layman Terms
Imagine a parabola as a U-shaped valley. The quadratic formula tells you where
the valley crosses the x-axis (ground level).
is above ground (D < 0), it never crosses — no real
solutions. If it just touches ground (D = 0), one solution. If it plunges
through (D > 0), two solutions.
The formula is just geometry in algebraic form! Completing the square
is like turning a messy room (x² + 6x + 8) into a neat box ((x+3)² - 1).
You’re not changing the room’s contents, just rearranging to see it
clearly.
This method scales to ANY quadratic — it’s how ancient
mathematicians discovered the formula 1000+ years ago! Ali Abdaal
(Utility & Exam Tips): Here’s the mistake 90% of students make: they
try to factor EVERY quadratic. If D isn’t a perfect square (like D = 10),
you CAN’T factor it nicely — just use the quadratic formula! ExamSID
drills this: if you can’t factor in 10 seconds, switch to the formula.
It’s faster and less error-prone. Worked Examples
Example 3.3.1 (Factoring — Concrete) Solve: x² - 5x + 6 = 0
Solution (Factoring): 1. Find p, q: p + q = -5, pq = 6 → p = -2, q = -3 2. Factor: (x - 2)(x - 3)
= 0 3. Solve: x = 2 or x = 3
Example 3.3.2 (Quadratic Formula — Standard) Solve: 2x² + 5x - 3 = 0
Example 3.3.3 (Completing the Square — Standard) Solve: x² + 6x + 8 = 0
Solution (Chinese 构造法): 1. Move constant: x² + 6x = -8 2. Add (6/2)² = 9 to both sides:
x² + 6x + 9 = 1 3. Left = (x + 3)²: (x + 3)² = 1 4. Square root: x + 3 = ±1 5. Solve: x = -3 + 1
Example 3.3.4 [Indian Method: Sum/Product Shortcut] For x² - 5x + 6 = 0, find sum
and product of roots WITHOUT solving.
Solution (Indian Shortcut): - Sum of roots = -b/a = -(-5)/1 = 5 - Product of roots = c/a = 6/1
Check: Roots are 2 and 3 → 2+3=5 ✓, 2×3=6 ✓
Answer: Sum = 5, Product = 6
Example 3.3.5 [Chinese Method: 赋值法] Verify x = 2 and x = 3 are roots of x² - 5x +
Chinese Method (赋值法): 1. Assign x = 2: 4 - 10 + 6 = 0 ✓ 2. Assign x = 3: 9 - 15 + 6 = 0 ✓
3. Both satisfy → verified ✓
Answer: Verified, x = 2 and x = 3 are roots.
One-Page Lock-In QUADRATIC EQUATIONS QUICK REFERENCE: Method When to Use Steps
Factoring
D = perfect square
Find p+q=b, pq=c → (x+p) (x+q)=0 x²-5x+6=0 → (x-2) (x-3)=0 Quadratic Formula ANY quadratic
Works always! Completing Square Derive formula, Chinese Add (b/2)² to both sides x²+6x+8=0 → (x+3)²=1
DISCRIMINANT (D = b² - 4ac): - D > 0 → 2 distinct real roots - D = 0 → 1 repeated
root - D < 0 → No real roots
SUM/PRODUCT OF ROOTS: - Sum = -b/a - Product = c/a
Section 3.3 Reminders
End Chapter Checkpoint

blocks.value: The Intuition
Permutations and combinations count how many ways you can arrange or
choose items. Permutations are for ordered lists (passwords: 123 ≠ 321).
are for unordered groups (teams: ABC = BCA). Factorial (n!) is
just multiplying all numbers from n down to 1.
The Build-Up (Formal & Rigorous) 9.3.1 Factorial
n! (n factorial) = n × (n-1) × (n-2) × … × 1
0! = 1 (by definition, critical for formulas to work)
9.3.2 Permutations (Order Matters)
Number of ways to arrange r items from a set of n items, where order matters: n Pr =
Example: 3P2 = 3!/(1!) = 6 (arrangements: AB, BA, AC, CA, BC, CB)
9.3.3 Combinations (Order Doesn’t Matter)
Number of ways to choose r items from a set of n items, where order doesn’t matter: n Cr
r!(n - r)! = n Pr
Example: 3C2 = 3!/(2!1!) = 3 (groups: AB, AC, BC)
9.3.4 Key Difference Permutation (n Pr) Combination (n Cr) Order matters Order does not matter
Used for arrangements, passwords, lineups
Used for teams, groups, selections Layman Terms
Imagine 3 people: A, B, C. Permutations of 2: AB, BA, AC, CA, BC, CB (6 total,
order matters).
Combinations of 2: AB, AC, BC (3 total, order doesn’t matter). n Pr
counts ordered lists, n Cr counts unordered groups. Factorial is just the
number of ways to arrange all n items: 3! = 6 ways to line up 3 people.
is like a chain reaction: 5! = 5×4×3×2×1 = 120.
It’s how many ways you can line up 5 items. Permutations are lineups
of part of the group, combinations are just picking groups without
lining them up. The universe loves counting — these formulas are
shortcuts for manual counting. Ali Abdaal Style: The #1 CET mistake
here is mixing up n Pr and n Cr.
Ask yourself: does order matter? If yes, use n Pr. If no, use n Cr. Pro tip:
n Cr is always smaller than n Pr for the same n and r, because it
removes duplicate ordered groups.
Memorize 0! = 1 — it’s tested constantly.
Worked Examples (5)
All examples use ExamSID convention for step-by-step factorial simplification.
Example 1 (Concrete — Factorial): Calculate 5!.
Example 2 (Standard — Permutation): Calculate 10P3.
Example 3 (Standard — Combination): Calculate 10C3.
Example 4 (Exam-Level — Arrangements): How many ways to arrange 3
letters from A, B, C, D?
Order matters (arrangements), use 4P3 = 4!/(1!) = 24
Example 5 (Exam-Level — Combinations): How many ways to choose 3
letters from A, B, C, D?
Order doesn’t matter (groups), use 4C3 = 4!/(3!1!) = 4
One-Page Lock-In
KEY FORMULAS (Memorize!): | Term | Formula | When to Use |
Permutation | n Pr = n!/(n-r)! | Order matters, arrange r from n | | Combination | n Cr = n!/
(r!(n-r)!) | Order doesn’t matter, choose r from n |
THE ONE THING TO NEVER FORGET: 0! = 1, always. Ask “does order matter?” to
choose between n Pr and n Cr. VISUAL MEMORY AID:
Permutation = Password (123 ≠ 321)
Combination = Team (ABC = BCA)

math.title: Variables

blocks.value: In mathematics, a variable is a letter or symbol that represents an unknown or changeable value. The most commonly used variables are x, y, z, a, b, c, n, and t — but any letter can serve as a variable. Variables are the foundation of algebra and appear in nearly every CET math question. Understanding what variables are and how they work is the first step toward mastering algebra.
a letter or symbol that stands for a number whose value can change or is not yet known. For example, in the expression 3x + 5, the letter x is the variable.
a fixed number that does not change. In the expression 3x + 5, the number 5 is a constant because it stays the same no matter what value x takes.
the number placed directly in front of a variable that tells you how many times to multiply that variable. In 3x, the coefficient is 3, meaning 3 times x.

Why do we use variables? Variables let us write general rules instead of working with specific numbers every time. For example, instead of calculating the area of a rectangle with length 5 and width 3, we write A = l × w so the formula works for any rectangle. This is the power of algebra — one formula can solve thousands of different problems.
Identify the variable, coefficient, and constant in 7y − 2. The variable is y, the coefficient is 7, and the constant is −2.
In the expression 4a + 3b − 10, identify all parts. The variables are a and b. The coefficient of a is 4, the coefficient of b is 3, and the constant is −10.

CET Tip: Many CET questions ask you to identify parts of an expression. Always look for the number right next to the variable — that is the coefficient. If a variable has no number in front of it (like just x), the coefficient is 1.
In the expression 5x² + 2x − 8: coefficient of x² is 5, coefficient of x is 2, constant is −8.
Identify the variable, coefficient, and constant in each expression: (a) 9m + 4 (b) −3p (c) n/2 − 5 (d) 2x + 3y

sections.title: Types of Variables: Independent and Dependent

blocks.value: In many real-world situations and CET word problems, variables are not all the same type. It is important to understand the difference between independent and dependent variables, especially when dealing with functions, graphs, and word problems.
the variable that you freely choose or control. It is the input of a situation. On a graph, it is usually plotted on the x-axis (horizontal axis).
the variable whose value depends on the independent variable. It is the output of a situation. On a graph, it is usually plotted on the y-axis (vertical axis).

Think of it this way: if you are buying apples at ₱30 each, the number of apples you buy is the independent variable (you choose it), and the total cost is the dependent variable (it depends on how many apples you buy). The relationship is: Total Cost = 30 × Number of Apples, or y = 30x.

A taxi charges ₱40 flag-down rate plus ₱10 per kilometer. Write the relationship and identify the variables. Let d = distance in km (independent variable) and C = total cost in pesos (dependent variable). The formula is C = 40 + 10d.

A student earns ₱150 per hour tutoring. If h represents hours worked and E represents earnings, write the equation. E = 150h. Here, h is independent (student chooses hours) and E is dependent (earnings depend on hours).

CET Tip: When a problem says 'let x represent...' pay attention to whether x is something you control (independent) or something that results from another quantity (dependent). This distinction matters for graphing and function questions.

For each situation, identify the independent and dependent variables and write an equation: (a) A phone plan charges ₱500 base plus ₱5 per text message. (b) A car travels at 60 km/h; find the distance after t hours. (c) A store sells notebooks for ₱25 each; find the total cost for n notebooks.

sections.title: Algebraic Expressions: Terms, Parts, and Structure

blocks.value: An algebraic expression is a mathematical phrase that contains variables, numbers, and operations (such as +, −, ×, ÷). Expressions do NOT have an equals sign. Understanding the structure of expressions is essential for simplifying, evaluating, and solving algebra problems on the CET.
a single part of an expression separated by + or − signs. In the expression 3x² + 5x − 7, the three terms are 3x², 5x, and −7.
terms that have the same variable raised to the same power. For example, 4x and −2x are like terms because they both contain x to the first power. But 3x and 3x² are NOT like terms.
terms that have different variables or different powers. For example, 5x and 3y are unlike terms, and so are 2x and 2x².
List all terms in the expression 6a − 4b + 2a + 9. The terms are: 6a, −4b, 2a, and 9. Notice that 6a and 2a are like terms because they both contain the variable a.
Are 5xy and 3xy like terms? Yes! Both have the same variables (x and y) with the same powers (each to the first power). Are 5xy and 5x²y like terms? No — the power of x is different.
CET Tip: Questions often test whether you can identify like terms. Remember: like terms must have the exact same variable(s) with the exact same exponent(s). The coefficients can be different.
Identify the like terms in each expression: (a) 3x + 5y − x + 2y (b) 4a² + 3a − a² + 7 (c) 2pq + 5p − 3pq + p
One of the most common sources of confusion in algebra is the difference between expressions and equations. The CET frequently tests this distinction, so you must know it cold.

a mathematical phrase that combines variables, numbers, and operations, but does NOT have an equals sign. Examples: 3x + 5, 2a − b, 7y². You can simplify or evaluate an expression, but you cannot 'solve' it.
a mathematical sentence that states two expressions are equal, connected by an equals sign (=). Examples: 3x + 5 = 20, 2a − b = 10. You can SOLVE an equation to find the value of the variable.

The key difference: an expression is like a phrase ('three times a number plus five'), while an equation is like a complete sentence ('three times a number plus five equals twenty'). You solve equations, not expressions.

Classify each as an expression or equation: (a) 4x − 7 — This is an expression (no equals sign). (b) 4x − 7 = 13 — This is an equation (has an equals sign). (c) 2(a + b) — Expression. (d) 2(a + b) = 10 — Equation.
