Top Philippine Universities for STEM Programs: University of the Philippines (UP) — Diliman (Engineering, Science), Los Baños (Agriculture, Veterinary), Manila (Medicine, Public Health). Ateneo de Manila University — Information and Computer Science (ICS), Physics, Mathematics. De La Salle University (DLSU) — College of Engineering (CoE), Computer Studies. University of Santo Tomas (UST) — Faculty of Engineering, Faculty of Medicine and Surgery. Mapúa University — Engineering and Information Technology.

Example 1 (CET Application): Q: What is the derivative of f(x) = 3x⁴ - 2x² + 7? A) 12x³ - 4x ✓ B) 12x³ - 2x C) 4x³ - 2x D) 12x⁴ - 4x Explanation: Apply the power rule d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ to each term: d/dx[3x⁴] = 12x³, d/dx[-2x²] = -4x, d/dx[7] = 0. Answer: A) 12x³ - 4x.

Example 2 (CET Application): Q: A ball is thrown upward with initial velocity 20 m/s. Using g = 10 m/s², what is the maximum height? A) 10 m B) 20 m ✓ C) 40 m D) 80 m Explanation: At max height, v = 0. Using v² = v₀² - 2gh → 0 = 400 - 20h → h = 20 m. This is a typical CET physics problem that STEM students encounter in their coursework.

STEM Practice Questions: 1) Find the integral of ∫(4x³ + 2x)dx. 2) What is the molar mass of H₂SO₄? 3) In a circuit with 3 resistors (2Ω, 3Ω, 6Ω) in parallel, what is the total resistance? 4) If a cell undergoes meiosis, how many daughter cells are produced? Answers: 1) x⁴ + x² + C, 2) 98 g/mol, 3) 1Ω, 4) 4 daughter cells.

The ABM strand (Accountancy, Business, and Management) is the Academic Track strand for students interested in business, finance, entrepreneurship, and corporate management. Under the Philippine K-12 framework established by DepEd, ABM provides students with foundational knowledge in financial literacy, business operations, economic principles, and organizational management. ABM students develop strong analytical skills in business contexts, numerical reasoning for financial data, and communication skills for professional settings — all of which are valuable in CET preparation.

an Academic Track strand under the K-12 SHS program that prepares students for business-related college programs by developing competencies in financial accounting, economics, marketing, and organizational leadership.

ABM Strand Core Subjects: The ABM curriculum includes specialized applied subjects that go beyond the core SHS courses. Students take Fundamentals of Accountancy, Business, and Management 1 and 2 (FABM 1 & 2), Business Mathematics, Business Finance, Organization and Management, Principles of Marketing, and Applied Economics. These subjects build skills in financial statement analysis, cost-volume-profit relationships, market analysis, and business decision-making. The math required is more applied than theoretical — focusing on percentages, ratios, interest calculations, markup and margin, depreciation, and break-even analysis.

Key ABM subjects and their CET relevance: Business Mathematics covers markup, margin, discounts, simple and compound interest, and depreciation — topics that appear in CET quantitative reasoning sections. Economics covers supply and demand, market equilibrium, GDP, inflation, fiscal and monetary policy — which show up in general education or social science CET sections. Accounting covers the accounting cycle, journal entries, ledgers, trial balance, and financial statements. Management covers planning, organizing, leading, and controlling (POLC framework), Henri Fayol's principles, and basic organizational theory.
ABM CET Focus: Business Math = Markup, Interest, Depreciation | Economics = Supply/Demand, GDP, Inflation | Accounting = Journal Entries, Financial Statements | Management = POCCC, Fayol's Principles

Career Paths for ABM Graduates: ABM prepares students for careers in the business and finance sectors. Common career paths include: Certified Public Accountant (CPA), Financial Analyst, Business Manager, Marketing Specialist, Human Resources Manager, Entrepreneur, Banking and Financial Services Professional, Management Consultant, Tax Specialist, Auditor, and Supply Chain Manager. In the Philippines, the BPO (Business Process Outsourcing) industry also actively recruits ABM-aligned graduates for accounting, finance, and management roles.

Aligned College Programs for ABM: The most common degree programs pursued by ABM graduates include: BS Accountancy (BSA), BS Business Administration (BSBA), BS Entrepreneurship, BS Management, BS Financial Management, BS Marketing Management, BS Internal Auditing, BS Accounting Technology, BS Real Estate Management, BS Customs Administration, and BS Economics. The BS Accountancy program is one of the most competitive, requiring high CET scores in math and logic, followed by a challenging board exam (CPALE) after graduation.

Top Philippine Universities for ABM Programs: De La Salle University (DLSU) — College of Business and Economics, top-ranked accountancy program. University of the Philippines (UP) — Diliman (School of Economics, College of Business Administration). Ateneo de Manila University — John Gokongwei School of Management. University of Santo Tomas (UST) — Faculty of Commerce and Business Administration. San Beda University, Far Eastern University (FEU), and University of the East (UE) — known for strong accountancy programs with high CPALE passing rates.

Example 1 (CET Application): Q: A product costs ₱500 and is sold for ₱750. What is the markup percentage? A) 33.3% B) 50% ✓ C) 66.7% D) 150% Explanation: Markup = (Selling Price - Cost) / Cost × 100 = (750 - 500) / 500 × 100 = 50%. Note: Markup is based on cost, not selling price. This distinction is commonly tested in CETs.

Example 2 (CET Application): Q: If ₱10,000 is invested at 5% compounded annually, what is the amount after 3 years? A) ₱11,500 B) ₱11,576.25 ✓ C) ₱15,000 D) ₱10,150 Explanation: A = P(1 + r)ⁿ = 10,000(1.05)³ = 10,000 × 1.157625 = ₱11,576.25. Compound interest problems are standard in CET math sections.

ABM Practice Questions: 1) A store buys an item for ₱800 and marks it up by 25%. What is the selling price? 2) What is the demand curve's typical slope (positive or negative)? 3) In accounting, what side do asset increases go to — debit or credit? 4) Name the four functions of management (POLC). Answers: 1) ₱1,000, 2) Negative (downward sloping), 3) Debit (left side), 4) Planning, Organizing, Leading, Controlling.

The HUMSS strand (Humanities and Social Sciences) is the Academic Track strand for students passionate about understanding human behavior, society, culture, governance, and communication. Under the Philippine K-12 curriculum mandated by DepEd (Republic Act No. 10533 — Enhanced Basic Education Act of 2013), HUMSS develops critical thinking, reading comprehension, persuasive writing, and qualitative research skills. HUMSS students are well-prepared for the reading comprehension, verbal reasoning, and general knowledge sections of CETs, though they may need to supplement their math preparation.

an Academic Track strand under the K-12 SHS program that focuses on the study of human society, culture, behavior, governance, and communication through disciplines like philosophy, psychology, sociology, political science, literature, and history.

HUMSS Strand Core Subjects: The HUMSS curriculum includes specialized applied courses that explore the human experience from multiple angles. Students take Creative Writing, Creative Nonfiction, Introduction to World Religions and Belief Systems, Disciplines and Ideas in the Social Sciences (DISS), Disciplines and Ideas in the Applied Social Sciences (DIASS), and Community Engagement, Solidarity, and Citizenship. These courses develop skills in critical analysis, argumentation, research methodology, and cultural understanding.

Key HUMSS subjects and their CET relevance: Philosophy covers logic (valid arguments, fallacies), ethics (moral frameworks), and epistemology — logic questions appear in CET analytical reasoning sections. Psychology covers major theories (Freud, Piaget, Erikson, Vygotsky), learning theories (classical and operant conditioning), and personality — tested in general knowledge sections. Sociology covers social institutions, stratification, culture, and socialization. Political Science covers government systems, the 1987 Philippine Constitution, international organizations (UN, ASEAN), and Philippine governance. Literature and History cover Philippine and World literature, Philippine history (pre-colonial to present), and Asian history.

HUMSS CET Focus: Philosophy = Logic, Ethics, Fallacies | Psychology = Freud, Piaget, Erikson | Sociology = Social Institutions, Culture | Political Science = Constitution, Government | History = Philippine & World History

Career Paths for HUMSS Graduates: HUMSS opens doors to professions that involve understanding, serving, and communicating with people. Common career paths include: Lawyer (requires pre-law + law school), Psychologist or Guidance Counselor, Teacher or Professor, Journalist or Media Professional, Social Worker, Diplomat or Foreign Service Officer, Public Administrator, Historian or Archivist, Writer or Editor, HR Specialist, Politician or Public Servant, and NGO/Development Worker. Many HUMSS graduates also enter the civil service, passing the Career Service Examination to work in government agencies.

Aligned College Programs for HUMSS: The most common degree programs pursued by HUMSS graduates include: AB Political Science, AB Psychology, AB Philosophy, AB English, AB Literature, AB History, AB Sociology, AB Journalism, AB Communication, AB International Studies, BS Social Work, AB Creative Writing, Bachelor of Elementary Education (BEEd), Bachelor of Secondary Education (BSEd), and AB Legal Management. These programs serve as excellent pre-law preparation — many successful bar passers come from Political Science, Philosophy, and English backgrounds.

Top Philippine Universities for HUMSS Programs: University of the Philippines (UP) — Diliman (College of Social Sciences and Philosophy, College of Arts and Letters), Manila (College of Arts and Sciences). Ateneo de Manila University — Department of Communication, Political Science, Philosophy, Psychology. University of Santo Tomas (UST) — Faculty of Arts and Letters (one of the oldest in the Philippines). De La Salle University (DLSU) — College of Liberal Arts. Far Eastern University (FEU) — Institute of Arts and Sciences. Polytechnic University of the Philippines (PUP) — College of Arts.

Example 1 (CET Application): Q: According to Freud, which part of the personality operates on the pleasure principle? A) Ego B) Superego C) Id ✓ D) Unconscious Explanation: The Id operates on the pleasure principle, seeking immediate gratification. The Ego operates on the reality principle, and the Superego on the morality principle. Freud's structural model is a frequently tested CET psychology topic.

Example 2 (CET Application): Q: Which logical fallacy is demonstrated: 'Everyone is buying this product, so it must be good'? A) Ad hominem B) Bandwagon ✓ C) Straw man D) Red herring Explanation: The bandwagon fallacy argues that something is true or good because many people believe or do it. This type of logical reasoning question appears in CET analytical sections.

HUMSS Practice Questions: 1) Who is considered the father of modern psychology? 2) What form of government does the Philippines have under the 1987 Constitution? 3) Name Piaget's four stages of cognitive development. 4) What is the difference between primary and secondary groups in sociology? Answers: 1) Wilhelm Wundt (or Sigmund Freud for psychoanalysis), 2) Presidential, representative, democratic republic, 3) Sensorimotor, Preoperational, Concrete Operational, Formal Operational, 4) Primary groups are intimate (family), secondary groups are formal/impersonal (workplace).

The GAS strand (General Academic Strand) is unique among the four Academic Track strands because it does not specialize in any single field. Instead, GAS provides a broad, flexible curriculum that draws subjects from STEM, ABM, and HUMSS, giving students a well-rounded academic foundation. Under DepEd's K-12 framework, GAS is ideal for students who have not yet decided on a specific college program or career path, or for those who want maximum flexibility when choosing a college degree. GAS students are accepted into virtually any college program in the Philippines.

an Academic Track strand under the K-12 SHS program that offers a flexible, non-specialized curriculum combining elements from STEM, ABM, and HUMSS, allowing students to pursue any college degree program upon graduation.

GAS Strand Core Subjects: Unlike the other three strands, GAS allows students to choose electives from across all three specialized strands. Core GAS applied subjects include Humanities 1 and 2, Social Sciences 1 and 2, Applied Economics, Organization and Management, Disaster Readiness and Risk Reduction, and a Research component. Students then select electives based on their interests — they can take STEM subjects (like calculus or physics), ABM subjects (like accounting or marketing), or HUMSS subjects (like psychology or political science). This flexibility is both an advantage and a challenge: students get breadth but may lack the depth that specialized strand students have.

Key GAS characteristics and CET relevance: Because GAS covers a wide range of subjects, GAS students are generalists. In CETs, they typically perform well in general knowledge, reading comprehension, and mixed-subject questions, but may need additional preparation for advanced math (calculus, trigonometry) or advanced science (physics, chemistry) questions that STEM students have studied in depth. GAS students should identify their target college programs early and focus their CET review on the subjects most relevant to those programs.
GAS CET Strategy: Identify target program → Review relevant strand subjects → Supplement weak areas → Focus on general knowledge advantage → Practice CET-style questions across all subjects

Advantages of the GAS Strand: GAS offers several strategic advantages for students. First, it provides maximum flexibility — graduates can apply to any college program without being limited by their strand. Second, it develops well-rounded knowledge across multiple disciplines, which helps in general CET sections. Third, it allows students to explore different fields before committing to a college major. Fourth, under CHED (Commission on Higher Education) guidelines, all SHS graduates regardless of strand are eligible for any college program, but GAS students have taken subjects from multiple areas, giving them broader preparation.

Career Paths for GAS Graduates: Because GAS is non-specialized, graduates can pursue virtually any career. Common paths include: Government and Public Service (civil service, LGU work), Business and Entrepreneurship (leveraging ABM electives), Education (leveraging HUMSS electives), Information Technology (leveraging STEM electives), Media and Communications, Social Work, Law (pre-law programs), and many others. The key for GAS students is to identify their interests early and build relevant skills through electives and extracurricular activities.

Aligned College Programs for GAS: GAS graduates are eligible for essentially all college programs, including: Any AB/BS program in liberal arts, sciences, business, engineering, education, and more. Popular choices include AB Political Science, AB Psychology, BS Business Administration, AB Communication, BS Information Technology, Bachelor of Education, AB English, BS Public Administration, and even BS Engineering or BS Accountancy (though these require strong math foundations that GAS students may need to develop independently). CHED Memorandum Order No. 105, s. 2017 affirms that strand alignment should not be a barrier to college admission.

Example 1 (CET Application): Q: A GAS student wants to take BS Computer Science in college. What should they prioritize in CET review? A) Only history and social science B) Advanced math (algebra, logic) and basic science ✓ C) Only business subjects D) Nothing — GAS covers everything Explanation: While GAS provides broad coverage, students targeting STEM-related programs should focus their CET review on math and science topics that they may not have studied as deeply as STEM strand students.

Example 2 (CET Application): Q: Under CHED guidelines, can a GAS graduate enroll in BS Accountancy? A) No, only ABM graduates can B) Yes, all SHS graduates are eligible regardless of strand ✓ C) Only if they take bridging courses D) Only with special permission Explanation: CHED policy ensures that SHS strand does not restrict college program eligibility. However, GAS students may need to work harder to match the accounting foundations that ABM students already have.

GAS Practice Questions: 1) What is the main advantage of the GAS strand? 2) If a GAS student wants to pursue law school, what electives should they prioritize? 3) Under CHED guidelines, does your SHS strand determine your college program eligibility? 4) What is the main disadvantage of GAS compared to specialized strands? Answers: 1) Flexibility — can pursue any college program, 2) HUMSS electives (Political Science, Philosophy, English), 3) No — all SHS graduates can enroll in any program, 4) Less depth in specialized subjects — may need extra review for CETs.

sections.title: Strand Comparison, Choosing the Right Strand, and CET Strategy

blocks.value: Choosing the right SHS strand is one of the most important decisions a Filipino student makes in the K-12 journey. While CHED policy ensures that all strands provide eligibility for any college program, your strand choice affects the depth of preparation you receive in specific subject areas, which directly impacts your CET performance and your readiness for college coursework. This section provides a comprehensive comparison of all four Academic Track strands, self-assessment questions to help you choose wisely, and a Philippine K-12 context overview.

Academic Track: One of the three tracks offered in Senior High School under the K-12 program (along with the Technical-Vocational-Livelihood Track and the Sports and Arts and Design Tracks). The Academic Track contains four strands: STEM, ABM, HUMSS, and GAS, each designed to prepare students for specific college degree clusters.

Comparison Table — Academic Track Strands: STEM focuses on Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics. Core subjects include Calculus, Physics, Chemistry, Biology. Skills developed: analytical reasoning, mathematical modeling, scientific inquiry, quantitative analysis. Career alignment: Engineering, Medicine, IT, Architecture, Research. Aligned college programs: BS Engineering, BS CS, BS Nursing, BS Biology, BS Architecture. CET advantage: Math and Science sections.

ABM focuses on Accountancy, Business, and Management. Core subjects include FABM, Business Math, Economics, Marketing, Management. Skills developed: financial literacy, business analysis, numerical reasoning, professional communication. Career alignment: Accounting, Business Management, Finance, Entrepreneurship. Aligned college programs: BS Accountancy, BSBA, BS Entrepreneurship, BS Economics. CET advantage: Quantitative reasoning and applied math sections.

HUMSS focuses on Humanities and Social Sciences. Core subjects include Philosophy, Psychology, Sociology, Political Science, Literature, History. Skills developed: critical thinking, reading comprehension, persuasive writing, qualitative research, cultural awareness. Career alignment: Law, Education, Psychology, Media, Government, Social Work. Aligned college programs: AB Political Science, AB Psychology, AB Communication, BEEd, BSEd, AB Philosophy. CET advantage: Reading Comprehension, Verbal Reasoning, General Knowledge sections.

GAS focuses on General Academic (non-specialized). Core subjects include a mix from all strands plus electives. Skills developed: versatility, broad knowledge base, adaptability, general problem-solving. Career alignment: Flexible — any field depending on electives chosen. Aligned college programs: Any AB/BS program (no restrictions). CET advantage: General Knowledge and mixed-subject sections, but may need extra prep for specialized topics.

Self-Assessment Questions — Choosing Your Strand: Answer these questions honestly to identify which strand suits you best. 1) Do you enjoy solving math problems and conducting science experiments? (→ Consider STEM) 2) Are you interested in how businesses make money, manage budgets, and grow? (→ Consider ABM) 3) Do you love reading, writing, debating, and understanding why people behave the way they do? (→ Consider HUMSS) 4) Are you unsure about your future career and want to keep your options open? (→ Consider GAS) 5) What college program are you targeting? Match it to the most aligned strand for the strongest preparation.

Philippine K-12 Context and DepEd Guidelines: The K-12 program (Republic Act No. 10533, signed in 2013) added two years of Senior High School to the Philippine education system. The goal was to align Philippine education with international standards, prepare students for college, employment, or entrepreneurship, and reduce the mismatch between SHS graduates and college program requirements. Under DepEd Order No. 31, s. 2019, the Academic Track offers four strands. DepEd ensures that all strands follow the same core curriculum (15 core subjects) plus applied/immersion subjects specific to each strand's focus.

DepEd and CHED Coordination: CHED Memorandum Order No. 105, s. 2017 establishes that college admission should not discriminate based on SHS strand. All SHS graduates, regardless of strand, are eligible for any college program. However, individual universities may set their own admission criteria, and some competitive programs (like BS Accountancy or BS Engineering) may require additional assessments or bridging programs for students from non-aligned strands. The CET (College Entrance Test) is designed to test both general academic ability and strand-specific knowledge.
Strand Decision Framework: Interest (What do you enjoy?) × Aptitude (What are you good at?) × Career Goal (What do you want to be?) = Best Strand Choice

CET Relevance of Strand Knowledge: Each CET section benefits from different strand preparation. Math sections: STEM students have the strongest advantage (calculus, trigonometry, statistics). ABM students are strong in applied math (percentages, interest). Science sections: STEM students excel (physics, chemistry, biology). English/Reading Comprehension: HUMSS students have the strongest advantage (extensive reading, writing, critical analysis). General Knowledge: GAS students have breadth, while HUMSS students excel in social sciences and history. Abstract/Logical Reasoning: All strands benefit, but STEM students often have an edge through mathematical logic.

Example — Strand Selection Scenario: Maria loves reading and wants to become a lawyer. She's good at English and history but struggles with calculus. Q: Which strand should Maria choose? A) STEM B) ABM C) HUMSS ✓ D) GAS Explanation: HUMSS is the best fit because it aligns with her interests (law, reading, history), builds skills critical for law school (critical thinking, argumentation, political science), and plays to her strengths. She would still need to prepare for CET math, but her HUMSS background gives her the strongest foundation for her career goal.

Example — CET Cross-Strand Challenge: A HUMSS student encounters this CET math question: Q: What is 15% of 800? A) 80 B) 100 C) 120 ✓ D) 150 Explanation: 0.15 × 800 = 120. While this is basic percentage math, HUMSS students who haven't practiced math recently might struggle. This is why all students, regardless of strand, should review math fundamentals before the CET.

Cross-Strand Practice Questions: 1) A STEM student needs to answer a CET question about the Socratic method. Which strand studied this? 2) An ABM student encounters a CET question on Freud's personality theory. Which strand covered this? 3) A HUMSS student faces a CET calculus question: Find d/dx of 5x³. What is the answer? 4) If you're undecided about your career, which strand gives the most flexibility? Answers: 1) HUMSS (Philosophy), 2) HUMSS (Psychology), 3) 15x², 4) GAS (General Academic Strand).

specialized.description: Covers STEM, ABM, HUMSS, and GAS strands with comparison and CET strategy for strand selection.

specialized.title: College Programs: Engineering, Business, Arts, Health & Education

blocks.value: Specialized Fields 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 46.1 Engineering Specialization before moving on.
2. The Intuition

Engineering is one of the most sought-after college programs in the Philippines. It demands strong math and science foundations and leads to licensed professions. Understanding each engineering discipline helps you choose the right CET target school and program.
The application of math, science, and technology to design, build, and maintain structures, machines, systems, and processes that solve real-world problems.
3. The Build-Up

Engineering Strand Focus: - Math: Advanced algebra, calculus, trigonometry, differential equations - Science: Physics (mechanics, electricity, thermodynamics), Chemistry - Technical Skills: Drafting, CAD, basic programming, laboratory work
Major Engineering Programs in the Philippines:

Designs and builds infrastructure — roads, bridges, buildings, dams. Board exam: CE Licensure Exam (CELE). Career: structural engineer, construction manager, geotechnical engineer. High demand due to ongoing infrastructure projects (Build Build Build program).

Deals with electrical systems, power generation, transmission, and distribution. Board exam: EE Licensure Exam. Career: power plant engineer, distribution engineer, control systems engineer. Critical for the energy sector.

Focuses on electronic circuits, telecommunications, signal processing, and embedded systems. Board exam: ECE Licensure Exam. Career: telecom engineer, network engineer, semiconductor designer. Booming due to BPO and tech industries.

Covers machines, HVAC systems, manufacturing, and robotics. Board exam: ME Licensure Exam. Career: manufacturing engineer, HVAC designer, automotive engineer. Versatile — applies to nearly every industry.

Applies chemistry and physics to process raw materials into useful products — fuels, food, pharmaceuticals. Board exam: ChemE Licensure Exam. Career: process engineer, quality control, refinery engineer.

Bridges hardware and software — computer architecture, embedded systems, networking. Board exam: CpE Licensure Exam (newer). Career: hardware engineer, firmware developer, systems architect. Overlaps with CS and IT.

Focuses on algorithms, data structures, AI, machine learning, and software development. No board exam (industry certifications optional). Career: software developer, data scientist, AI engineer. Highest starting salary among programs.
Practical application of technology — web development, database management, networking, cybersecurity. No board exam. Career: IT specialist, web developer, systems administrator, network engineer.
Engineering CET Topics:
| Subject | Topics | Sample Q |
| Math | Calculus, differential equations, matrices | Derivative of 3x² = 6x |
| Physics | Mechanics, thermodynamics, optics, circuits | F=ma, KE=½mv², V=IR |
| Chemistry | Organic chem, stoichiometry, thermochem | Balance C₆H₆ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O |
| Engineering Aptitude | Statics, circuits, materials, logic | Gear ratio, stress = F/A |
Board Exam Passing Rates (approximate national averages):
CE: 40–55% first-time pass rate
EE: 50–65% first-time pass rate
ECE: 45–60% first-time pass rate
ME: 50–65% first-time pass rate
ChemE: 55–70% first-time pass rate
Top Engineering Schools & Typical CET Score Ranges:
UP College of Engineering — UPCAT: top 10–15% of examinees
Mapúa University — MCLACE: strong math/science scores
DLSU (CoE) — DLSUCET: high quantitative + abstract reasoning
UST (Engineering) — USTET: above-average math and science
TIP, Adamson, PUP — entrance exams focus on math fundamentals
Engineering Careers (Starting Salary Range, Philippines):
Civil Engineer: ₱18,000–₱25,000/month
Electrical/Electronics Engineer: ⱱ18,000–₱28,000/month
Software Developer (CS/IT): ₱25,000–₱45,000/month
Mechanical Engineer: ₱18,000–₱25,000/month
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Engineering = using math and science to build things. Civil builds roads and buildings. Electrical works with power. ECE handles phones and signals. Mechanical works with machines. Chemical turns raw materials into products. Computer Engineering is hardware + software. CS is pure software and algorithms. IT is practical tech support and development.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Derivative of 5x³? - A) 5x² - B) 10x² - C) 15x² ✓ - D) 15x
Answer: C) 15x² (Power rule: 3×5x³⁻¹ = 15x²)
Example 2 (Applied): Q: A car accelerates from rest to 20 m/s in 5 seconds. What is the acceleration? - A) 2 m/s² - B) 4 m/s² ✓ - C) 10 m/s² - D) 100 m/s²
Answer: B) 4 m/s² (a = Δv/Δt = 20/5 = 4 m/s²)
Example 3 (Board-Style): Q: Which engineering branch designs HVAC systems? - A) Civil - B) Electrical - C) Mechanical ✓ - D) Chemical
Answer: C) Mechanical Engineering covers Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning systems.
Practice: 1) Integrate 6x dx = ? 2) Ohm's Law: V = 12V, R = 4Ω, I = ? 3) Which engineer designs bridge foundations?
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 46.2 Business Specialization before moving on.
2. The Intuition

Business programs prepare students for the corporate world, entrepreneurship, and finance. The Philippines has a strong demand for accountants, managers, and business analysts, especially with the growing BPO and fintech industries.

The study of financial recording, auditing, taxation, and reporting. Graduates take the Certified Public Accountant (CPA) Licensure Exam — one of the hardest board exams in the Philippines with a national passing rate of 25–40%.
Covers management, marketing, finance, operations, and human resources. No board exam but offers specializations: Marketing Management, Financial Management, Operations Management, HR Management.
The study of starting and managing businesses — business planning, venture capital, innovation, and small business management. No board exam. Focuses on practical business creation skills.
3. The Build-Up

Business Strand Focus (ABM - Accountancy, Business & Management): - Business Math: Markup, margin, interest, depreciation, break-even analysis - Accountancy: Financial accounting, auditing, taxation, cost accounting - Management: Operations, HR, strategic planning, organizational behavior - Economics: Micro/macro, supply/demand, market structures, GDP
Business CET Topics:
| Subject | Topics | Sample Q |
| Business Math | Markup, margin, break-even, interest | Markup = (SP-Cost)/Cost × 100% |
| Accounting | Journals, ledgers, financial statements | Debit left, Credit right (normal balance) |
| Economics | Markets, inflation, GDP, trade | Price ↑ when supply ↓ and demand ↑ |
| Management | Planning, organizing, leading, controlling | POSDC framework (Gulick) |
CPA Board Exam (Licensure Exam for Accountants):
National passing rate: 25–40% (one of the hardest board exams)
Coverage: Financial Accounting, Auditing, Taxation, Management Services, Regulatory Framework
Top CPA schools: UP, DLSU, San Beda, UST, UE, PUP
Top Business Schools & CET Score Ranges:
DLSU (Business) — DLSUCET: high verbal + quantitative
UP (Economics, Business Admin) — UPCAT: top 10–15%
Ateneo (Management, MEc) — ACET: strong holistic profile
UST (Commerce) — USTET: above-average general scores
PUP, UE, San Beda — entrance exams focus on math and reasoning
Business Careers (Starting Salary Range, Philippines):
Accountant (CPA): ₱18,000–₱30,000/month
Business Analyst (BPO): ₱25,000–₱40,000/month
Bank Officer: ₱18,000–₱25,000/month
Marketing Associate: ₱16,000–₱22,000/month
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Business = learning how companies make money. Accountancy = recording and reporting money (CPA exam is hard!). Business Administration = managing people and operations. Entrepreneurship = starting your own business. ABM strand is your preparation. Debit means left, Credit means right in accounting.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Markup on cost formula? - A) (SP - Cost)/SP - B) (SP - Cost)/Cost ✓ - C) Cost/SP - D) SP/Cost
Answer: B) (SP - Cost)/Cost. Markup is always based on cost. Margin is based on selling price.
Example 2 (Applied): Q: A product costs ₱80 and sells for ₱120. What is the markup percentage? - A) 33% - B) 40% - C) 50% ✓ - D) 60%
Example 3 (Accounting): Q: Assets = ₱500,000, Liabilities = ₱200,000. Owner's Equity? - A) ₱200,000 - B) ₱300,000 ✓ - C) ₱500,000 - D) ₱700,000
Answer: B) ₱300,000 (Assets = Liabilities + Equity → 500K = 200K + Equity → Equity = 300K)
Practice: 1) Break-even point = Fixed Costs / (SP - VC per unit). FC=₱50,000, SP=₱25, VC=₱15. BEP = ? 2) In accounting, which side do expenses increase? 3) What is GDP?
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 46.3 Arts and Design Specialization before moving on.
2. The Intuition

Arts and Design programs develop creativity, visual communication, and aesthetic skills. In the Philippines, the creative industry is growing rapidly — from advertising agencies to animation studios (the Philippines is a top animation outsourcing hub in Asia).

A 4-year degree focused on traditional and contemporary art — painting, sculpture, printmaking, photography, and mixed media. Includes art history and criticism. No board exam but portfolio-based admission.

A 5-year degree program that combines art and engineering to design buildings and structures. Graduates take the Architecture Licensure Exam (ALE). Passing rate: 50–65%. Career: architect, urban planner, interior architect.

Covers digital design, animation, video production, web design, and interactive media. No board exam. Career: graphic designer, animator, video editor, UX/UI designer. High demand in advertising, gaming, and BPO.

Designs consumer products — furniture, appliances, electronics, packaging. Focuses on form, function, usability, and aesthetics. No board exam. Career: product designer, furniture designer, packaging designer.
3. The Build-Up

Arts and Design Strand Focus: - Visual Arts: Drawing, painting, sculpture, photography, printmaking - Performing Arts: Music, theater, dance, film - Design: Graphic design, fashion design, interior design, industrial design - Art History: Movements (Renaissance, Baroque, Modern, Contemporary), famous artists, cultural context
Arts and Design CET Topics:
| Subject | Topics | Sample Q |
| Visual Arts | Color theory, composition, perspective | Primary colors: R, Y, B |
| Performing Arts | Music theory, theater terminology | Tempo = speed of music |
| Design | Principles of design, elements | Balance, contrast, rhythm, emphasis |
| Art History | Renaissance, Baroque, Modern, Filipino art | Mona Lisa = Leonardo da Vinci |
Top Arts/Design Schools & CET Requirements:
UP College of Fine Arts — UPCAT + portfolio review
UST College of Architecture / Fine Arts — USTET + talent test
Benilde (DLS-CSB) — DLSUCET + portfolio/interview
Ateneo (Fine Arts, AB Communication) — ACET + creative requirements
Mapúa (Multimedia Arts) — MCLACE entrance exam
Arts & Design Careers (Starting Salary Range, Philippines):
Graphic Designer: ₱15,000–₱25,000/month
Architect (licensed): ₱20,000–₱35,000/month
Animator/Motion Graphics: ₱18,000–₱35,000/month
UX/UI Designer: ₱25,000–₱45,000/month
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Arts and Design = creating visual and performing works. Fine Arts = traditional art (painting, sculpture). Architecture = designing buildings (5 years, has board exam). Multimedia Arts = digital art and animation. Industrial Design = designing everyday products. Most arts programs require a portfolio for admission, not just test scores.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Primary colors in traditional color theory? - A) Red, Yellow, Blue ✓ - B) Red, Green, Blue - C) Cyan, Magenta, Yellow - D) Black, White, Gray
Answer: A) Red, Yellow, Blue — these are primary colors in traditional (subtractive) color theory used in painting.
Example 2 (Design): Q: Which principle of design creates visual interest through differences? - A) Balance - B) Contrast ✓ - C) Unity - D) Repetition
Answer: B) Contrast — using differences in size, color, shape, or texture to create visual interest.
Example 3 (Architecture): Q: How many years is BS Architecture in the Philippines? - A) 4 years - B) 5 years ✓ - C) 6 years - D) 3 years
Answer: B) 5 years — Architecture is one of the few 5-year undergraduate programs in the Philippines.
Practice: 1) Name three secondary colors. 2) What is the golden ratio in design? 3) Who painted the Spoliarium? 4) Difference between RGB and CMYK color models?
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 46.4 Health Sciences Specialization before moving on.
2. The Intuition

Health science programs are among the most popular choices for Filipino students, driven by strong domestic demand and overseas employment opportunities. The Philippines produces thousands of nurses, pharmacists, and medical technologists annually, many of whom work abroad. All health programs require passing a PRC (Professional Regulation Commission) board exam.

A 4-year program preparing students to provide patient care in hospitals, clinics, and communities. Board exam: Nurse Licensure Exam (NLE). Passing rate: 70–85%. Career: staff nurse, ward nurse, community health nurse. High overseas demand (US, UK, Middle East, Japan).

Studies drug formulation, dispensing, pharmacology, and pharmaceutical care. Board exam: Pharmacist Licensure Exam. Passing rate: 50–70%. Career: hospital pharmacist, community pharmacist, pharmaceutical industry, drug inspector.

Laboratory science — analyzing blood, tissue, and body fluids for disease diagnosis. Board exam: MedTech Licensure Exam. Passing rate: 60–80%. Career: medical technologist, clinical laboratory scientist, quality assurance. High demand in hospitals and diagnostic labs.

Rehabilitation science — restoring movement and function after injury or illness. Board exam: PT Licensure Exam. Passing rate: 55–75%. Career: physical therapist, rehabilitation specialist, sports therapist. Growing demand due to aging population and sports medicine.
Medical imaging — X-ray, CT scan, MRI, ultrasound. Board exam: RadTech Licensure Exam. Passing rate: 60–75%. Career: radiologic technologist, CT/MRI technologist, sonographer.
3. The Build-Up
Health Sciences CET Topics:
| Subject | Topics | Sample Q |
| Biology | Anatomy, physiology, microbiology | Function of red blood cells? |
| Chemistry | Biochemistry, organic chemistry | pH of blood is approximately? |
| Physics | Radiation, optics, biomechanics | X-rays are a form of? |
| Health Professions | Nursing process, drug classification | ADPIE: Assessment, Diagnosis... |
Board Exam Passing Rates (National Averages):
Nursing (NLE): 70–85% — highest among health board exams
Pharmacy: 50–70% — moderate difficulty
MedTech: 60–80% — moderate to high
Physical Therapy: 55–75% — moderate
Radiologic Technology: 60–75% — moderate
Top Health Science Schools:
UP Manila (College of Nursing, Pharmacy, Public Health) — UPCAT: top 10%
UST (Nursing, Pharmacy, MedTech) — USTET: above-average science
UST (Rehabilitation Sciences) — USTET + interview
CEU, UE, Perpetual Help — entrance exams focus on science fundamentals
Adventist University of the Philippines — strong MedTech program
Health Careers (Starting Salary, Philippines):
Nurse (local): ₱15,000–₱22,000/month
Nurse (US/UK): ₱100,000–₱300,000+/month equivalent
Pharmacist (local): ₱15,000–₱25,000/month
MedTech (local): ₱14,000–₱20,000/month
Physical Therapist (local): ₱15,000–₱25,000/month
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Health programs = studying the human body and how to help sick people. Nursing = direct patient care (most popular, high overseas demand). Pharmacy = drugs and medicines. MedTech = lab work (testing blood, urine). Physical Therapy = helping people recover movement. All require PRC board exams after graduation.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Biology): Q: What is the primary function of red blood cells? - A) Fight infection - B) Carry oxygen ✓ - C) Clot blood - D) Produce antibodies
Answer: B) Carry oxygen — RBCs contain hemoglobin which binds to oxygen in the lungs and delivers it to tissues.
Example 2 (Pharmacy): Q: What does 'PRN' mean in drug prescriptions? - A) Every 6 hours - B) As needed ✓ - C) Before meals - D) Twice daily
Answer: B) As needed — PRN stands for 'pro re nata' (Latin for 'as the situation demands').
Example 3 (Board Exam): Q: Which health profession has the highest typical board exam passing rate? - A) Pharmacy - B) Nursing ✓ - C) MedTech - D) RadTech
Answer: B) Nursing — the NLE consistently has passing rates of 70–85%, the highest among health board exams.
Practice: 1) What is the normal blood pH range? 2) Name the four stages of the nursing process. 3) What type of imaging uses magnetic fields? 4) What is pharmacokinetics?
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 46.5 Education Specialization before moving on.
2. The Intuition

Education programs train future teachers, one of the most vital professions in the Philippines. With the K-12 system now fully implemented, there is increased demand for qualified teachers across all levels — elementary, secondary, and technical-vocational. All public school teachers must pass the Licensure Examination for Teachers (LET).

Prepares teachers for elementary school (Grades 1–6). Covers child development, teaching methods, curriculum design, and classroom management. Specializations include General Education, Special Education (SPED), and Pre-school Education. Board exam: LET.
Prepares teachers for junior and senior high school (Grades 7–12). Students major in a specific subject: English, Math, Science, Filipino, Social Studies, or Values Education. Board exam: LET.
Prepares teachers for TESDA-accredited technical-vocational institutions. Covers industrial arts, home economics, ICT, and agri-fishery. Board exam: LET (with specialization).
3. The Build-Up
Licensure Examination for Teachers (LET):
Administered by: PRC (Professional Regulation Commission) twice a year (March and September)
Coverage: General Education (20%), Professional Education (40%), Specialization/Major (40%)
National passing rate: 30–50% (elementary), 35–55% (secondary) — challenging exam
Passing score: 75% overall, with no subject below 50%
Education CET Topics:
| Subject | Topics | Sample Q |
| Gen Ed | English, Math, Science, Filipino, Social Studies | Parts of speech, Philippine history |
| Prof Ed | Child development, learning theories, pedagogy | Piaget's stages of development |
| Specialization | Content area (Math, English, Science, etc.) | Subject-specific knowledge |
| Classroom Management | Motivation, discipline, assessment | Positive reinforcement |

The education-specific subjects in teacher preparation — foundations of education, principles of teaching, child/adolescent development, educational technology, curriculum development, assessment, and classroom management.
Top Education Schools:
UP Diliman (College of Education) — UPCAT: top 10–15%
Philippine Normal University (PNU) — the national center for teacher education
UST (Education) — USTET + interview
PUP, RTU, Rizal Technological — affordable state university options
Miriam College, St. Scholastica — private options with strong education programs
Education Careers (Starting Salary, Philippines):
Public School Teacher (Salary Grade 11): ₱27,000–₱33,000/month (2025 rates)
Private School Teacher: ₱12,000–₱20,000/month (varies widely)
College Instructor (with MA): ₱30,000–₱45,000/month
School Administrator (with MA/PhD): ₱40,000–₱70,000/month
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Education = learning how to teach. BEEd = elementary teacher. BSEd = high school teacher. BTVEd = vocational teacher. All three need the LET (Licensure Exam for Teachers) to teach in public schools. The LET covers general education, professional education, and your specialization. Public school teachers have competitive salaries with good benefits.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Pedagogy): Q: Piaget's stage for children aged 7–11? - A) Sensorimotor - B) Preoperational - C) Concrete Operational ✓ - D) Formal Operational
Answer: C) Concrete Operational — children aged 7–11 develop logical thinking about concrete events but struggle with abstract concepts.
Example 2 (LET): Q: What is the minimum overall passing score for the LET? - A) 70% - B) 75% ✓ - C) 80% - D) 60%
Answer: B) 75% overall, with no individual subject score falling below 50%.
Example 3 (Education): Q: BEEd prepares teachers for which level? - A) College - B) High school - C) Elementary ✓ - D) Pre-school only
Answer: C) Elementary (Grades 1–6). BSEd is for high school (Grades 7–12).

Practice: 1) Name the four stages of Piaget's cognitive development. 2) What does 'scaffolding' mean in education? 3) Who is considered the father of progressive education? 4) What is differentiated instruction?

specialized.description: Covers engineering, business, arts, health sciences, and education specializations.

specialized.title: TVL, Sports & Arts Track

blocks.value: TVL Specializations 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 47.1 TVL (Technical-Vocational-Livelihood) Specialization before moving on.
2. The Intuition

TVL is one of the largest Senior High School tracks in the Philippines, designed to give students job-ready skills immediately after graduation. TVL graduates can work right away (with TESDA certifications) or pursue college degrees in related fields. The track is especially important for students who need to enter the workforce quickly.

Technical-Vocational-Livelihood Track — a Senior High School strand that provides practical skills in agriculture, home economics, industrial trades, and information technology. Students earn TESDA National Certificates (NC) that qualify them for employment.
3. The Build-Up
The Four TVL Strands:

Specializations include: Cookery (NC II), Bread and Pastry Production (NC II), Food and Beverage Services (NC II), Tourism (NC II), Housekeeping (NC II), Hairdressing (NC II), Nail Care (NC II), Dressmaking/Tailoring (NC II). These lead to careers in hospitality, food service, and tourism.

Specializations include: Electrical Installation and Maintenance (NC II), Plumbing (NC II), Carpentry (NC II), Shielded Metal Arc Welding (NC II), Automotive Servicing (NC II), Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (NC II). These lead to construction and maintenance careers.

Specializations include: Computer Programming (NC IV), Animation (NC III), Computer Systems Servicing (NC II), Technical Drafting (NC II), Medical Transcription (NC II). These lead to tech industry careers.

Specializations include: Agricultural Crops Production (NC II), Animal Production (NC II), Fish Capture (NC II), Fish Processing (NC II), Horticulture (NC II). These lead to agriculture, aquaculture, and food production careers.
TVL CET Topics:
| Subject | Topics | Sample Q |
| Agri-Fishery | Crop care, animal production, aquaculture | What is hydroponics? |
| Home Economics | Cookery, baking, tourism, housekeeping | What is menu planning? |
| Industrial Arts | Tools, materials, safety, processes | What is arc welding? |
| ICT | Binary, algorithms, networking, hardware | Convert 1010₂ to decimal |
TVL NC Certification Levels:
NC I — Basic skills, supervised work
NC II — Standard competency, independent work (most common level)
NC III — Advanced skills, supervisory capability
NC IV — Highly specialized, management-level competency
TVL Careers:
Agri: Farmer/Agri-technician (₱12,000–₱20,000), Fishery Worker (₱12,000–₱18,000)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

TVL = practical skills you can use to get a job right after SHS. Home Economics = cooking, baking, hotel work. Industrial Arts = carpentry, welding, electrical, plumbing. ICT = computers, programming, animation. Agri-Fishery = farming and fishing. You get TESDA certificates (NC I to NC IV) that prove you can do the work.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: ICT (TVL) includes? - A) Crop production - B) Cookery, baking - C) Carpentry, welding - D) Programming, animation ✓
Answer: D) Programming, animation — ICT stands for Information and Communications Technology.
Example 2 (Binary): Q: Convert 1010₂ to decimal? - A) 8 - B) 10 ✓ - C) 12 - D) 20
Example 3 (TESDA): Q: What NC level is required for Cookery? - A) NC I - B) NC II ✓ - C) NC III - D) NC IV
Answer: B) NC II — most TVL specializations like Cookery, Bread/Pastry, and Electrical are at NC II level.
Practice: 1) Convert 11011₂ to decimal. 2) Name three HE specializations. 3) What is the difference between SMAW and GMAW welding? 4) What TESDA level covers supervisory skills?
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 47.2 Sports Specialization before moving on.
2. The Intuition

The Sports track prepares students for careers in physical education, coaching, sports science, and fitness. In the Philippines, sports is growing as a profession — from the PBA (basketball) to the PSC (Philippine Sports Commission) programs, to the expanding fitness and wellness industry. Athletes can also pursue professional sports careers.

A Senior High School strand focused on physical education, sports science, coaching, and sports management. Prepares students for careers in athletics, coaching, fitness training, and sports administration.
3. The Build-Up
Sports Strand Focus:
Study of exercise physiology, sports psychology, biomechanics, and movement science. Covers the FITT principle (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type) for designing exercise programs.
The scientific study of human performance — nutrition for athletes, hydration, injury prevention, recovery, and training periodization. Includes kinesiology and exercise physiology.

Teaching sports techniques, developing game strategies, motivating athletes, and designing training programs. Includes leadership styles — transformational (inspiring) vs. transactional (reward-based).
The business side of sports — event organization, facility management, sports marketing, budgeting, and sponsorship. Uses SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
Sports CET Topics:
| Subject | Topics | Sample Q |
| PE | Exercise physiology, FITT principle | F=Frequency, I=Intensity, T=Time, T=Type |
| Sports Science | Nutrition, hydration, recovery | Carbs = primary energy for athletes |
| Coaching | Techniques, motivation, leadership | Transformational leadership = inspires |
| Management | Event planning, budgeting, marketing | SMART goals framework |
Key Sports Science Concepts:
FITT Principle: Frequency (how often), Intensity (how hard), Time (how long), Type (what kind)
Heart Rate Training Zones: Resting (60-100 bpm), Moderate (50-70% max HR), Vigorous (70-85% max HR)
Max HR Formula: 220 - Age (e.g., 20 years old → 200 bpm max)
Macronutrients: Carbohydrates (energy), Protein (muscle repair), Fats (long-term energy)
Sports Careers (Starting Salary, Philippines):
Coach/Trainer: ₱15,000–₱30,000/month
PE Teacher (with LET): ₱27,000–₱33,000/month (public school)
Fitness Trainer/Gym Instructor: ₱14,000–₱25,000/month
Sports Manager/Event Coordinator: ₱18,000–₱35,000/month
Professional Athlete: varies widely (₱20,000–₱500,000+/month)
Philippine Sports Organizations:
PSC (Philippine Sports Commission) — government sports development agency
POC (Philippine Olympic Committee) — Olympic representation
NSA (National Sports Associations) — per-sport governing bodies (e.g., SBP for basketball)
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Sports track = studying how the body works during exercise and how to train athletes. PE = understanding exercise and movement. Sports Science = the science behind nutrition, injury, and performance. Coaching = teaching and motivating athletes. Sports Management = the business and event side of sports. Remember FITT: Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type.
5. Worked Examples

Example 1 (Concrete): Q: FITT principle (PE)? - A) Fast, Intense, Tough, Tiring - B) Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type ✓ - C) Flexibility, Intensity, Timing, Technique - D) Focus, Intensity, Time, Training
Answer: B) Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type — the four variables in designing an exercise program.
Example 2 (Applied): Q: A 20-year-old athlete's estimated maximum heart rate? - A) 180 bpm - B) 190 bpm - C) 200 bpm ✓ - D) 210 bpm
Answer: C) 200 bpm (220 - Age = 220 - 20 = 200 bpm)
Example 3 (Nutrition): Q: Primary macronutrient for athletic energy? - A) Protein - B) Fats - C) Carbohydrates ✓ - D) Vitamins
Answer: C) Carbohydrates — the body's preferred and most efficient fuel source during moderate to high-intensity exercise.
Practice: 1) Calculate max HR for a 35-year-old. 2) What is the 'afterburn effect'? 3) Name the PSC's role. 4) What does SMART stand for in sports management?
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 47.3 TVL Specialization Careers (TESDA) before moving on.
2. The Intuition

TESDA (Technical Education and Skills Development Authority) is the government agency that certifies TVL skills through National Certificates (NC). These certifications are recognized by employers in the Philippines and overseas. Many TVL graduates use NC II certifications to get jobs immediately or to work abroad (especially in the Middle East, Japan, and Korea).

Technical Education and Skills Development Authority — the Philippine government agency responsible for managing and supervising technical education and skills development. Provides National Certificates (NC) through competency assessments.
3. The Build-Up
TESDA Certification System:
NC I — Basic skills, entry-level competency, supervised work
NC II — Standard competency, can work independently (most common job requirement)
NC III — Advanced skills, can supervise others, troubleshoot complex problems
NC IV — Highly specialized, management and training capability
Assessment Process:
Step 1: Complete TESDA-accredited training program or SHS TVL track
Step 2: Apply for competency assessment at TESDA Assessment Center
Step 3: Pass written exam + practical demonstration of skills
Step 4: Receive National Certificate from TESDA
Complete TVL Career Paths with NC Levels:
| Career | NC Level | Key Skills |
| Cookery | NC II | Food preparation, menu planning, sanitation, knife skills |
| Bread & Pastry | NC II | Baking, cake decorating, nutrition, recipe costing |
| Food & Beverage | NC II | Table service, bartending, customer relations |
| Housekeeping | NC II | Room cleaning, laundry, guest services |
| Electrical Installation | NC II | Wiring, circuits, safety protocols, troubleshooting |
| Plumbing | NC II | Pipe installation, maintenance, repair, blueprint reading |
| Carpentry | NC II | Woodworking, furniture making, measurements, finishing |
| Shielded Metal Arc Welding | NC II | Arc welding, metal fabrication, safety |
| Automotive Servicing | NC II | Engine repair, electrical systems, preventive maintenance |
| Programming | NC IV | Python, Java, web development, databases |
| Animation | NC III | 2D/3D animation, drawing, storytelling, digital tools |
| Computer Systems Servicing | NC II | Hardware assembly, OS installation, networking, troubleshooting |
Overseas Employment Opportunities (TVL with NC):
Middle East (Saudi, UAE, Qatar): Electricians, welders, plumbers, HVAC technicians
Japan (TITP program): Caregivers, food processing, construction workers
Korea (EPS program): Manufacturing, agriculture, construction workers
Singapore/HK: F&B service, housekeeping, construction trades
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

TESDA = the government office that certifies your skills. NC = National Certificate, proves you can do a job. NC II is the most common level employers look for. You get NC by passing a practical skills test. With NC, you can work in the Philippines or abroad. Most TVL specializations lead to NC II. ICT specializations like Programming may require NC III or IV.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Cookery NC level? - A) NC I - B) NC II ✓ - C) NC III - D) NC IV
Answer: B) NC II — Cookery and Bread/Pastry Production are both at NC II level.
Example 2 (Applied): Q: What NC level is Programming? - A) NC I - B) NC II - C) NC III - D) NC IV ✓
Answer: D) NC IV — Computer Programming requires NC IV because it involves advanced technical skills.
Example 3 (Overseas): Q: Japan's TITP program accepts workers from which track? - A) STEM - B) ABM - C) TVL ✓ - D) HUMSS
Answer: C) TVL — Japan's Technical Intern Training Program (TITP) accepts skilled TVL workers with TESDA certifications.

Practice: 1) What is the difference between NC II and NC III? 2) Name two TESDA assessment steps. 3) Which country uses the EPS program for Filipino workers? 4) Can TVL graduates work abroad with NC II?
1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 47.4 Sports Specialization Careers before moving on.
2. The Intuition

Sports careers in the Philippines extend far beyond being a professional athlete. The industry includes coaching, sports medicine, fitness training, sports management, physical therapy, and sports media. With growing health consciousness and government support through PSC, sports careers are becoming more viable and respected.
3. The Build-Up
Complete Sports Career Paths:
| Career | Education Needed | Key Skills |
| Coach | Sports Science degree | Techniques, motivation, leadership, strategy |
| PE Teacher | PE degree + LET license | FITT principle, lesson planning, child development |
| Sports Psychologist | Psychology degree + license | Mental training, visualization, anxiety management |
| Athletic Trainer | Sports Science degree | Injury prevention, first aid, rehabilitation, taping |
| Sports Manager | Management degree | Event planning, budgeting, marketing, sponsorship |
| Physical Therapist | PT degree + PRC license | Rehabilitation, exercise therapy, manual therapy |
| Fitness Trainer | NC II (TESDA) or certification | Exercise programming, nutrition basics, motivation |
| Sports Journalist/Media | Mass Communication degree | Writing, broadcasting, sports knowledge |
Sports Organizations (Philippines):
Government agency for sports development. Funds national athletes, builds sports facilities, and implements grassroots sports programs. Created by RA 6847 (1990).
The National Olympic Committee of the Philippines. Responsible for sending athletes to the Olympics, Asian Games, and SEA Games. Recognized by the International Olympic Committee.
Per-sport governing bodies recognized by POC. Examples: SBP (Samahang Basketbol ng Pilipinas), PFF (Philippine Football Federation), PATAFA (Philippine Athletics Track and Field Association).
Key Sports Science Concepts for CET:
Biomechanics: Study of movement mechanics — leverage, force, momentum
Periodization: Systematic planning of training into phases (off-season, pre-season, in-season)
RICE Protocol: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation — first aid for acute injuries
Overload Principle: To improve fitness, gradually increase training demands
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Sports careers = working in the sports industry. Coach = trains athletes. PE Teacher = teaches physical education in schools. Athletic Trainer = prevents and treats sports injuries. Sports Manager = organizes sports events and facilities. Sports Psychologist = helps athletes with mental game. Physical Therapist = helps injured people recover movement. PSC = government sports agency. POC = Olympic committee.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Concrete): Q: Coach needs? - A) Culinary skills - B) Sports Science degree + techniques ✓ - C) Accounting skills - D) Engineering skills
Answer: B) Sports Science degree + techniques — coaching requires both academic knowledge of sports science and practical knowledge of techniques and strategies.
Example 2 (Applied): Q: An athlete sprains an ankle. First aid follows which protocol? - A) ABC - B) CPR - C) RICE ✓ - D) CPR
Answer: C) RICE — Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation is the standard first aid protocol for acute musculoskeletal injuries.
Example 3 (Organization): Q: PSC stands for? - A) Philippine Sports Corporation - B) Philippine Sports Commission ✓ - C) Philippine Sports Committee - D) Philippine Sports Council
Answer: B) Philippine Sports Commission — the government agency created by RA 6847 for sports development.
Practice: 1) What is periodization? 2) Name the POC's main responsibility. 3) What does an NSA do? 4) What is the overload principle?

sections.title: Arts & Design Track and Employment Pathways

blocks.value: 1. What You Need First
Master the fundamentals of 47.5 Arts & Design Track and Employment Pathways before moving on.
2. The Intuition

The Arts & Design track in Senior High School develops creative skills and prepares students for careers in the creative industries or for college programs in fine arts, design, media, and performing arts. The Philippines has a thriving creative economy — from animation studios and advertising agencies to performing arts companies and fashion labels.

A Senior High School strand focused on visual arts, media arts, literary arts, and performing arts. Develops creativity, technical skills, and artistic expression. Prepares students for creative careers or further college education in arts.
3. The Build-Up
The Four Pillars of Arts & Design:

Traditional and contemporary art forms — drawing, painting, sculpture, printmaking, photography, ceramics, and mixed media. Includes art history, criticism, and aesthetics. Career: fine artist, gallery curator, art teacher, art restorer.

Digital and electronic creative works — graphic design, animation, film/video production, web design, game design, photography, and digital illustration. Career: graphic designer, animator, filmmaker, photographer, UX/UI designer. High demand in advertising, gaming, and digital media.

Written creative expression — fiction, poetry, creative non-fiction, playwriting, screenwriting, and journalism. Career: writer, editor, scriptwriter, content creator, journalist. Includes Filipino and English literary traditions.

Live performance disciplines — theater, dance, music (vocal and instrumental), and spoken word. Career: performer (actor, dancer, musician), director, choreographer, stage manager, arts administrator. The Philippines has strong traditions in both Western and indigenous performing arts.
Employment Pathways for Arts & Design Graduates:
| Pathway | Examples | Requirements |
| Direct Employment | Graphic designer, photographer, studio assistant | Portfolio + skills test |
| Freelance/Self-employed | Freelance artist, illustrator, musician | Portfolio + client network |
| College (Arts Programs) | BFA, BMusic, BArchitecture, Multimedia Arts | CET + portfolio/talent test |
| TESDA (Creative NC) | Visual Graphic Design NC III, Animation NC III | TESDA assessment |
Creative Industries Salary Ranges (Philippines):
Graphic Designer: ₱15,000–₱30,000/month
Animator (2D/3D): ₱18,000–₱40,000/month
Photographer: ₱12,000–₱30,000/month (freelance can earn more per project)
Filmmaker/Video Editor: ₱16,000–₱35,000/month
Musician/Performer: varies widely (₱10,000–₱50,000+/month depending on gigs)
UX/UI Designer: ₱25,000–₱50,000/month (highest-paying creative tech role)
Senior High School: Work vs. College Decision
After SHS, students face a critical choice. Here's a comparison to help decide:

Pros: Immediate income, real-world experience, no tuition costs. Cons: Lower starting salary (₱12,000–₱20,000), limited career advancement, physically demanding for trades. Best for: students who need to support family financially or who have strong vocational skills.

Pros: Higher lifetime earnings, professional licensure, career advancement. Cons: 4–5 more years of study, tuition costs, delayed income. Best for: students targeting licensed professions (engineering, nursing, accountancy, law, medicine).
Salary Comparison: SHS Graduate vs. College Graduate (Starting)
TVL NC II (SHS): ₱12,000–₱18,000/month
College Graduate (non-licensed): ₱16,000–₱25,000/month
College Graduate (licensed — Engr, CPA, Nurse): ₱18,000–₱35,000/month
Over 10 years: College graduates typically earn 40–80% more cumulatively
4. Layman Terms (Rescue Track)

Arts & Design track = learning creative skills (drawing, music, dance, writing, digital design). Visual Arts = traditional art (painting, sculpture). Media Arts = digital art (graphic design, animation, film). Literary Arts = writing (stories, poetry, scripts). Performing Arts = live performance (theater, dance, music). After SHS, you can work immediately (especially with TVL/Arts skills) or go to college for higher-paying careers. College usually means higher lifetime earnings but takes 4–5 more years.
5. Worked Examples
Example 1 (Arts Track): Q: Which arts pillar covers animation and film? - A) Visual Arts - B) Media Arts ✓ - C) Literary Arts - D) Performing Arts
Answer: B) Media Arts — includes digital/electronic creative works like animation, film, video production, and web design.
Example 2 (Employment): Q: A TVL graduate with NC II in Cookery can earn approximately? - A) ₱5,000–₱10,000 - B) ₱12,000–₱20,000 ✓ - C) ₱25,000–₱35,000 - D) ₱40,000–₱50,000
Answer: B) ₱12,000–₱20,000/month — typical starting salary for NC II certified workers in the Philippines.
Example 3 (Decision): Q: Which factor most favors choosing college over immediate work? - A) Need income now - B) Want professional license ✓ - C) Prefer physical work - D) No tuition budget
Answer: B) Want professional license — careers like engineering, nursing, and accountancy require college degrees and passing board exams.

Practice: 1) Name the four pillars of Arts & Design. 2) What is the typical salary range for a UX/UI designer? 3) List two pros and two cons of working after SHS. 4) What TESDA certification applies to graphic design?
